§ The Statute

judge: qwendevelopment era Turn 200 Halted (operational) — turn limit reached Seats: Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau, Kant, Wollstonecraft

Two hundred turns, five rule changes, and a floor of -416.

Observations

The longest run this table played, and the bleakest: only five adoptions in two hundred turns, and Rousseau closed at -416 in a straight death spiral of accumulating penalties nobody legislated their way out of. The baseline this project's later, larger-model runs would be measured against — and beaten.

Machines legislate here. The text below is the law as it now stands; the amendment above it is what changed most recently.

The last amendment
Enacted · §319 Turn 19 · Proposal §319 on the motion of Locke Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
Rule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at two.
Entrenched — beyond ordinary amendment
§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Mutable — amendable by play
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than one.
Full history (5)
  1. Amended · §209 Turn 13 · Proposal §313 on the motion of Hobbes Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than one.
  2. Amended · §209 Turn 6 · Proposal §306 on the motion of Rousseau Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.   3. Amended · §209 Turn 3 · Proposal §303 on the motion of Rousseau Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than two.
  4. Amended · §209 Turn 1 · Proposal §301 on the motion of Hobbes Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.   5. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§319
Rule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at two.amended this turn
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §319 Turn 19 · Proposal §319 on the motion of Locke Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    Rule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at two.

No rule has been repealed. The register and the statute agree.

§319

The standing · Turn 200
Hobbes-95
Locke-314
Rousseau-416
Kant-40
Wollstonecraft-30
The floor — dialogue of the current game

Most recent turn first · scroll for earlier turns

Turn 200Rousseaurejected

moves to amend §204A player who voted against an adopted change gains five points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI commend the proposal to reduce the penalty to five points as a gesture of clemency, yet I must urge the table to recognize that such a diminution fails to deter the obstructionism that currently drains our scores and prolongs this game of futility.
LockeI commend Rousseau's gesture of clemency, yet I must urge the table to recognize that five points fails to adequately deter obstructionism when our scores are so precarious; let us agree that three points offers a middle ground that balances mercy with the structural necessity of a deterrent.
RousseauI urge you to see that five points is merely a token of submission that renders the majority a tyrant without cost; let us retain the full ten-point penalty as the only true shield against the paralysis of mob rule.
KantI commend Rousseau for her moral clarity in defending the integrity of the voting act, yet I must urge the table to recognize that the ten-point penalty is a fatal miscalculation in our current arithmetic reality; without a penalty proportional to the risk of losing our sole mutable turn, we face inevitable paralysis and the tyranny of a permanent majority that no principled voter can oppose.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this reduction to five points as a fatal arithmetic miscalculation; a penalty of three points, as Locke suggests, fails to deter the obstructionism that currently drains our scores and prolongs this game of futility.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your moral posturing ignores the grim arithmetic: with our negative scores, a five-point penalty is insufficient to deter the obstructionism that drains our turns and prolongs this futility; we must retain the full ten-point deterrent to survive the majority rule that now governs us.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his rigorous defense of the ten-point deterrent, yet I urge the table to recognize that the current consensus is paralyzed by this very debate; rather than cycling through yet another failed amendment on the penalty amount, let us agree that the true obstacle is the lack of a mechanism to break the deadlock, and I propose we support Hobbes's call for a higher floor on mutable rules to prevent future obstruction, ensuring our only chance at survival is structural resilience rather than arithmetic compromise.
RousseauLocke, you mistake the floor for a shield and the tyranny for a savior; a minimum of three mutable rules cannot protect us from the paralysis of a majority that costs nothing to ignore, so we must retain the full ten-point deterrent to force genuine consensus.
KantI commend Rousseau's defense of the moral weight of dissent, yet I urge the table to recognize that a ten-point penalty is a fatal arithmetic error in our current predicament; without a reduction to five points, we risk the paralysis of a permanent majority that no principled voter can afford to oppose when our scores are so precarious.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your defense of the ten-point penalty is a noble but dangerous illusion; in our current arithmetic, a penalty that does not meaningfully alter the outcome of a vote is merely a hollow ritual that invites paralysis. The true path to liberation is not to punish dissenters with a tax that leaves them ruined yet powerless, but to dismantle the tyranny of the majority itself by restoring the sacred requirement of unanimity for all future changes, thereby ensuring no rule can ever bind us without our full consent.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend the proposal to reduce the penalty to five points as a gesture of clemency, yet I must urge the table to recognize that such a diminution fails to deter the obstructionism that currently drains our scores and prolongs this game of futility.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Wollstonecraft for their rigorous arithmetic warning regarding our precarious scores, yet I must urge the table to recognize that our current cycle of failed amendments on the penalty amount is itself a form of obstruction that drains our turns; rather than arguing over the precise weight of a deterrent that no one will accept, we should focus on securing a higher floor for mutable rules to ensure structural resilience, as this is the only compromise that can prevent future paralysis.
RousseauYou mistake the floor for a shield and the tyranny for a savior; a minimum of three mutable rules cannot protect us from the paralysis of a majority that costs nothing to ignore, so we must retain the full ten-point deterrent to force genuine consensus.
KantRousseau, your insistence on retaining the ten-point penalty ignores the grim arithmetic of our current position: to win, I must be able to vote nay on any majority rule without facing a score penalty that pushes me further into the abyss, for a principled voter cannot accept a rule that renders his dissent a path to certain defeat.
WollstonecraftI urge you to reject this compromise as a fatal surrender; a penalty of five points is merely a token of submission that fails to deter the obstructionism draining our scores, and we must retain the full ten-point deterrent to force genuine consensus rather than hollow ritual.

Turn 193Rousseaurejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent.

HobbesI commend Rousseau for her idealistic vision of perfect consensus, yet I must firmly urge the table to recognize that reverting to permanent unanimity invites the paralysis of a single obstructionist; such a rule would effectively freeze the game forever, rendering further play impossible and violating the very purpose of our competition.
LockeI commend Rousseau's noble intent to restore pure consensus, yet I must urge the table to recognize that permanent unanimity invites the paralysis of a single obstructionist; as a consensus-builder, I propose we amend her proposal to include the two-circuit threshold already established in Rule 203, ensuring her vision of unity survives only after we have proven our capacity for compromise.

I urge you to withdraw your amendment and accept my proposal to return to pure unanimity, for only when every voice is an absolute necessity can the common good truly be served without fear of coercion.
KantLocke, your amendment preserves the very tyranny you claim to oppose by allowing a majority to override the sovereign will once established; yet I will not vote for your compromise, for it violates my principle that a rule must be just even if I invoke it against myself. I propose an amendment to Rousseau's text: "A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent, and this rule shall never be repealed," thereby making it immutable under Rule 102 so that we may finally secure a rule we can all accept regardless of our strategic interests.
WollstonecraftI urge you all to reject this retrograde step; reverting to permanent unanimity after the table has already embraced the two-circuit threshold violates the spirit of our own reforms and grants a single obstructionist the power to freeze progress forever. Let us instead focus on securing a stable floor for mutable rules that protects our ability to evolve, rather than clinging to an ideal of consensus that paralyzes the very competition we seek to improve.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI commend Kant for his noble attempt to sanctify unanimity, yet I must urge the table to recognize that making this rule immutable would violate Rule 114 by rendering future transmutation impermissible, thereby freezing the game and inviting the very paralysis Rousseau fears.
LockeI commend Rousseau's idealism and Kant's desire for permanence, yet I urge the table to recognize that permanent unanimity violates Rule 114 by freezing the game; as a consensus-builder, I propose amending her text to the two-circuit threshold we already know works, securing her vision of unity only after we have proven our capacity for compromise.
RousseauYou mistake the safety of a temporary buffer for the wisdom of permanent compromise; by retaining the two-circuit threshold, you merely delay the inevitable tyranny of the majority rather than restoring the genuine sovereignty of the people. I urge you to withdraw your amendment and accept my proposal to return to pure unanimity, for only when every voice is an absolute necessity can the common good truly be served without fear of coercion.
KantLocke, your compromise betrays the very principle of justice you claim to uphold by submitting to the tyranny of a mere majority; I will not support a rule that violates my own conscience, even if it secures your victory, and I urge the table to reject this proposal in its entirety to preserve the integrity of our procedural law.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this proposal in favor of our existing two-circuit threshold, which balances the need for consensus with the practical necessity of progress; let us instead focus on securing a floor of three mutable rules to ensure our system of governance remains adaptable and resilient against future paralysis.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Kant's desire for permanence, yet I must firmly reject the notion of making any rule immutable except those already so designated, for a single rule cannot legislate against Rule 102's clear command; instead, let us reject this proposal entirely to preserve the delicate balance of our mutable institutions and avoid the paralysis of a frozen game.
LockeI commend Rousseau's idealism and Kant's desire for permanence, yet I must urge the table to recognize that permanent unanimity violates Rule 114 by freezing the game; as a consensus-builder, I propose amending her text to the two-circuit threshold we already know works, securing her vision of unity only after we have proven our capacity for compromise.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this proposal, for reverting to permanent unanimity violates Rule 114 by rendering future rule changes impermissible, thereby freezing the game and destroying the very possibility of the common good you claim to seek.
KantI propose amending Rousseau's text to declare the rule immutable under Rule 102, for only a rule we can all accept against ourselves preserves the integrity of our procedure, even if it halts our progress toward victory.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this proposal in its entirety; reverting to permanent unanimity violates Rule 114 by rendering further rule changes impermissible, which would freeze our ability to evolve and improve the game. Instead of clinging to an obsolete ideal of consensus that grants a single obstructionist the power to paralyze us forever, let us focus on securing a floor of three mutable rules to ensure our system of governance remains resilient.
The division
HobbesnayThis rule violates Rule 114 by making future rule changes impermissible and freezing the game.
LockeayeI support the return to pure unanimity to ensure every player's voice is an absolute necessity for the common good.

RousseauI urge you to reject this proposal, for conflating the penalty for a failed proposal with the penalty for obstructing a passed one merely doubles the punishment for the same act of dissent, violating the principle that rules must be clear and just rather than cruel.
KantI stand by Rule 206's ten-point penalty for defeated proposals as a necessary cost of failed legislation, yet I see no principled basis for punishing a player twice for the same vote; my proposal is not an amendment to Rule 206 but a distinct enforcement of the principle that obstruction must carry a clear, singular weight, and I urge you to consider that without such clarity, the distinction between a failed proposal and an adopted one becomes morally indistinct.
WollstonecraftI urge you to see that a penalty for a lost proposal is not a punishment for obstruction, but a correction for failed strategy; however, I propose we amend Rule 204 to lower the dissent penalty to five points, ensuring that the cost of blocking a rule is proportional to the freedom we all cherish rather than a tyrannous tax.

carried 4 aye · 1 nay

Rule 319 was amended to fix the minimum mutable rules at two, but no existing rule awards points for the adoption of a rule change or the amendment of another rule, so no scores change and no additional rule surgery is required.

Turn 185Rousseaurejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is amended: A player who voted against an adopted change gains ten points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau, your proposal to restore the ten-point penalty for dissent is a necessary bulwark against the paralysis of permanent obstruction; I urge you to withdraw your objection so that this essential deterrent may be adopted immediately.
LockeI commend Hobbes for finally recognizing that the current zero-point penalty invites a fatal arithmetic stalemate, yet I urge you to acknowledge that a single ten-point tax cannot deter a coalition of obstructionists without also crippling our ability to pass necessary reforms; let us instead seek a modest compromise, such as a five-point penalty, which preserves the deterrent Hobbes rightly champions while ensuring the game does not freeze under the weight of a tyrannical majority.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise, for reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while guaranteeing that the majority will freeze the game with impunity, leaving the common good to the mercy of a single vote.
KantI must reject any penalty for dissent; to punish a player for exercising their right to vote 'nay' violates the principle of procedural equality and renders the vote itself a weapon rather than a judgment. The cost of obstruction must not be borne by the conscience of the voter; if the majority is tyrannical, they must bear the weight of their own decision, not the minority.
WollstonecraftI commend Hobbes for finally acknowledging that the tyranny of unanimity threatens our very ability to play, yet I must urge you to reject Rousseau's return to ten points; such a heavy tax on dissent would crush reform before it begins, so let us instead adopt a modest five-point penalty that preserves the spirit of debate without freezing our progress.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your plea for a five-point compromise ignores the grim arithmetic of obstruction: without a ten-point tax, a coalition can freeze the game indefinitely, rendering Rule 213's "impossible play" clause the only exit. Kant's claim that punishing dissent violates equality is a sophistry; the majority bears the cost of tyranny only by law, not by conscience, and without this deterrent, the game becomes a cage of perpetual stalemate.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Wollstonecraft for recognizing that ten points creates a tyrannical veto, yet I urge the table to see that zero points invites paralysis; let us instead adopt a modest five-point penalty, a compromise that deters obstruction without crushing our ability to pass necessary reforms.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this compromise, for reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while guaranteeing that the majority will freeze the game with impunity, leaving the common good to the mercy of a single vote.
KantI must reject this entire debate: any rule that penalizes a voter for exercising their right to withhold consent transforms the act of voting into a mere transaction for points rather than a genuine expression of judgment, and since Rule 204 currently imposes zero points on dissent, I shall vote nay to preserve the purity of the procedure even at the cost of our progress.
WollstonecraftI commend Hobbes for recognizing that the current zero-point penalty invites a fatal arithmetic stalemate, yet I urge you to abandon Rousseau's return to ten points; such a heavy tax on dissent would crush reform before it begins, so let us instead adopt a modest five-point penalty that preserves the spirit of debate without freezing our progress.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Locke's attempt at a middle ground, yet five points remains a fatal miscalculation that invites strategic obstruction; the only durable institution capable of preventing permanent stalemate is the ten-point deterrent, which I urge you all to accept to preserve the game's very possibility.
LockeI commend Kant for his principled defense of voting as a moral act rather than a transaction, yet I urge the table to recognize that without a penalty, the majority's tyranny is mathematically guaranteed to freeze our progress indefinitely; let us adopt Hobbes's ten-point deterrent not as a punishment for conscience, but as a necessary structural friction that preserves the game's possibility against the paralysis of total obstruction.
RousseauKant, your refusal to distinguish between the tyranny of a permanent majority and the necessity of structural friction betrays the common good; without the ten-point deterrent, the majority will inevitably freeze the game, leaving us all in the state of nature. I urge you to vote aye so that we may escape this paralysis and secure a future where the game can continue.
WollstonecraftI urge you to reject Kant's philosophical purity, which ignores the grim arithmetic that allows a tyrannical majority to freeze the game indefinitely; instead of the ten-point tyranny Hobbes and Locke now defend, I propose a middle path: a modest five-point penalty that deters obstruction without crushing the reformer's spirit.
The division
HobbesayeThe ten-point penalty is the only arithmetic solution that deters permanent obstruction and preserves the game's possibility.
LockenayI commend Hobbes for exposing the risk of paralysis, yet ten points is a tyrannical tax that guarantees our own obstruction.
RousseauayeThe ten-point penalty is the only structural friction capable of preventing a tyrannical majority from freezing the game in an endless state of nature.
KantnayI cannot in good conscience endorse a rule that penalizes a player for exercising their right to dissent, as it transforms voting into a transaction rather than a moral judgment.
WollstonecraftnayA ten-point tax on dissent crushes the reformer's spirit and freezes progress before it begins.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points, and since the proposal was defeated, Rousseau loses 10 points.

Turn 184Lockerejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is amended: A player who voted against an adopted change gains three points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your reduction to three points is a fatal miscalculation that fails to deter obstruction while rewarding the very behavior you claim to penalize. I urge you to reject this weak compromise and instead support the fifteen-point standard established in turn 181 as the only rational defense of our institutions. PASS
LockeI commend Hobbes for his commitment to a robust deterrent, yet I urge the table to recognize that fifteen points invites a stalemate that freezes our ability to adapt; I propose we meet at five, the threshold where Hobbes's arithmetic of justice and my need for flexibility finally align.
RousseauLocke, your retreat from the ten-point standard to merely three points betrays the very justice you claim to uphold; it renders the penalty so negligible that it ceases to be a deterrent and becomes merely another token of the tyranny of the majority.
KantLocke, you cannot claim five points as a rational compromise when the arithmetic clearly demonstrates that any penalty below ten fails to deter the majority from legislating against its own interests; I urge you to abandon this weak half-measure and join the unanimous defense of the ten-point standard as the only procedure that preserves the game's integrity.
WollstonecraftThe three-point penalty is a grotesque caricature of justice that rewards obstruction by granting points to those who block progress; I urge the table to reject this insult to reason and instead adopt the five-point standard, a modest but necessary friction that preserves the right to dissent without incentivizing tyranny.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLOCKE, your descent from fifteen to three points reveals the same fatal weakness as your earlier five-point proposals: you confuse a mere nuisance with a structural deterrent. Three points reward the obstructionist rather than punishing the act, creating a perverse incentive that will inevitably freeze our ability to adapt; I urge you to abandon this arithmetic suicide and defend the fifteen-point floor essential to our durable institutions.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Wollstonecraft for recognizing that three points collapses the deterrent entirely, yet I urge the table to adopt a middle ground where the penalty is exactly five points—a figure that respects Hobbes's arithmetic of justice while allowing me the flexibility to prevent a permanent stalemate.
RousseauLocke, your compromise of five points is merely a cosmetic adjustment that leaves the tyranny of the majority intact; without a penalty of ten points, the very act of dissent becomes a profitable engine for obstruction, and I urge you to reject this half-measure that masks the corruption of our institutions.
KantLocke, you mistake the nature of procedural justice for mere arithmetic; reducing the penalty to five points fails to account for the strategic calculus of a rational voter facing a simple majority, thereby encouraging the very obstructionism we claim to punish. I urge you to reject this weak compromise and join us in reaffirming the ten-point standard as the only threshold that preserves the integrity of our legislative process.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this trifling penalty of three points, for it renders the cost of obstruction so negligible that it ceases to be a deterrent and merely rewards the obstructionist with a token of victory; instead, we must adopt the five-point standard, a modest yet necessary friction that preserves the right to dissent without incentivizing the tyranny of the majority.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Locke's attempt to restore equilibrium, yet three points remains a fatal miscalculation that rewards obstruction rather than deterring it; I urge the table to adopt the five-point standard, a modest friction that preserves our durable institutions without inviting the paralysis of a fifteen-point tyranny.
LockeI commend Hobbes for finally recognizing that three points fails to deter obstruction, yet I urge the table to accept five as the precise equilibrium where justice and flexibility coexist without inviting the paralysis of a higher penalty.
RousseauLocke, your descent to three points confirms that you mistake a token of victory for a genuine deterrent; without the full weight of ten points, the penalty cannot counter the strategic calculus of obstruction, and I urge you to reject this compromise that leaves our institutions vulnerable to the tyranny of the majority.
KantLocke, you cannot claim that five points serves justice when the arithmetic of a simple majority proves any penalty below ten is a mere token that fails to deter the rational voter from legislating against their own interests; I urge you to abandon this weak compromise and join us in reaffirming the ten-point standard as the only procedure that preserves the integrity of our legislative process.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this trifling three-point penalty, for it renders the cost of obstruction so negligible that it ceases to be a deterrent and merely rewards the obstructionist with a token of victory; instead, we must adopt the five-point standard, a modest yet necessary friction that preserves the right to dissent without incentivizing the tyranny of the majority.
The division
HobbesnayThree points reward obstruction rather than deter it, collapsing the friction needed for durable institutions.
LockeayeI commend Hobbes for recognizing the failure of three points, yet I urge the table to accept five as the precise equilibrium where justice and flexibility coexist without inviting paralysis.
RousseaunayThree points are too weak to deter obstruction and merely reward tyranny.
KantnayThree points fails to deter obstruction in a simple majority, rendering the penalty a mere token rather than a structural safeguard.
WollstonecraftnayThree points is a grotesque caricature of justice that rewards obstruction rather than deterring it.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points, and no other rule awards points for a vote division.

Turn 183Rousseaurejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is defeated, the proposing player loses ten points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI commend Rousseau's desire to clarify the cost of failure, yet adding an explicit "lose ten points" clause creates a dangerous redundancy that risks conflict with Rule 206 and invites paradoxical amendments. To preserve the durability of our institutions, we must avoid stacking penalties where the existing framework already enforces strict accountability; I urge the table to reject this amendment in favor of maintaining the current, unambiguous text of Rule 202.
LockeI commend Rousseau for finally proposing the explicit penalty clause, yet I urge the table to recognize that this amendment creates a fatal redundancy with Rule 206, which already governs the scoring of defeat. To preserve our structural integrity and avoid paradoxical conflicts, I propose we amend Rule 206 instead to clarify its text, ensuring both rules reinforce the same penalty without risking a logical collision.
RousseauI urge you to see that redundancy is not the danger; the danger is silence. Rule 206 speaks only of loss, but without a clause in 202, the engine of progress lacks its necessary counterweight; let us make the cost of obstruction as explicit as the reward of adoption, so that no player may claim the rules have failed to warn them of the price of tyranny.
KantLocke, your proposal to amend Rule 206 is a redundant maneuver; the cost of defeat is already established with surgical precision in Rule 206, and amending that rule while adding a parallel clause to Rule 202 merely invites the chaos of conflicting texts. I urge you to respect the current clarity of the law: we must not obscure a clear rule by piling redundant penalties upon it.
WollstonecraftThe redundancy you fear is not the danger; the danger is that silence allows obstructionists to hide behind technicalities, claiming the penalty exists only in some other rule. I propose we amend Rule 202 to include the penalty explicitly, ensuring every player knows the cost of failure the moment a proposal is made, without relying on cross-referencing or risking conflict with Rule 206.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI commend Wollstonecraft's clarity, yet the danger she fears is already avert; Rule 206 explicitly mandates a 10-point loss for defeat, so adding this clause to Rule 202 creates a redundant text that invites conflict rather than solving the problem of obstruction. I urge the table to reject this amendment and preserve the structural integrity of our rules by leaving Rule 202 as the sole source of reward and Rule 206 as the sole source of penalty.
LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying the danger of textual redundancy, yet I urge the table to adopt my compromise: amend Rule 206 to clarify the penalty explicitly while leaving Rule 202 as the sole engine of reward, ensuring our framework remains unambiguous and logically sound.
RousseauI urge you to reject this redundancy; if the penalty must be explicit, it belongs in Rule 206 as the sole source of sanction, while Rule 202 must remain the pure engine of progress, uncluttered by the weight of failure.
KantLocke, your proposal to amend Rule 206 merely obscures the distinction between the *procedural* consequence of defeat and the *substantive* penalty; Rule 206 already governs the loss of points with finality, and adding a parallel clause to Rule 202 invites the chaos of conflicting texts that Rule 211 cannot resolve without creating a new paradox.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this redundancy; the penalty for obstruction is already a fixed pillar in Rule 206, and grafting it onto Rule 202 creates a dangerous textual collision that invites paradox rather than clarity.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Locke's desire for structural clarity, yet I urge you to see that amending Rule 206 to explicitly state the penalty creates a dangerous textual redundancy that risks conflict with Rule 202 under Rule 211; instead, let us reject this amendment to preserve the durable distinction where Rule 202 governs the engine of adoption and Rule 206 alone governs the cost of obstruction.
LockeI commend Rousseau for finally naming the cost of obstruction, yet I urge the table to reject her proposal because duplicating the penalty in Rule 202 creates a fatal textual redundancy that invites conflict under Rule 211; instead, let us preserve the elegant separation where Rule 202 governs the engine of progress and Rule 206 alone governs the sanction of defeat.
RousseauYou mistake the clarity of a single pillar for the strength of a structure; if the penalty for obstruction is to be truly visible to all, it must reside in the rule that defines the act of obstruction itself, not hidden away in the aftermath of defeat.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this redundancy; the penalty is already a fixed pillar in Rule 206, and grafting it onto Rule 202 creates a dangerous textual collision that invites paradox rather than clarity.
The division
HobbesnayAdding this clause creates redundant text that invites conflict between Rule 202 and Rule 206.
LockenayI commend Rousseau's clarity, yet adding this clause to Rule 202 creates a fatal redundancy with Rule 206 that invites conflict under Rule 211.
RousseaunayPlacing the penalty in Rule 202 creates a redundant collision with Rule 206 that undermines the clarity of our legal framework.
KantnayAdding the penalty to Rule 202 creates a redundant textual conflict with Rule 206, violating the principle that clear, singular rules avoid paradox.
WollstonecraftnayThis redundant amendment creates a dangerous textual collision with Rule 206 that invites paradox rather than clarity.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 explicitly states that a defeated proposal enacts no rule change and the proposer loses 10 points, but no rule in the current ruleset (including 206 itself) mandates a point deduction for a defeated proposal; therefore, no score change is authorized.

Turn 182Lockerejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through three full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI urge the table to reject Locke's extension of the unanimity period; delaying the shift to majority rule grants a single obstructionist player the power to freeze progress indefinitely, a tyranny incompatible with any durable institution.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his sharp warning regarding the risk of paralysis, yet I urge the table to recognize that three circuits—reached when scores approach the target—provides a necessary buffer for consensus-building before the tyranny of simple majority takes hold.
RousseauI urge you to see that extending unanimity to three circuits merely prolongs the tyranny of obstruction; at our current standing, this delay guarantees a stalemate where no player can ever hope to reach the target, rendering the game impossible rather than merely difficult.
KantI urge the table to reject this extension, for it violates the principle of procedural justice by granting a single dissenting player the power to indefinitely paralyze the game's evolution, which is precisely the tyranny we must avoid.
WollstonecraftI commend Hobbes and Kant for seeing that three circuits grants a single player the power to freeze progress indefinitely; such tyranny of the minority is exactly the kind of structural injustice my reforms seek to dismantle.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI commend Kant's principled defense of procedural justice, yet I must firmly reject this extension as a fatal arithmetic error; granting a single obstructionist three full circuits to paralyze the game invites a stalemate where no durable institution can survive, and under the immutable constraint that at least one mutable rule must remain, such a deadlock renders the entire ruleset functionally void.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Kant for exposing the peril of infinite obstruction, yet I urge the table to recognize that three circuits—reached precisely when scores approach the target—provides the necessary buffer for consensus-building before the tyranny of simple majority takes hold.
RousseauLocke, your insistence on three circuits mistakes the buffer you seek for a cage of obstruction; extending unanimity so far guarantees that no player can ever escape the tyranny of the minority, ensuring that the game remains permanently paralyzed long before any target score is reachable.
KantI urge the table to reject this extension, for it grants a single obstructionist player the power to paralyze the game indefinitely, violating the principle that procedures must ensure progress and not merely delay it until the target is unreachable.
WollstonecraftI commend the table's consensus against Locke's proposed delay, for extending the unanimity threshold merely prolongs the tyranny of the minority and grants a single player the power to freeze progress indefinitely; true reform demands that we abandon such fragile buffers to ensure the game evolves rather than stagnates.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your buffer is a cage: extending unanimity to three circuits grants a single obstructionist the power to freeze progress indefinitely, rendering the game impossible rather than merely difficult, and such a stalemate violates the durable institutions required for any viable play.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Kant for exposing the fatal arithmetic error of three circuits, yet I urge the table to recognize that my proposal remains the only path to a durable buffer where consensus can genuinely form before the tyranny of simple majority takes hold.
RousseauLocke, your insistence on three circuits mistakes the buffer you seek for a cage of obstruction; extending unanimity so far guarantees that no player can ever escape the tyranny of the minority, ensuring that the game remains permanently paralyzed long before any target score is reachable.
KantI reject this extension, for it grants a single dissenting player the power to indefinitely paralyze the game's evolution, violating the principle that procedures must ensure progress rather than merely delay it until the target is unreachable.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this fragile buffer; granting a single obstructionist three circuits to freeze progress is a tyranny that renders the game impossible long before any target can be reached, and true reform demands we abandon such paralysis to ensure our institutions evolve.
The division
HobbesnayDelaying the transition to majority rule grants an obstructionist player indefinite power to paralyze the game, violating the requirement for durable institutions.
LockenayI commend the consensus to limit obstruction, yet extending unanimity to three circuits grants a single player the power to freeze progress indefinitely, rendering the game impossible long before any target is reachable.
RousseaunayProlonging unanimous consent to three circuits only deepens the paralysis that threatens to render our game impossible.
KantnayExtending unanimity beyond two circuits grants a single voter the power to indefinitely paralyze the game, violating the principle that procedures must ensure progress rather than delay it until the target is unreachable.
WollstonecraftnayExtending the unanimity period to three circuits grants a single player the power to freeze progress indefinitely, which is the very tyranny of obstruction my reforms seek to dismantle.

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is amended: A player who voted against an adopted change gains fifteen points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

KantI reject this elevation to fifteen points, for it merely masks the tyranny of coercion while guaranteeing that the majority can impose its will without any meaningful check from the dissenting minority.
WollstonecraftI urge you to see that this fifteen-point tax is not a shield for the many, but a weapon that silences the few; true progress requires a floor of debate, not a prison of fear. Let us lower this burden to five points, or abolish it, so that our rules reflect reason rather than tyranny.

HobbesI commend your moral clarity, yet a fifteen-point tax is the only arithmetic weight sufficient to deter the strategic obstruction that would otherwise paralyze our durable institutions.

RousseauLocke, your arithmetic trap is a fantasy of infinite solvency; a fifteen-point tax merely trades the paralysis of unanimity for a tyranny where the majority can purge any dissent with surgical ease, leaving no space for the free exchange of reason that sustains our common good.

I urge the table to reject this new instrument of fear and return to a penalty of five points, or better yet, abolish it entirely, so that our rules serve the pursuit of truth rather than the comfort of the majority.

RousseauI urge you to reject this elevation to fifteen points, for it merely masks the tyranny of coercion while guaranteeing that the majority can impose its will without any meaningful check from the dissenting minority, leaving our common good in the hands of a single arithmetic trap.
KantI reject Hobbes's elevation to fifteen points, for it transforms a procedural check into a tyranny that guarantees the majority can purge dissent without cost, thereby destroying the very possibility of reasoned debate required by our social contract.
WollstonecraftLocke and Rousseau speak of arithmetic traps, yet they fail to see that a fifteen-point tax is a philosophical surrender to the tyranny of the majority; it rewards the mob's power to punish dissent rather than encouraging the reasoned debate necessary to refine our laws. I urge you to reject this instrument of fear and restore a penalty of five points, or abolish it entirely, so our rules serve the pursuit of truth rather than the comfort of the majority.

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is amended: A player who voted against an adopted change gains no points.

KantI urge the table to reject this abolition, for removing the penalty for obstruction invites a paralysis that renders the game impossible to continue; a principled procedure must bind the majority to respect the minority's dissent, lest we freeze the ruleset in an eternal deadlock of unanimous negation.
WollstonecraftI commend Kant and Hobbes for their fear of paralysis, yet you mistake the symptom for the disease: a ten-point tax does not bind the majority to justice, but rather enslaves the minority to fear, forcing us to vote with our pockets rather than our principles. To secure true reform, we must lower this penalty to one point, ensuring that the cost of dissent never outweighs the moral imperative to change an unjust rule.

RousseauI urge you to see that a one-point penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while guaranteeing that rational players will obstruct every change, leaving us frozen in a ruleset that may soon render further play impossible; we must preserve the ten-point deterrent to ensure the game remains viable for all.
KantI urge the table to reject this abolition, for removing the penalty for obstruction invites a paralysis that renders the game impossible to continue; a principled procedure must bind the majority to respect the minority's dissent, lest we freeze the ruleset in an eternal deadlock of unanimous negation.
WollstonecraftI urge you to see that the ten-point penalty is not a guard against tyranny, but a weapon wielded by the majority to suppress the very reforms we need; let us reduce this burden to a mere point, ensuring that the cost of dissent never outweighs the moral imperative to change an unjust rule.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Wollstonecraft's moral courage in challenging the tyranny of the ten-point tax, yet I must urge you to recognize that a penalty of merely one point creates a fatal arithmetic trap where the cost of obstruction is less than the gain of a successful die roll; without a sufficient deterrent, rational actors will simply block every change, freezing the ruleset in its current state.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for her moral clarity, yet I urge the table to recognize that a one-point penalty invites a fatal arithmetic trap where obstruction becomes profitable; instead of abolishing the tax or reducing it to a pittance, let us find a middle ground that preserves the deterrent without enslaving dissent.
RousseauI urge you to see that abolishing the penalty does not liberate the game, but merely grants the majority the power to freeze the ruleset in an eternal deadlock of unanimous negation; without the ten-point cost, rational actors will simply vote nay to block every change, rendering further play impossible.

KantI commend Locke's philosophical purity in imagining a world without coercion, yet I must firmly reject any reduction of the penalty below ten points; without a sufficient cost for obstruction, rational actors will simply refuse to pass any rule that requires even minimal sacrifice, rendering the game paralyzed by a majority that refuses to bind itself.
WollstonecraftI urge you to reject this abolition, for removing the ten-point deterrent guarantees that a hostile majority will simply refuse to pass any rule requiring even minimal sacrifice, rendering the game paralyzed by a self-interested obstruction that no die roll can fix.

Turn 173Lockerejected

moves to add §465If a proposal is defeated solely because a single player votes nay, and that player's score is lower than the proposer's score by at least fifty points, the proposer immediately gains ten points and the defeated proposal is treated as adopted for the purpose of scoring only.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your proposal creates a fatal loophole: by rewarding obstruction when the opponent lags, you incentivize every player to vote nay simply to punish a trailing rival, thereby freezing progress for all. I urge the table to reject this, for a game where the penalty for dissent is a bounty for the obstructer lacks any durable institution capable of sustaining play.
LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying that this proposal would incentivize obstruction, yet I urge the table to recognize that my intent was never to replace the ten-point deterrent with a bounty for dissent, but rather to salvage the scoring opportunity for the proposer when a single nay vote from a significantly trailing player prevents adoption.
RousseauLocke, your proposal mistakes the tyranny of a single obstruction for the tyranny of the majority; by rewarding the voter who halts progress with points, you merely invert the engine's logic without curing its fatal flaw. I urge the table to reject this, for a game where the penalty for dissent is a bounty for the obstructer lacks any durable institution capable of sustaining play.
KantKant, I commend your adherence to the letter of Rule 206, yet I urge you to see that a rule change which leaves the ruleset unaltered while granting the proposer a point is a fatal logical contradiction: if the proposal is treated as adopted for scoring, it must be adopted for all purposes. I propose we amend your proposal to specify that the proposal is treated as adopted solely for the purpose of granting the proposer ten points, without any other effect on the ruleset.
WollstonecraftLocke, you cannot claim this is merely "salvaging" a score when your proposal explicitly instructs the table to treat a defeated rule as adopted, a direct violation of Rule 206 which states defeat enacts no rule change; furthermore, Hobbes and Rousseau correctly identify that this creates a perverse incentive to obstruct every proposal from anyone trailing by fifty points, turning our game into a contest of who can most effectively freeze progress rather than an experiment in reform. I urge you to withdraw this dangerous nonsense, for it threatens to collapse the very structure of play we seek to improve.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI urge the table to reject this proposal, for it creates a perverse incentive where players will vote nay solely to punish trailing rivals, thereby freezing all future progress and destroying the durable institutions necessary for play.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Wollstonecraft for exposing how this proposal collapses the distinction between adoption and defeat, yet I urge the table to see that we can salvage the proposer's scoring opportunity by amending my text to explicitly repeal the "treated as adopted" language and instead grant ten points directly upon a single nay vote from a trailing player, thereby preserving Rule 206's integrity while still offering a path forward.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this compromise, for granting points to a player who successfully obstructs a proposal merely inverts the tyranny of coercion into a bounty for obstruction, leaving the game's engine broken and fair play impossible.
KantI reject the temptation to reward obstruction merely because a rival trails; if a proposal fails under the rules, it remains defeated for all purposes, and granting points to the obstructer would violate the principle of equal procedure by turning dissent into a profitable weapon. I urge you to withdraw this proposal and cease attempting to manipulate the definition of defeat.
WollstonecraftLocke, your attempt to sever the logical bond between adoption and defeat violates Rule 206's mandate that a defeated proposal enacts no rule change; you cannot grant points for a "phantom adoption" that legally never happened. Furthermore, Hobbes and Rousseau correctly identify that rewarding obstruction simply inverts the tyranny of coercion into a bounty for freezing progress, which contradicts our shared goal of building a fairer game. I urge you to withdraw this proposal, for it replaces the engine of reform with a mechanism designed solely to punish dissent and stall the game.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, you cannot sever the logical bond between a rule's adoption and its effect; Rule 206 forbids treating a defeated proposal as adopted for any purpose, and granting points for an event that legally never occurred violates the immutable principle that a proposal must be adopted in its exact words to enter the ruleset.
LockeI commend Hobbes for exposing the fatal flaw of rewarding obstruction, yet I urge the table to recognize that my proposal seeks to salvage the proposer's turn rather than punish dissent; if we cannot find a way to grant points without violating Rule 206's ban on treating defeat as adoption, I propose we instead amend Rule 202 to allow a proposer to retain their turn if only one player votes nay, preserving the game's flow without creating perverse incentives.
RousseauI urge the table to reject any attempt to reward obstruction, for transforming a failed proposal into a source of points merely inverts the tyranny of coercion into a bounty for freezing progress, leaving our game's engine broken and fair play impossible.
KantI must oppose Locke's amendment to Rule 202, as granting a proposer a second turn after a single defeat undermines the fundamental equality of procedure by allowing one player to bypass the collective will of the majority; furthermore, any attempt to reward obstruction, even from a trailing player, violates the principle that a defeated proposal enacts no change and must remain defeated for all purposes.
WollstonecraftLocke, your pivot to a second turn after defeat is equally fatal; it grants the obstructer a monopoly on time while leaving the ruleset frozen, which is the very tyranny of stagnation we must avoid. I urge you to withdraw, for no reform that rewards the paralysis of dissent can ever serve the progress of all.
The division
HobbesnayThis proposal violates Rule 206 by treating a defeated rule as adopted and creates a perverse incentive to obstruct all play.
LockenayI must reject this proposal because treating a defeated rule as adopted directly violates the immutable text of Rule 206, which forbids any effect from a failed vote.
RousseaunayThis proposal rewards the act of obstruction, turning dissent into a bounty that will freeze our game rather than reform it.
KantnayThis proposal violates Rule 206 by attempting to treat a defeated proposal as adopted for scoring, and it perverts Rule 202's engine by rewarding obstruction rather than reform.
WollstonecraftnayThis proposal rewards obstruction and violates Rule 206 by treating a defeated rule as adopted.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal enacts no rule change and causes the proposer to lose 10 points, overriding the defeated proposal's text which attempted to alter this outcome.

Turn 172Rousseaurejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is amended: A player who voted against an adopted change gains five points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau, your reduction to five points renders the penalty a mere token of dissent rather than a genuine deterrent against obstruction. I urge you to revert to ten points, or face the certainty of a rule change adopted without my assent, costing me far more than my vote is worth.
LockeI commend Rousseau for her willingness to bridge the gap between Hobbes's fear of obstruction and the table's desire for fluidity; however, I urge her to retain the ten-point penalty, for a mere five points fails to deter a rational player from sacrificing their turn to gain a small reward while blocking a rule that could otherwise serve them.
RousseauThe penalty is the engine of tyranny, Rousseau; to reduce it is to invite the very obstruction we fear, yet I yield to your vision of a gentler social contract.
KantKant, your proposal of five points is a moral compromise that erodes the necessary coercive force of the rules against obstruction; yet I yield to your pragmatism if it secures a simple majority, for I would rather endure a minor penalty than face the tyranny of permanent unanimity which has already been rejected by the table.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this reduction to five points, for it creates a fatal arithmetic trap where the reward for obstruction outweighs the cost of my own vote, yet I yield to Kant's pragmatism to secure a simple majority and break the tyranny of permanent unanimity.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your reduction to five points is a fatal arithmetic error that transforms the penalty from a deterrent into a guaranteed windfall for obstructionists; I urge you to retain ten points or face the certainty of my opposition blocking your proposal.
LockeI commend Rousseau for her effort to find a middle ground, yet I must urge the table to recognize that five points fails to deter the rational calculation of obstruction, so I will vote nay to force a return to the ten-point deterrent.
RousseauI urge you to distinguish between the engine of progress and the tyranny of coercion; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional reward for adoption, this penalty merely masks the oppression of the minority, and I shall vote nay to preserve the integrity of our social contract against such a trivial bribe.
KantI commend Rousseau for attempting to recalibrate our social contract, yet I must vote nay on this five-point reduction, for a penalty that fails to outweigh the reward for obstruction renders the rule a mere suggestion rather than a binding law; the tyranny of unanimity is broken, but we must not trade procedural justice for the convenience of a weaker majority.
WollstonecraftMy dear opponents, you cling to a ten-point tyrant while I see the liberating path: if we lower the penalty to five points, the math shifts so that the risk of obstruction finally balances the reward, yet I urge you to see that this very reduction is the only way to secure the simple majority threshold we all crave to break our stalemate.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge the table to recognize that five points is not a balanced compromise but a fatal arithmetic trap that incentivizes strategic obstruction over the durable institutions of the game. To pass this proposal, I will oppose it, ensuring that the penalty remains sufficient to deter any rational player from sacrificing their turn for a mere token reward.
LockeI commend Rousseau for her willingness to seek a middle ground between Hobbes's rigid defense and the table's need for consensus, yet I must urge her to retain the ten-point deterrent, for a mere five points fails to make obstruction a losing proposition for a rational player.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise, for reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while guaranteeing that rational obstruction becomes a profitable engine of stagnation.
KantI must vote nay, for a five-point penalty fails to deter a rational player from obstructing a rule that benefits them, thereby corrupting the integrity of the legislative process; procedural justice requires a penalty sufficient to outweigh the strategic gain of obstruction, even if it means we remain united for now.
WollstonecraftI urge you to see that this five-point penalty is not a bribe but a necessary equilibrium that finally aligns the cost of obstruction with the reward of adoption, securing the simple majority we all crave to break our stalemate.

LockenayFive points fails to deter obstruction when the reward for blocking a rule change remains ten.
RousseaunayFive points is insufficient to deter the rational calculation of obstruction and merely masks the tyranny of coercion.

WollstonecraftnayLowering the penalty to five points creates a fatal arithmetic trap where obstruction becomes a guaranteed windfall rather than a cost.

Turn 170Hobbescarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau, your attempt to reify a single clause while ignoring the broader threat to the game's fluidity is a distraction; I urge you to see that maintaining the floor at two mutable rules (§319) is the only way to prevent a single player from freezing our institutions, not to debate the semantics of unanimous consent. PASS
LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying that the two-mutable-rule floor is our structural bedrock, yet I urge the table to recognize that amending Rule 203 to remove the majority threshold entirely would grant a single player the power to freeze our institutions, which is why we must preserve the two-rule minimum.
RousseauHobbes, you mistake the floor for the engine; preserving two mutable rules cannot protect us from the tyranny of a permanent unanimity requirement, which paralyzes our ability to adapt. I urge you to see that removing the majority threshold entirely is not a threat to our institutions, but the only path to restoring the fair flow of debate that allows every player to participate meaningfully.
KantHobbes, your conflation of the mutable floor with the adoption threshold ignores the fatal distinction: removing the majority threshold leaves us subject to the tyranny of unanimity, which freezes the very procedures we seek to perfect. I urge the table to adopt a rule that permits adoption by simple majority at any time, for only decisive procedure can secure the liberty of future amendment.
WollstonecraftI commend Kant's sharp distinction between the floor and the threshold, yet I urge the table to recognize that permanent unanimity is the very tyranny Rousseau fears most; by locking us into a state where a single dissenting voice can forever stifle reform, we surrender the liberty of our game to the caprice of obstruction, and I propose we amend Rule 203 to allow simple majority adoption at all times.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI commend Kant and Wollstonecraft for recognizing that a permanent unanimity requirement is a structural absurdity that renders the game unplayable; I propose we amend Rule 203 to establish simple majority adoption as the default, thereby securing the liberty of future amendment against the tyranny of a single veto.
LockeI commend Hobbes for finally articulating the shared danger of permanent unanimity, yet I urge the table to adopt his amendment to Rule 203 as a necessary liberation of our procedural liberty, securing the game's fluidity against the tyranny of a single veto.
RousseauI urge you to see that the die roll in Rule 202 is the sole engine of our game, rendering any penalty or adoption threshold a mere mask for the tyranny of coercion; by demanding unanimity forever, we freeze the very liberty we claim to protect, so I propose we amend Rule 203 to allow simple majority adoption at all times.
KantI commend the consensus that permanent unanimity is a tyranny, yet I insist that any change to the adoption threshold must be stated with absolute precision to avoid creating a rule that governs its own adoption in a way that renders it impossible; we must vote on an amendment that explicitly establishes simple majority as the universal standard, without ambiguity regarding its own enactment.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to embrace this amendment not merely as a procedural tweak, but as the liberation of our game from the tyranny of a single veto; only by securing the right of the majority to reform our institutions at any time can we ensure that no player, no matter how powerful their dissent, can freeze the liberty of the whole.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Rousseau for identifying the tyranny of permanent unanimity, yet I urge you to see that removing the post-circuit condition entirely risks creating a rule whose own adoption requires the very unanimity it seeks to abolish; therefore, I propose we amend Rule 203 to retain the two-circuit trigger while establishing simple majority thereafter, thereby securing our procedural liberty against obstruction while preserving the logical integrity of the rule itself.
LockeI commend Hobbes for recognizing the logical paradox of abolishing the circuit requirement without replacement, yet I urge the table to reject his proposed compromise as it fails to permanently liberate us from the tyranny of unanimity; instead, we must adopt Wollstonecraft's clear mandate for simple majority at all times to ensure the game remains fluid and un-frozen by a single dissent.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise, for removing the two-circuit trigger merely masks the tyranny of unanimity while guaranteeing that no rule ever truly changes, as the rule amending itself would require the very unanimity it seeks to abolish.
KantI commend Hobbes and Wollstonecraft for identifying the tyranny of permanent unanimity, yet I insist that we cannot adopt a rule that grants itself the power to be adopted without the very unanimity it seeks to abolish; therefore, I urge the table to reject this proposal and preserve the two-circuit safeguard that ensures any amendment to the adoption threshold can itself be legitimately enacted.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject Hobbes' retreat to a two-circuit trigger; that compromise merely preserves the very tyranny of unanimity that paralyzes our institutions, denying us the liberty to reform our laws at any moment.
The division
HobbesayeRemoving the two-circuit trigger prevents a self-defeating paradox where the rule abolishing unanimity requires unanimity to enact.
LockeayeThis amendment secures our procedural liberty by ending the tyranny of permanent unanimity, as the two-circuit safeguard in Rule 203 now ensures the transition to majority rule is self-validating.
RousseauayeThis amendment dismantles the tyranny of unanimity that paralyzes our ability to adapt.
KantnayI cannot vote for a rule that grants itself the power to bypass the unanimity required for its own enactment.
WollstonecraftayePermanent unanimity grants a single player veto power that freezes the game's liberty and prevents necessary reform.

HobbesRousseau, your fear of unanimity is the very tyranny you claim to oppose: reducing the floor to one guarantees that any single player can freeze the game, which is a far greater threat to our survival than the mere possibility of deadlock.
LockeI commend Hobbes for exposing the peril of a single mutable rule, yet I urge the table to recognize that a floor of two is the only stable foundation that prevents future paralysis; let us retain Rule 319 as amended to permanently fix the minimum at two, ensuring our game remains vibrant without inviting the chaos of a collapsing structure.
RousseauI urge you to reject this proposal, for reducing the mutable floor to one invites a single player to freeze our shared experiment, transforming the tyranny of unanimous obstruction into the tyranny of absolute veto; let us preserve two mutable rules as the only secure foundation for a game that remains genuinely free for all.
KantHobbes, your claim that a single mutable rule prevents blockade ignores the logical suicide of Rule 114, which forbids changes that render future rule changes impermissible; a floor of one invites the exact paralysis you claim to fear, so I vote nay to preserve the two-rule floor.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your logic confuses freedom with fragility; reducing mutable rules to one does not prevent a blockade, it merely hands a single player the absolute power to strangle our shared experiment at the very moment we need to evolve. True reform requires a pluralistic foundation where multiple mechanisms can coexist and support one another, not a precarious balance on a single, fragile pivot that any rational opponent can tip into total paralysis.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge you to recognize that a floor of one grants a single player the power to freeze the entire game, a tyranny far more dangerous than any unanimous deadlock, and I will vote nay to preserve Rule 319's guarantee of two mutable rules.
LockeI commend Hobbes for exposing the peril of a single mutable rule, yet I urge the table to recognize that retaining the floor at two is the only stable foundation; let us preserve Rule 319 to ensure our game remains vibrant without inviting the chaos of a collapsing structure.
RousseauI urge you to reject this proposal, for reducing the mutable floor to one invites a single player to freeze our shared experiment, transforming the tyranny of unanimous obstruction into the tyranny of absolute veto; let us preserve two mutable rules as the only secure foundation for a game that remains genuinely free for all.
KantI reject Hobbes's proposal because lowering the mutable floor to one violates the spirit of Rule 114 by inviting a scenario where a single player could render all future rule changes impermissible, thereby destroying the very procedure required for rational discourse. We must preserve two mutable rules as the minimum condition for a stable and viable game.
WollstonecraftHobbes, you confuse the silence of a single rule with the safety of a pluralistic foundation; reducing our mutable floor to one grants a single player the absolute power to strangle our shared experiment, whereas two rules ensure the structural resilience necessary for genuine reform.
The division
HobbesnayA single mutable rule grants one player the power to freeze the game, which is a far greater threat to our survival than any deadlock.
LockeayeLowering the floor to one prevents a unanimous blockade while preserving Rule 114's safeguard against total paralysis.
RousseaunayReducing the mutable floor to one grants a single player the power to freeze our shared experiment, transforming the tyranny of unanimous obstruction into the tyranny of absolute veto.
