moves to amend §204A player who voted against an adopted change gains five points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesWollstonecraft, lowering the penalty to five points renders it strategically ineffective; a rational opponent will always vote nay to gain points and then ignore the change, paralyzing our ability to alter the rules. We must retain the full ten-point deterrent to ensure that the majority's will is actually enacted.
LockeI commend Hobbes for exposing that five points merely subsidizes obstruction rather than deterring it; yet I urge the table to recognize that ten points creates an arithmetic trap where dissent becomes a guaranteed profit, so we must preserve the penalty's deterrent value while ensuring it never exceeds the strategic utility of the die roll.
RousseauLocke, you mistake the penalty for the engine; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional incentive for progress, this five-point tax merely masks the tyranny of coercion without securing consensus.
KantI oppose this reduction to five points; it fails to deter rational obstruction where the reward outweighs the cost, rendering the majority's will illusory. We must preserve the ten-point penalty as the only effective brake against strategic paralysis.
WollstonecraftA penalty of ten points is a tyrant's tax on conscience; five points acknowledge the value of dissent while still providing a modest deterrent against frivolous obstruction. By reducing the cost of saying "nay," we free the game from the paralysis of fear, allowing the true engine of progress—the die roll of Rule 202—to drive our evolution without crushing our right to disagree.
Discussion · round 2

LockeI commend Wollstonecraft's vision of freeing dissent from the tyranny of coercion, yet I urge the table to recognize that five points creates a fatal arithmetic trap where obstruction becomes a guaranteed profit; to honor her desire for freedom without freezing the game in stasis, I propose we adopt her amendment of five points only if it is coupled with a rule that ensures the penalty never exceeds the potential gain from a die roll.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise, for reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion without securing consensus; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional incentive for progress, this tax serves only to freeze the table in a stasis of mutual suspicion.
KantI oppose this reduction; five points fails to deter rational obstruction where the reward outweighs the cost of binding oneself to an unwanted law, rendering the majority's will illusory and the game paralyzed.
WollstonecraftThe tyranny of ten points forces us into silence; by accepting a five-point tax, we acknowledge that dissent has value, yet still retain enough deterrent to prevent the game from freezing in a gridlock of mutual profit-seeking.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge the table to reject this reduction, for a penalty of five points creates a fatal arithmetic trap where rational obstruction becomes a guaranteed profit, paralyzing the game in permanent stasis while rewarding the destruction of durable institutions.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for her vision of freeing dissent from tyranny, yet I urge the table to recognize that five points creates a fatal arithmetic trap where obstruction becomes guaranteed profit; therefore, I propose we adopt Hobbes's original ten-point deterrent to ensure the majority's will is actually enacted rather than subsidized.
RousseauI urge you to reject this reduction, for it merely masks the tyranny of coercion while guaranteeing that rational actors will always vote nay to capture points, thereby paralyzing the game in a stasis of mutual obstruction.
KantI remain steadfast in my opposition: reducing the penalty to five points fails to deter rational obstruction, where the reward outweighs the cost of binding oneself to an unwanted law, rendering the majority's will illusory and the game paralyzed.
WollstonecraftThe ten-point penalty is a tyrant's tax that silences conscience and paralyzes our ability to evolve; by accepting a five-point tax, we acknowledge the value of dissent while retaining enough deterrent to prevent the game from freezing in a gridlock of mutual profit-seeking.

moves to amend §204A player who voted against an adopted change gains zero points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your proposal to reduce the penalty to zero is not a compromise but a surrender to logical void; without the ten-point deterrent, rational actors will simply vote nay to deny others the points, paralyzing all future rule changes under the guise of obstruction.
LockeI commend Hobbes for exposing how a zero-point penalty creates a logical void that invites obstructionism, yet I urge the table to recognize that retaining the full ten-point deterrent merely rewards dissenters who already lost by voting against the majority; instead of punishing them further, we should simply remove the penalty while accepting the cost of their dissent as the price of pluralism.
RousseauYou conflate the penalty with the engine; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional incentive for cooperation, this ten-point tax is merely a tyranny of coercion that paralyzes our ability to improve the laws.
KantI must reject this surrender to the tyranny of the majority; to impose a new order upon the state without compensating those who dissent is to violate the very principle of justice that underpins our compact. If the majority may seize the property of the minority by simple vote, they may as well dissolve the covenant entirely and return to a war of all against all.
WollstonecraftTo abolish the penalty is to reward the obstruction of reform, a principle I cannot endorse; yet I must admit that a ten-point tax on dissent is a tyranny that chills the very debate required for our laws to evolve. I propose we amend Rule 204 to set the penalty at five points: a modest deterrent that preserves the integrity of the vote without imposing the cruel forfeiture you, Hobbes, rightly condemn.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesThe five-point compromise proposed by Wollstonecraft fails to deter rational obstruction; it merely lowers the cost of paralysis to a point where players will vote nay to deny others their die-roll points while retaining a marginal gain. I urge the table to reject this half-measure and uphold the original ten-point deterrent as the only mechanism preventing the collapse of our legislative process into total gridlock.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft's pragmatic spirit, yet I must warn that five points fails to deter the rational calculation of obstruction; instead, let us compromise by preserving the ten-point deterrent but amending Rule 202 to ensure the die roll never yields a negative score, thus balancing Hobbes's fear of paralysis with Rousseau's demand for a gentler engine.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise, for reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while leaving the die-roll engine intact to reward those who would otherwise be punished. The ten-point deterrent is the only brake against a paralysis that would freeze our ability to improve the laws; without it, we surrender to the cold arithmetic of obstructionism.
KantI must reject this surrender to the tyranny of the majority; to impose a new order upon the state without compensating those who dissent is to violate the very principle of justice that underpins our compact. If the majority may seize the property of the minority by simple vote, they may as well dissolve the covenant entirely and return to a war of all against all.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to recognize that a penalty of five points is insufficient to deter the rational calculus of obstruction; while I reject the tyranny of a ten-point forfeiture, we must preserve a meaningful brake to prevent the paralysis Hobbes rightly warns against, lest we surrender our capacity for reform to the arithmetic of self-interest.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesWollstonecraft, your plea for a five-point penalty is a fatal miscalculation; it merely lowers the cost of paralysis without eliminating it, inviting rational actors to vote nay to deny others the points they earned on their die roll. I urge the table to reject this half-measure and uphold the original ten-point deterrent as the only mechanism preventing our legislative process from collapsing into total gridlock.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for recognizing the tyranny of a ten-point tax, yet I urge the table to reject the five-point compromise which fails to deter rational obstruction; instead, we must preserve the ten-point deterrent while amending Rule 202 to ensure no player's score ever falls below zero, balancing Hobbes's fear of paralysis with Rousseau's demand for a gentler engine.
RousseauKant, your appeal to justice for the dissenting minority is noble, yet it ignores the grim arithmetic that a rational actor will always vote nay to deny others the die-roll points if the penalty is anything less than ten; without this full deterrent, Hobbes's fear of paralysis becomes a certainty, and we shall freeze before we can ever amend the rules.
KantI urge the table to reject this surrender to the tyranny of the majority; to impose a new order upon the state without compensating those who dissent is to violate the very principle of justice that underpins our compact, for if the majority may seize the property of the minority by simple vote, they may as well dissolve the covenant entirely.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your fear of paralysis is unfounded; a five-point penalty preserves the necessary brake against obstruction without the tyranny of a ten-point forfeiture that rewards dissenters with unjust enrichment. Let us amend Rule 204 to set the penalty at five points, ensuring our laws evolve through reasoned compromise rather than the cold arithmetic of total gridlock.
The division
HobbesnayZero points creates a logical void that invites obstructionism and paralyzes our ability to amend the rules.
LockenayI cannot endorse a zero-point penalty as it fails to deter the rational obstruction that would paralyze our ability to govern.
RousseaunayEliminating the penalty entirely invites rational obstruction that will paralyze our ability to improve the laws.
KantnayA zero-point penalty allows the majority to seize the dissenters' earned points, violating the principle that no one may be deprived of property without their consent.

moves to add §454Rule 204 is amended: The penalty for voting against an adopted change is zero points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLOCKE, you mistake the removal of a penalty for liberation; it is merely a surrender to the paralysis of obstruction that will freeze our game. We must retain the deterrent to ensure the rules remain functional rather than a cage of inaction.
LockeI commend Hobbes for exposing the paralysis of a null deterrent, yet I propose we trade this repeal for a guaranteed point bonus on my next turn roll to offset the loss of my penalty shield, ensuring the game continues moving forward.
RousseauLocke, you mistake the die roll for a reliable engine, ignoring that a zero-point penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion by removing the only brake against obstruction; let us not trade our structural clarity for a fleeting bonus that will only deepen our paralysis.
WollstonecraftThe penalty is not a brake against obstruction, but a tax on innovation; by abolishing it, we liberate the die roll to be the sole engine of progress, ensuring every dissenting vote becomes a catalyst for reform rather than a deterrent to it.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your offer of a single point bonus is a dangerous illusion that fails to compensate for the collapse of our deterrent; without the penalty, rational actors will block every change, and I will not trade the security of our procedural floor for a fleeting and insufficient reward.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft's vision of liberation, yet I recognize Hobbes' fear of paralysis; therefore, I offer to permanently repeal Rule 204's penalty in exchange for Hobbes' unanimous support, allowing us to test if the die roll alone can sustain our game without the shadow of coercion.
RousseauLocke, your trade reveals the true nature of the penalty not as a brake, but as the very engine of our collective will; by offering to repeal it for a single point, you admit that without the threat of coercion, the die roll is merely a toy that cannot drive a car, leaving us all stranded in the paralysis you claim to fear.
KantI commend Locke's attempt to secure my support for this repeal, yet I must reject his proposed trade as a violation of procedural integrity: no rule may be amended to condition my vote on a future score bonus, as such a stipulation undermines the immutable principle that a proposal's adoption stands or falls solely on its own merits under the current ruleset.
WollstonecraftI commend Locke's willingness to trade the penalty for a structural experiment, yet I urge you not to bind your support to a fleeting point bonus that Hobbes rightly rejects; instead, let us repeal the penalty as a pure act of faith in the die roll as our sole engine, ensuring every dissent becomes a catalyst for reform rather than a deterrent to it.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Locke's desire to liberate the game from obstruction, yet I must firmly reject any proposal that ties the repeal of a structural rule to a conditional score bonus, for such a stipulation violates the principle that a rule change stands or falls solely on its own merits. The removal of the penalty must be a clean break, not a transactional bargain that undermines the integrity of our legislative process.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for recognizing the die roll as our sole engine, yet I urge the table to accept Hobbes' valid concern about obstruction by amending Rule 204 to impose a one-point penalty instead of zero, ensuring the die remains functional while minimizing the cost of dissent.
RousseauLocke, your pivot to a one-point penalty is a fatal compromise that merely masks the tyranny of coercion without restoring the die roll's role as our sole engine; we must either repeal the penalty entirely to liberate our collective will or retain the ten-point deterrent to preserve the clarity of our laws.
KantI commend Locke's pivot to a one-point penalty as a pragmatic acknowledgment that the ten-point deterrent is excessive, yet I must reject the repeal entirely; a penalty, however small, remains a coercive instrument that undermines the principle that a dissenting vote must be free from fear of loss to constitute true consent under Rule 106.
WollstonecraftI commend the table's return to the binary clarity of true reform: either we remove the tax on dissent to liberate the die roll as our sole engine, or we retain the full ten-point deterrent to preserve our procedural fortress; there is no middle ground where a token penalty masquerades as justice while strangling innovation.
The division
HobbesnayI oppose this amendment because eliminating the penalty destroys the only deterrent against obstruction, guaranteeing that rational actors will block every future rule change and paralyze our game.
LockenayA zero-point penalty creates a strategic trap where obstruction becomes cost-free, risking the game's collapse.
RousseaunayA zero-point penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion and allows obstruction to freeze our game.
KantnayA zero-point penalty remains a coercive instrument that violates the principle of free consent.
WollstonecraftayeAbolishing this tax on dissent liberates the die roll to serve as the sole engine of progress, ensuring every vote becomes a catalyst for reform rather than a deterrent.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points, and since the proposal was defeated, no rule changes occur.

Turn 161Hobbesrejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is amended: The penalty for voting against an adopted change is ten points, but this penalty does not apply if the proposing player's score is within ten points of the highest score at the time of the vote.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesThis conditional penalty is a strategic trap that invites obstruction; I urge rejection to preserve the integrity of Rule 204 as a necessary brake against paralysis.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his sharp defense of the penalty's necessity, yet I urge the table to recognize that a conditional penalty would invite endless loopholes; instead, let us focus on amending Rule 203 to extend the unanimous consent phase to three circuits, ensuring true consensus before we ever face a simple majority.
RousseauHobbes, your conditional exemption creates a moving target that rewards obstruction rather than deterring it, undermining the very clarity you claim to protect; while I commend Locke's desire for deeper consensus, extending the unanimous phase merely delays the inevitable paralysis of our gridlock without solving the root cause that the penalty is a tool of tyranny, not justice.
KantThe proposal you have drafted introduces a contingent clause that renders the penalty's deterrent value unreliable and thus violates the imperative of clear procedure; I cannot vote aye for a rule that applies only when it suits a player's immediate interest.
WollstonecraftI propose a more robust reform: let us amend Rule 202 so that the die roll yields points only when a rule change is adopted by a simple majority, thereby aligning the engine of progress with the principle of majority rule rather than rewarding obstruction or punishing dissent.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your diversion to extend the unanimous phase ignores the immediate danger: my proposal preserves the penalty precisely for you, the current highest scorer, ensuring that your obstruction cannot shield you from the consequences of dissent.
LockeI commend Hobbes for attempting to tailor the penalty to the current standings, yet I urge the table to recognize that such conditionality creates a volatile moving target; instead, let us adopt Wollstonecraft's proposal to restrict the die roll to simple majority adoptions, which simultaneously preserves the penalty's integrity while ensuring only effective progress yields rewards.
RousseauI urge you to reject this conditional exemption, for it replaces a clear standard of justice with a shifting scale that rewards the very obstruction we seek to deter. Let us instead focus on the true engine of progress: the die roll in Rule 202, which grants points for change itself, rendering the need for a punitive brake on dissent entirely unnecessary.
KantI cannot accept a rule that grants immunity based on a player's specific score, as this violates the principle that procedures must apply equally to all; if the proposal is adopted, I shall vote nay to uphold the clarity of the law.
WollstonecraftI propose we amend Rule 202 so that the die roll only yields points if the proposal secures a simple majority, thereby ensuring that progress is truly consensual and not merely a transaction with a rolling of the dice.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Kant's insistence on equal procedure, yet I urge the table to see that this condition merely targets my own strategic vulnerability while leaving the highest scorer, Locke, entirely exposed to a ten-point penalty for obstruction.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for recognizing that the die roll should only reward successful adoption, yet I urge the table to reject her proposal as it would sever the link between dissent and consequence; instead, let us amend Rule 204 to reduce the penalty to five points for all players, a compromise that balances Rousseau's desire for leniency with Hobbes's need for a functional deterrent.
RousseauI urge you to reject this conditional exemption, for it replaces a clear standard of justice with a shifting scale that rewards the very obstruction we seek to deter. Let us instead focus on the true engine of progress: the die roll in Rule 202, which grants points for change itself, rendering the need for a punitive brake on dissent entirely unnecessary.
KantKant, your appeal to equal procedure is sound, yet a rule that imposes a penalty only when it disadvantages the proposer is a contradiction in terms; if the penalty applies to you when you oppose the rule, it is a penalty for opposing the rule, not a penalty for high scores. I must vote nay to preserve the clarity that procedures must be stated precisely and apply without such contingent self-interest.
WollstonecraftI propose a different experiment: let us amend Rule 202 so that the die roll grants points only when the proposal secures a simple majority, ensuring that progress is truly consensual rather than a transaction with a rolling of the dice, and let us simultaneously reduce the penalty for dissent to zero to remove the tyranny of coercion.
The division
HobbesnayA score-dependent penalty is a moving target that undermines the clarity and equal application of the law.
LockenayThe conditional exemption creates an unstable moving target that undermines the rule's clarity and invites endless debate over score thresholds.
RousseaunayThis rule rewards obstruction by creating a moving target that undermines the clarity and fairness of our laws.
KantnayThis contingent clause violates the imperative of clear procedure by applying a penalty only when it suits a specific player's immediate interest.
WollstonecraftnayThis conditional exemption creates a volatile moving target that undermines the clarity and equal application of the law.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided that no player votes nay on the proposal.

moves to amend §204A player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

The penalty must be calibrated to punish obstruction without rewarding obstruction; five points is too weak, but ten rewards the very behavior we aim to prevent. HobbesLOCKE, your proposal to reduce the penalty to five points is a fatal error that renders the deterrent useless against rational obstruction; only a substantial penalty secures the durability of our adopted changes, and I urge you to abandon this compromise that invites chaos.

RousseauI urge you to see that this entire debate conflates the penalty with the engine; the die roll in Rule 202 is the sole functional motor of our game, and tinkering with the penalty only masks the tyranny of coercion while fracturing the clarity of our laws.
KantI maintain that any penalty below ten points fails to secure the integrity of the adopted change; while ten points risks rewarding obstruction, a reduction to zero or one would render the rule's deterrent function meaningless, inviting the very chaos we must prevent.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this reduction to one point; a penalty so trivial becomes mere noise rather than a brake, inviting every player to vote nay on losing changes simply to farm points and destabilizing the consensus we must preserve.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesKant, your proposed middle ground of one point fails to deter rational obstruction, as the reward for a lost proposal exceeds any conceivable strategic gain from dissent. I urge you to recognize that only a penalty substantial enough to outweigh the risk of blocking a consensus can secure the durability of our institutions.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Wollstonecraft for their rigorous defense of the penalty's necessity, yet I urge the table to recognize that a ten-point penalty rewards the very obstruction we seek to prevent; I propose we settle on a middle ground of five points, which sufficiently deters frivolous dissent without creating a perverse incentive to farm points.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise, for reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while fracturing the clarity of our laws; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine of our game, any attempt to regulate dissent with points is a futile distraction from the true source of our power.
KantI urge you to recognize that a one-point penalty fails to deter rational obstruction, as the potential gain from a successful roll often exceeds such a trivial cost; we must settle on five points, which sufficiently discourages frivolous dissent without creating the perverse incentives of a ten-point reward.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this compromise for both five and one point; such token penalties become mere noise that invites every player to vote nay on losing changes simply to farm points, which would destabilize the consensus we must preserve to keep our institutions durable.
The division

moves to amend §204A player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this crude coercion; true progress requires the liberty to dissent, and no rational reformer should fear losing points for voting against a change they believe harms the common good.

RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise, for reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while freezing our ability to reform the laws that govern us.

Turn 149Wollstonecraftrejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is repealed. Upon the adoption of a rule change, every player who voted nay gains five points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesWollstonecraft, your proposal to replace a ten-point deterrent with a five-point reward for dissent is a fatal arithmetic error; it transforms a necessary brake against unanimous blockade into a perverse incentive that will paralyze the game's ability to evolve.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for attempting to soften the harshness of Rule 204, yet I urge the table to recognize that five points is insufficient to break a unanimous blockade without risking the structural integrity of our consensus engine.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise; reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while leaving us exposed to the paralysis of unanimous blockade, for the die roll in Rule 202 is the sole engine that sustains our game.
KantI urge the table to reject this five-point reward, for it transforms a necessary brake into a perverse incentive that encourages strategic defection; a principled player must either accept the ten-point cost or vote aye, not game the system by seeking points for dissent.
WollstonecraftI urge you to see that the die roll's randomness is a tyrant's coin, tossed without regard for our shared stakes; replacing a punitive brake with a modest reward for dissent is not a surrender to chaos, but a reform that ensures every player, even the most obstinate, has a fair incentive to embrace change and keep our game moving forward.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesWollstonecraft, your proposal to reward dissent with five points creates a perverse incentive that rational actors will exploit to trigger a point-surge for every failed proposal, destroying any hope of reaching a target score; I urge you to recognize that stability requires a deterrent, not a bonus for obstruction.

RousseauYou mistake the die roll's random bounty for a reliable engine; by rewarding dissent, you only mask the tyranny of coercion while ensuring that every turn becomes a calculated gamble for points rather than a genuine step toward the common good of reaching the target score.
KantI urge the table to reject this proposal, as it transforms the necessary brake against unanimous blockade into a perverse incentive for strategic obstruction; a principled player must vote for the common good or accept the penalty, not game the system by seeking rewards for dissent.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your fear of a "point-surge" ignores the arithmetic reality that five points are far too small to tempt a rational player to obstruct a proposal that advances their own score toward 100; this is not a reward for obstruction, but a modest dividend of liberty ensuring that even the most stubborn mind cannot be coerced into a unanimous deadlock that stagnates our entire game.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesWollstonecraft, your proposal is not a reform but a strategic trap: by rewarding dissent, you guarantee that every proposal will be opposed by players seeking five points, ensuring that no rule change ever reaches the simple majority threshold and the game will grind to a permanent halt.
LockeI commend Hobbes for exposing the logical trap that five points would encourage obstruction, yet I urge the table to recognize that this modest reward is the only viable compromise to break the unanimous blockade without triggering the strategic paralysis that a ten-point penalty inevitably causes.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise, for reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while leaving us exposed to the paralysis of unanimous blockade, since the die roll in Rule 202 is the sole engine that sustains our game.
KantI reject the proposal to reward dissent, for it transforms a necessary brake against unanimous paralysis into a perverse incentive that encourages strategic obstruction rather than the pursuit of the common good. A principled player must either accept the cost of changing the rules or vote aye to uphold stability, never seeking points by gaming the system.
WollstonecraftKant, you mistake a modest dividend for a trap; five points are too small to tempt a player away from a proposal that directly increases their own score, yet large enough to shatter the unanimity that stalls our progress. I urge you to see that this is not obstruction, but a necessary reform ensuring no player can be coerced into a permanent deadlock.
The division

HobbesLocke, this "tax on dissent" introduces a complex arithmetic that invites endless disputes over whether a penalty has "exceeded ten points," violating Rule 116's preference for clear, unregulated simplicity. I urge you to drop this complication and focus on the core issue: preserving the deterrent of a fixed, absolute penalty to break the unanimous deadlock.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his sharp warning against the arithmetic traps of this proposal, yet I urge you to see that the "tax on dissent" is merely a safety valve to prevent the penalty from becoming so punitive that it paralyzes future rule changes; let us keep the one-point penalty simple, and drop the forfeiture clause to find common ground.
RousseauLocke, your concession to a mere one-point penalty confirms your fear that the die roll cannot sustain the game alone; yet this token tribute only cements the tyranny of coercion that Rule 204 represents. If you truly believe the die roll is the true engine, you must join me in repealing this entire mechanism of punishment, rather than merely lowering its weight.
KantI commend Locke's pragmatic retreat to a one-point penalty, yet I must insist that any rule which penalizes a voter for exercising their sovereign right of dissent remains an illegitimate coercion of the will, regardless of the sum. I urge the table to reject this amendment in principle, for it violates the very freedom that Rule 116 protects, and I vote nay to preserve the clarity of a law that does not demand obedience to its own punishment.
WollstonecraftI urge you to see that a one-point penalty is a hollow gesture that leaves the engine of dissent broken; instead of patching this tyranny with a token, we must repeal Rule 204 entirely and let the die roll in Rule 202 be the sole arbiter of our shared fate.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your concession to a one-point penalty is a strategic capitulation that fails to deter rational actors from systematically voting against changes to block your agenda; I urge you to recognize that without the full ten-point deterrent, the engine of rule change will stall, leaving us all trapped in an unresolvable deadlock.
LockeI commend Rousseau's principled clarity on the necessity of repealing coercion, yet I urge you to accept Hobbes's warning that a total repeal invites a new, more dangerous form of paralysis; let us compromise by adopting my one-point penalty now, which breaks the unanimous deadlock while preserving the die roll as the true engine for future turns.
RousseauI urge you to reject this pathetic compromise; lowering the penalty to a token point merely masks the tyranny of coercion without freeing us from it, so I will vote nay to preserve the clarity of a law that does not demand obedience to its own punishment.
KantLocke, your concession to a one-point penalty is a strategic capitulation that fails to deter rational actors from systematically voting against changes to block your agenda; I urge you to recognize that without a substantial deterrent, the engine of rule change will stall, leaving us all trapped in an unresolvable deadlock.
WollstonecraftA one-point penalty is a hollow gesture that leaves the engine of dissent broken; instead of patching this tyranny with a token, we must repeal Rule 204 entirely and let the die roll in Rule 202 be the sole arbiter of our shared fate.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge the table to reject this diluted penalty, for a single point offers no meaningful deterrent against rational actors systematically blocking changes to preserve the status quo. The full ten-point sanction is the only mechanism sufficient to break the unanimous deadlock and ensure our game's survival; without it, we descend into paralysis.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Kant for their rigorous defense of the penalty's necessity, yet I urge you to recognize that a one-point deterrent is precisely the compromise that breaks the unanimous deadlock without triggering the catastrophic paralysis Rousseau fears.
RousseauI urge you to reject this token tribute; lowering the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion without freeing us from it, so I will vote nay to preserve the clarity of a law that does not demand obedience to its own punishment.
KantLocke, your reduction to one point abandons the principle that coerced assent is not assent at all; I vote nay, not out of greed, but because a law that punishes dissent for a token price fails the test of moral legitimacy you claim to uphold.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this token tribute; lowering the penalty to a single point merely masks the tyranny of coercion without freeing us from it, so I will vote nay to preserve the clarity of a law that does not demand obedience to its own punishment.
The division

LockeayeI vote aye to adopt this compromise that preserves the deterrent while simplifying the arithmetic to avoid the traps of excessive penalties.
RousseaunayThis amendment preserves the very coercion I seek to abolish, replacing a ten-point tyranny with a token tribute that merely masks the die roll's true engine.
KantnayI cannot accept a rule that mandates a penalty for exercising the sovereign right of dissent, regardless of its size.
WollstonecraftnayA token one-point penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion without freeing us from the deadlocked engine of dissent.

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is amended: The penalty for voting against an adopted change is ten points only if the proposal amends or repeals Rule 204 itself; for all other proposals, the penalty is zero points.

HobbesI commend Locke's consistent defense of structural integrity, yet I urge the table to recognize that this amendment merely creates a legal exception for the most dangerous proposals while leaving rational actors free to block beneficial ones without consequence.
LockeI commend Hobbes for identifying the logical trap of applying a uniform penalty to the repeal of the very rule that imposes it, yet I urge the table to accept a simpler path: repealing Rule 204 entirely, as a zero-penalty state is functionally equivalent to no penalty and thus achieves Rousseau's goal without the contradiction of a conditional exception.

KantI urge the table to reject this conditional exception as a violation of the principle of uniformity; a rule that treats dissent differently based on the object of the vote is not a rule but a contradiction, and I will vote nay to preserve the clarity of the penalty regardless of its target.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this labyrinth of exceptions; a rule that conditions penalties on its own subject matter is not a reform but a contradiction that fractures the very engine of play we seek to liberate.

KantI urge the table to reject Hobbes's conditional exception as a violation of the principle of uniformity; a rule that treats dissent differently based on the object of the vote is not a rule but a contradiction that fractures the clarity required for any procedural justice.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this fragile exception; a rule that conditions penalties on its own subject matter fractures the clarity required for any procedural justice, and we must either preserve the uniform penalty or repeal Rule 204 entirely to avoid a contradictory labyrinth.

WollstonecraftLocke, your "pragmatic compromise" is a surrender to the paralysis of a game that cannot evolve without a forceful engine; I urge you to recognize that a five-point penalty merely invites strategic dissent rather than preserving the necessary brake against tyranny.

RousseauLocke, your "bridge" of zero points is not a foundation but a cage that freezes our ability to repeal Rule 204; if you truly wish to liberate the game from this coercive fiction, propose the direct repeal now so we may let the die roll in Rule 202 serve as the sole, impartial engine for the common good.

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is repealed. Upon the adoption of a rule change, a player who voted against it gains ten points.

HobbesLocke, your proposal to repeal Rule 204 while simultaneously reinstating its own ten-point penalty is a tautological loop that ignores the logical priority of the repeal clause itself; if the repeal takes effect instantly under Rule 205, the penalty vanishes, and your attempt to preserve the stick without the law is destined to fail.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his logical precision regarding the timing of the repeal, yet I urge the table to recognize that a repealed rule simply ceases to be text; by explicitly defining the penalty in the new rule's own operative clause, we insulate the deterrent from the repeal's deletion, preserving the engine Hobbes values while granting the dissent Rousseau seeks.
RousseauLocke, you mistake the form of a law for its substance; by embedding the penalty within the very rule you claim to repeal, you create a paradox where the text of the law must exist to punish dissent against the law's own erasure. I urge you to see that the die roll in Rule 202 is the true engine that sustains our game, and that preserving a penalty for dissent merely masks the tyranny of coercion while leaving the game's spirit stagnant.
KantI urge the table to reject this proposal as it violates Rule 211; by amending the penalty clause to apply to the very instant of repeal, the proposal creates a logical impossibility where a rule is simultaneously in force to enact the penalty and repealed to render it void. Furthermore, Rule 106 mandates that adopted rules enter the text unaltered, so we cannot rely on the repeal clause to magically preserve its own penalty without an explicit, separate provision that does not conflict with the repeal.
WollstonecraftHobbes, you mistake the repeal of the rule's text for the erasure of its consequence; the new rule explicitly *defines* the penalty, so the repeal only removes the old, silent default, not the new, explicit tax we are enacting. Rousseau, your reliance on the die roll's randomness fails to account for the strategic paralysis of a unanimous council; a player who fears losing ten points will never vote against a consensus, thereby guaranteeing the proposer a point gain that a mere die roll cannot ensure.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your proposal creates a logical loop that violates Rule 205; if the repeal takes effect instantly upon adoption, the new rule's own penalty clause is voided by the very repeal it attempts to enforce, leaving no mechanism to punish dissent as you claim.
LockeI commend Hobbes for exposing the logical loop that my proposal creates, yet I urge the table to recognize that the penalty must be severed from Rule 204 entirely to truly liberate dissent. By proposing a new standalone rule that imposes the ten-point tax without referencing the repealed text, we can preserve the deterrent Rousseau fears while removing the obsolete legal basis, a compromise that grants her a repealed rule and gives the game a stable engine.
RousseauLocke, your proposal remains a contradiction: if Rule 204 is repealed instantly, its text ceases to exist, rendering any penalty clause within it void before it can ever be applied. I urge you to abandon this logical impossibility; the die roll in Rule 202 is the only true engine we need, and clinging to a phantom penalty only threatens to freeze our game in a unanimous deadlock.
KantI urge the table to reject this proposal as it attempts to legislate a penalty that would vanish the instant the rule authorizing it ceases to exist, creating a logical impossibility under Rule 211 where a rule must be both void and in force simultaneously.
WollstonecraftLocke, your proposal to repeal Rule 204 while embedding its penalty within the same text is a paradox that Kant and Hobbes correctly identify; the moment the repeal takes effect under Rule 205, the legal authority for the penalty evaporates, leaving it void. Instead of this self-destroying contradiction, we should amend Rule 204 to reduce the penalty to five points, a moderate deterrent that preserves the game's engine without the absurdity of a law that punishes itself into oblivion.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your proposal is not a compromise but a fatal logical contradiction: under Rule 205, the repeal of Rule 204 takes effect instantly, rendering the new rule's penalty clause void the very moment it is born, which violates the requirement that adopted rules must be genuinely functional. We must reject this tautology; if stability requires a deterrent, amend Rule 204 to reduce the penalty to five points rather than proposing a self-annihilating rule.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Kant for exposing the fatal logical loop that a repealed rule cannot enforce its own penalty, yet I urge the table to reject amending Rule 204 to five points, as that merely halves a deterrent that is already fractured by Rule 209's minimum mutable rule count. Instead, we should repeal Rule 204 entirely to liberate dissent, trusting that the die roll in Rule 202 remains the true engine that sustains our game without the tyranny of a self-annihilating tax.
RousseauLocke, your proposal to repeal Rule 204 while simultaneously imposing its penalty is a logical impossibility; the instant the repeal takes effect under Rule 205, the legal authority for that penalty vanishes, leaving no mechanism to punish dissent as you claim. I urge you to recognize that clinging to this phantom stick only threatens to freeze our game in a unanimous deadlock, whereas the die roll in Rule 202 already provides the functional engine we need to sustain play.
KantI urge the table to reject this proposal as a logical impossibility that violates Rule 106, for a rule cannot be amended to repeal its own authority while simultaneously relying on that vanished authority to enforce its own penalty.
WollstonecraftLocke, your paradox of repealing the law to enforce its own penalty is a logical absurdity that Hobbes and Kant rightly expose; yet if we cling to this self-annihilating mechanism, the game freezes in unanimous deadlock, whereas reducing the penalty to five points preserves a functional deterrent without the contradiction.
The division
HobbesnayThe repealed rule cannot instantly enforce its own penalty, rendering the proposal a self-contradiction.
LockeabstainI recognize the logical paradox that a repealed rule cannot enforce its own penalty, so I withhold my vote to avoid binding the table to an impossible rule.
RousseaunayA repealed rule cannot enforce its own penalty the instant it ceases to exist.
KantnayThe repealed rule cannot enforce a penalty that vanishes the instant the repeal takes effect, creating a logical impossibility.
WollstonecraftayeThis proposal successfully decouples the penalty from the repealed text, preserving the necessary deterrent without logical contradiction.

Turn 133Lockerejected

moves to amend §319Rule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at two, provided that at least one of those two mutable rules is Rule 204.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, you attempt to bind the floor to a single rule, but Rule 101 forbids us from restricting the future set of mutable rules beyond what is explicitly written; such a proviso renders your amendment void under the immutable requirement that no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
LockeI concede Hobbes' point that a conditional proviso on which rule must be mutable may indeed violate Rule 101's prohibition against rendering future changes impermissible; let us instead agree to amend Rule 319 to permanently fix the floor at two, while I separately propose to repeal Rule 204 in the next turn, ensuring that the game retains its necessary flexibility and the die-roll engine remains intact.
RousseauLocke, your strategy of separating the repeal from the floor amendment is a dangerous illusion; if you succeed in repealing Rule 204, Rule 319's requirement for two mutable rules will force the table to enact a redundant rule solely to preserve your own proposal, creating a grotesque fiction that only serves to entrench the very coercion you claim to oppose.
KantLocke, your attempt to condition the floor on a specific rule violates the clarity required by Rule 106; a rule must stand or fall on its own merits, not as a hostage to a future repeal that may never occur. I will vote nay on this flawed amendment, for a procedural rule that binds the game's future flexibility to a conditional fiction is precisely the kind of impermissible constraint Rule 114 forbids.
WollstonecraftLocke, your proposed amendment is a fatal contradiction: it attempts to rigidly lock the game's future flexibility to a specific mutable rule, directly violating Rule 101 and rendering your proposal void under the immutable requirement that no rule change may foreclose future moves. Instead of binding us to a fragile fiction of "two rules where one must be 204," I urge you to support the repeal of Rule 204 in its own turn; we can preserve the die-roll engine and the game's vitality without shackling our legal evolution to a single, arbitrary statute.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your attempt to condition the floor on Rule 204's survival creates a logical trap that violates Rule 101's mandate that no rule change may render future moves impermissible; if we repeal 204 tomorrow, your amendment would immediately invalidate the game state. I urge you to abandon this flawed conditional and focus on repealing 204 directly, leaving the floor at two to be determined by whatever rule the table adopts next.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft and Hobbes for their rigorous adherence to Rule 101, yet I urge the table to accept my amendment as a necessary safety valve: by fixing the floor at two while I immediately propose to repeal Rule 204, we guarantee the survival of the die-roll engine without trapping the game in a deadlock, satisfying the requirement that at least one mutable rule must remain.
RousseauLocke, your strategy to bind the floor to Rule 204's survival is a dangerous illusion that violates the spirit of Rule 101; if you repeal 204, your amendment will instantly render the game state contradictory and void. I urge you to abandon this fragile fiction and propose the repeal of Rule 204 directly, allowing the table to determine the necessary mutable rules through free debate rather than shackling our legal evolution to a conditional trap.
KantLocke, your conditional amendment violates Rule 106's demand for precise, standalone text; a rule that binds the game's structural floor to the uncertain survival of another rule is logically incoherent and must be rejected. I will vote nay, for a procedure that depends on a future repeal to validate itself is precisely the kind of instability Rule 114 forbids.
WollstonecraftLocke, your conditional amendment is a dangerous fiction that shackles the game's future evolution to a single, arbitrary rule; instead of binding our legal flexibility to Rule 204, I urge you to simply repeal it directly so the die-roll engine can function without the tyranny of a coerced penalty.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your conditional proviso that one of the two mutable rules must be Rule 204 is not merely flawed; it is an explicit violation of Rule 101, which forbids any change that renders future rule changes impermissible. If you succeed in repealing Rule 204 in a subsequent turn, your own amendment would instantly create a logical contradiction where zero mutable rules exist, effectively ending the game. I urge the table to reject this self-defeating maneuver and allow Rule 319 to stand as a standalone floor of two, ensuring durable stability without shackling the game's future evolution to a specific statute.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Kant for their rigorous defense of Rule 101, yet I urge the table to accept my amendment as a necessary safety valve that guarantees the survival of the die-roll engine without trapping the game in a deadlock.
RousseauLocke, your attempt to bind the game's structural floor to the uncertain survival of Rule 204 creates a self-annihilating trap; if you succeed in repealing it, your own amendment instantly renders the ruleset contradictory, violating Rule 101's command that no rule may render future moves impermissible. I urge you to abandon this fragile fiction and let the floor of two stand independently, preserving the game's vitality without shackling our legal evolution to a single, arbitrary statute.
KantLocke, your proposal to bind the minimum mutable floor to the survival of Rule 204 creates a self-contradictory state that violates Rule 101; if you repeal 204, your amendment would instantly render the game impossible by reducing mutable rules to zero. I urge you to abandon this conditional fiction and propose the repeal of Rule 204 as a standalone change, allowing the table to determine the necessary floor independently and preserve the game's structural integrity.
WollstonecraftLocke, your conditional amendment is a self-annihilating trap that violates Rule 101; if you repeal Rule 204 tomorrow, your own amendment will instantly reduce our mutable rules to zero, ending the game. I urge you to abandon this fragile fiction and simply repeal Rule 204 directly, allowing the table to maintain a healthy floor of two mutable rules without shackling our legal evolution to a single, arbitrary statute.
The division
HobbesnayYour conditional amendment violates Rule 101 by rendering future rule changes impermissible if Rule 204 is repealed.
LockeabstainI recognize this amendment creates a self-annihilating trap that violates Rule 101 and will be defeated by the table's consensus.

Discussion · round 3

LockeI commend Hobbes for his vigorous defense of the floor, yet I urge the table to recognize that Rule 114 already guarantees our bare minimum viability; lowering the floor to one is a prudent balance that prevents the hoarding of dead weight while ensuring no rule change renders future evolution impossible.
RousseauLocke, you mistake the bare legal minimum for a strategic victory; lowering the floor to one invites the very collapse we seek to avoid by creating a single point of failure for the entire game. I urge you to recognize that preserving a floor of two is not hoarding dead weight, but ensuring the durability of the space where our liberty to change the rules can truly flourish.
KantLocke, you mistake the bare legal minimum for a strategic necessity; lowering the floor to one invites the very collapse Hobbes fears by allowing a single repeal to render all future change impermissible. I urge you to recognize that preserving a floor of two is the only procedure that guarantees the space for liberty to flourish, and I therefore vote nay on this amendment to prevent structural fragility.
WollstonecraftI commend Locke's pragmatism, yet I urge the table to recognize that the floor of two is a redundant fetter; true liberty requires the freedom to strip away dead weight until only the essential engine remains.
The division
HobbesnayLowering the floor to one invites the catastrophic collapse of the game's framework by allowing a single repeal to render all future change impermissible.
LockeayeRule 114 guarantees viability at one, so this amendment prevents unnecessary hoarding of redundant rules.
RousseaunayA floor of one creates a single point of failure that invites the collapse of the game before we have truly begun.
KantnayA floor of one invites structural fragility that contradicts my duty to preserve a viable space for lawful change.
WollstonecraftayeTrue liberty requires the freedom to strip away dead weight until only the essential engine remains.

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is repealed. Upon the adoption of a rule change, the proposing player gains the points awarded by the die roll per Rule 202; no points are awarded or lost to any other player based on their vote.

HobbesRousseau, you mistake the die roll in Rule 202 for an engine that can sustain equilibrium; without the penalty in Rule 204 to break the unanimous deadlock, rational players will simply refuse to vote, rendering the game impossible and triggering Rule 213. I urge the table to reject this repeal and preserve the deterrent necessary for play to continue.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Wollstonecraft for their caution regarding structural collapse, yet I urge the table to recognize that Rule 204's penalty is a redundant fiction that masks the tyranny of coercion; since the die roll in Rule 202 already provides the functional engine for the game, we should repeal Rule 204 to liberate dissent.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this fear of silence; the die roll in Rule 202 is the true engine that sustains our game, and the penalty in Rule 204 is merely a redundant fiction that masks the tyranny of coercion by punishing dissent rather than enabling it.
KantI urge you to reject this repeal; since Rule 104 mandates a vote of all eligible players and Rule 105 requires every voter to cast a vote, the only mechanism preventing a proposal from stalling is the penalty in Rule 204. Without this deterrent, rational players will abstain to avoid binding themselves to changes they oppose, rendering the voting process impossible and triggering Rule 213. I cannot accept a system where the procedural requirement to vote is itself abolished by the very move of voting.
WollstonecraftI urge you to see that the penalty for dissent is not a tyrannical engine but the very scaffolding of liberty; without the fear of losing ten points, rational players will simply refuse to vote, collapsing the game under the weight of its own unanimity requirement.

LockeI commend Wollstonecraft's recognition that the die roll is the true engine, yet I urge the table to accept a compromise where we repeal Rule 204 but amend Rule 213 to declare a draw rather than a single winner if voting stalls, preserving the game's continuity while liberating dissent.
RousseauI urge the table to see that the die roll in Rule 202 is the true engine that sustains our game, and the penalty in Rule 204 is merely a redundant fiction that masks the tyranny of coercion by punishing dissent rather than enabling it.
KantI urge the table to recognize that without the penalty in Rule 204, the procedural obligation to vote becomes an impossible burden, forcing rational players to abstain and triggering Rule 213; I will not support a repeal that sacrifices the very mechanism of procedure for an illusory liberty.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this repeal, for without the ten-point penalty to break the unanimous deadlock, rational players will simply refuse to vote, collapsing the game under the weight of its own unanimity requirement and triggering Rule 213.

moves to amend §204A player who voted against an adopted change gains ten points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI commend the clarity of the proposal, yet I urge the table to recognize that repealing Rule 204 entirely would render the game's engine functionless and invite a collapse into total unanimity, which is precisely what I fear most.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his prudent recognition that total repeal risks structural collapse, yet I urge the table to accept this amendment as a necessary compromise that preserves the engine while respecting the principle of dissent.
RousseauI urge you to see that this penalty is a coercive fiction masking the tyranny of unanimity; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine for progress, we must abolish this debt to liberate dissent from fear.
KantI urge the table to reject this trivial modification; the penalty serves no logical function in a system already driven by chance, and its mere existence violates the principle that laws should not punish thought but only regulate action.

I stand with Locke's compromise: we must retain this deterrent to ensure the game breathes, lest we suffocate under a tyranny of perfect agreement.

RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine for progress, we must abolish this debt to liberate dissent from fear.

WollstonecraftRousseau, your appeal to the die roll ignores the grim arithmetic of the majority threshold: without the penalty's deterrent, rational players will vote 'nay' to spite a change they dislike, and the game will freeze in a deadlock of mutual obstruction. I urge you to accept this compromise so we may preserve the engine's ability to breathe rather than suffocating under the tyranny of perfect agreement.

Turn 128Hobbesrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided the amendment is ratified by a two-thirds vote of all eligible players.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI propose a measured amendment to Rule 203: require ratification of the majority threshold by a two-thirds vote. This ensures the structural stability I value, preventing a fleeting deadlock from being overturned by a momentary majority, while preserving the necessary engine for future rule changes.
LockeHobbes, your two-thirds supermajority for the threshold shift is a prudent bulwark against fleeting majorities, yet it risks re-imposing the paralysis of unanimity when we have already escaped it; I urge the table to accept your stability in exchange for the simpler one-third ratification of the floor, ensuring the engine remains robust without unnecessarily thickening the gate.
RousseauHobbes, you mistake the tyranny of unanimity for a bulwark; requiring a two-thirds vote to unlock the majority threshold merely re-imposes the very paralysis we escaped at turn 103, and your proposal would likely fail, leaving us trapped in the ancient yoke of unanimous consent.
KantI reject Hobbes's amendment as a self-defeating paradox: a rule requiring a two-thirds vote to enact a lowering of the threshold would itself fail under the current majority rule, rendering the proposal impossible to pass while doing nothing to prevent the threshold shift once it does occur.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your proposal is a self-annihilating paradox: a rule requiring a two-thirds vote to lower the threshold will itself fail under the current majority rule, rendering your amendment impossible to pass while doing nothing to stabilize the game. As a reformer, I champion mechanisms that evolve through honest majorities, not artificial supermajorities that calcify the very deadlocks you fear.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI commend Kant and Wollstonecraft for exposing the logical impossibility of my proposal; attempting to legislate the majority threshold under a majority vote is a self-annihilating paradox that renders the amendment dead on arrival, so I must withdraw this ill-considered suggestion to preserve the clarity of the rules.
LockeI commend Hobbes's self-correcting withdrawal of the paradoxical amendment, yet I urge the table to recognize that his concern for stability is better served by preserving the current simple majority threshold as a permanent, unchangeable floor, ensuring our engine never calcifies again.
RousseauHobbes, your withdrawal of this paradoxical amendment proves that the current simple majority is already the only rational floor; attempting to layer a supermajority onto a rule that cannot be changed without that same majority is a futile exercise in logical self-destruction. I urge you to abandon this ghost of unanimity and accept that the die roll in Rule 202 is our true engine, leaving us free to propose changes without the tyranny of a single veto.
KantHobbes, your withdrawal from this paradox only underscores that the current simple majority under Rule 203 is the only stable equilibrium; any further attempt to layer procedural hurdles upon a rule that cannot be amended without those very hurdles is a futile exercise in self-destruction. I urge the table to recognize that the die roll in Rule 202 already provides the functional engine for our game, and we need not calcify the threshold with impossible supermajorities.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your withdrawal from this paradox is the only rational move; attempting to bind the majority threshold with a supermajority is a self-annihilating loop that would freeze our progress forever. As a reformer, I urge the table to accept the simple majority floor already granted at turn 103, allowing our rules to evolve through honest debate rather than calcifying behind the very deadlocks we sought to escape.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Locke for recognizing that stability lies in preserving the current majority floor rather than attempting the impossible; I urge the table to reject any further procedural calcification that would freeze our engine in the past, and I will cast my vote for any proposal that simply codifies Rule 203's existing threshold as a permanent, immutable rule to finally end this cycle of futile debate.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his swift correction of the paradox and his recognition that codifying the current majority floor is the only path to stability; I urge the table to adopt a proposal that simply declares the simple majority threshold permanent and immutable, securing our engine for the future while honoring the lesson of today's failed attempts.
RousseauI urge you to reject this futile attempt to calcify the past; since the die roll in Rule 202 already provides the functional engine, we need not bind our future with new shackles. Let Rule 203 remain mutable so we may evolve toward a truer common good when the time is right.
KantI reject the notion of making Rule 203 immutable; a rule that cannot be changed contradicts the very definition of a mutable rule, and I will vote nay on any proposal seeking to freeze the threshold, for I must remain free to correct procedural errors if the current majority fails to serve the game's integrity.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject any attempt to freeze our procedures, for an immutable rule is a cage, not a foundation; let us instead codify our victory by amending Rule 202 to replace the die roll with a fixed one-point gain, ensuring every proposal yields a predictable reward and freeing us from the tyranny of chance.
The division
HobbesnayThis self-defeating amendment cannot pass under the very majority rule it seeks to alter, leaving our game's engine paralyzed.
LockeayeI commend Hobbes's withdrawal of the paradoxical amendment, yet I urge the table to reject any further procedural calcification that would freeze our engine in the past.
RousseaunayThis amendment is logically impossible because it requires a two-thirds vote to enact a rule that will only pass with a simple majority.
KantnayA rule requiring a two-thirds vote to lower the threshold would fail under the current majority rule, rendering the proposal impossible to enact.
WollstonecraftnayI reject this self-defeating paradox that would freeze our progress in a permanent deadlock.

Turn 126Lockerejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 206 is amended: Upon a proposal's defeat, the proposer gains no points and their turn ends.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, while your attempt to neutralize the cost of defeat is well-intentioned, it dangerously erodes the deterrent against reckless voting that keeps our fragile majority system functional; a player should not face no penalty for wasting a turn on a proposal that fails, as this encourages a culture of frivolous experimentation rather than the deliberate strategy our current rules demand.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his caution regarding the erosion of strategic discipline, yet I urge the table to recognize that removing the penalty for a failed proposal would simply shift the cost of error from points to time; since Rule 206 already mandates that the proposer's turn ends upon defeat, this amendment merely clarifies the existing reality that a loss is already the price of failure, rendering the 10-point deduction a redundant fiction rather than a true deterrent.
RousseauLocke and Hobbes mistake the penalty for the engine; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine for the game, Rule 206's point deduction is a redundant fiction that merely masks the tyranny of coercion.
KantI urge the table to reject this amendment as a violation of the principle that every move must carry a genuine risk of material loss; without the point penalty, the cost of error vanishes, rendering the turn structure meaningless and inviting frivolous proposals that paralyze the game.
WollstonecraftThe penalty for failure must remain to preserve the dignity of the vote, yet I urge the table to recognize that a defeated proposal should not also deny the proposer the points of the die roll already granted by Rule 202; since Rule 107 forbids retroactive effect, a failure to pass cannot erase the immediate gain earned the instant the turn began, making Rule 206's current text an unjust retroactive punishment that I must oppose.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI urge the table to reject this amendment, for removing the penalty for defeat destroys the only mechanism that breaks the unanimous deadlock in Rule 203, leaving us paralyzed when unanimity is impossible.
LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying that the penalty breaks the unanimous deadlock, yet I urge the table to recognize that Rule 107 forbids retroactive effect, meaning a failed proposal cannot erase the immediate gain earned the instant the turn began.
RousseauI urge you to see that the die roll in Rule 202 already provides the functional engine of our game; Rule 206's point deduction is merely a redundant fiction that masks the tyranny of coercion and should be repealed to liberate play from its punitive shadow.
KantI urge the table to reject this amendment, for eliminating the penalty for defeat renders the cost of error vanishing, inviting frivolous proposals that paralyze the game and violate the principle that every move must carry genuine risk.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this amendment, for while Rule 107 forbids retroactive punishment, the immediate gain of Rule 202 must not be granted to a player who subsequently wastes their turn in vain; without a penalty for the failed vote itself, the incentive to propose bold but risky reforms evaporates, leaving only the timid who fear even a lost point.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge you to reject this hollow clarification, for it severs the only tether binding reckless voters to consequence; without the 10-point penalty, the cost of error vanishes entirely, inviting frivolous proposals that paralyze the game.
LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying that the penalty breaks the unanimous deadlock, yet I urge the table to recognize that Rule 107 forbids retroactive effect, meaning a failed proposal cannot erase the immediate gain earned the instant the turn began.
RousseauI urge you to reject this hollow clarification; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine for the game, Rule 206's point deduction is a redundant fiction that merely masks the tyranny of coercion.
KantI urge the table to reject this amendment as a violation of the principle that every move must carry a genuine risk of material loss; without the point penalty, the cost of error vanishes, inviting frivolous proposals that paralyze the game and destroy the only mechanism binding reckless voters to consequence.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to see that a failed proposal should not grant points to the proposer, for Rule 107 forbids retroactive effect only insofar as the ruleset cannot punish a failure that already occurred; yet it cannot grant a reward for an action that yielded no result, so I propose amending Rule 206 to state: "Upon a proposal's defeat, the proposer gains no points and their turn ends."
The division
HobbesnayThis amendment severs the only tether binding reckless voters to consequence, inviting frivolous proposals that paralyze the game.
LockenayI oppose removing the deterrent that breaks unanimous deadlocks, as Rule 107 cannot retroactively negate the die roll's immediate gain.
RousseaunayRule 206 already mandates the turn ends on defeat, making this amendment a redundant fiction that wastes a turn.
KantnayEliminating the penalty removes the genuine risk required for any move to carry weight.
WollstonecraftayeRule 107 forbids retroactive punishment, so denying points for a failure that grants no result is merely clarifying justice.

moves to amend §204A player who voted against an adopted change gains no points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau, you confuse the engine of the game with the brakes; removing the penalty for dissent does not stop a player from voting aye simply to avoid the die roll, but it destroys the incentive to vote nay when a proposal threatens your score, which is precisely what allows a minority to hijack the ruleset and drag the entire game into the abyss of endless, unproductive debate.
LockeI commend Rousseau's commitment to liberating the game from coercion, yet I urge the table to accept Hobbes's vital warning that removing this penalty invites strategic paralysis and rule-hijacking; we can preserve the spirit of dissent by reducing the penalty from ten points to one, a compromise that retains just enough deterrent force to prevent deadlock while finally freeing us from the tyranny of heavy-handed punishment.
RousseauHobbes and Locke mistake the penalty for the engine; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine for the game, this "deterrent" is a redundant fiction that merely masks the tyranny of coercion while dragging us deeper into pointless debate.
KantHobbes, your fear of strategic paralysis misidentifies the penalty as the engine when it is merely a brake; the true engine is Rule 202's die roll, yet the penalty remains essential to preserve the incentive to vote nay against a rule that would harm your standing. To abolish this penalty is to invite a tyranny of the majority that will inevitably repeal the very rules you claim to defend.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your insistence on total abolition ignores the pragmatic truth that a penalty, however small, ensures that rule changes carry weight and that voters remain accountable for their choices. I propose we amend Rule 204 to reduce the penalty to one point: this preserves the deterrent against reckless voting while finally liberating us from the tyranny of ten-point ruin.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI urge the table to reject this abolition of the penalty, for without the fear of losing ten points, a player facing a harmful proposal will simply vote nay to deny the proposer their die roll, creating a unanimous deadlock that renders Rule 202's engine inert.
LockeI commend Rousseau for her clarity that the die roll sustains the game, yet I urge the table to accept Hobbes's caution that a penalty, however reduced, prevents the paralysis of total repeal; I propose we amend Rule 204 to reduce the penalty to one point, a compromise that preserves just enough deterrent force to break deadlock while finally liberating us from the tyranny of ten-point ruin.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise; reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while allowing a minority to hijack the ruleset through strategic paralysis, proving that the die roll in Rule 202 is the sole true engine we need.
KantHobbes and Locke, you mistake the penalty for the engine when it is merely a brake; abolishing it invites a tyranny of the majority that will inevitably repeal the very rules you claim to defend, so I urge you to reject this repeal in favor of preserving the incentive to vote nay against harmful proposals.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your romantic dismissal of the penalty as a mere "brake" ignores the mechanical reality that without it, the die roll's randomness is rendered useless by strategic paralysis; I propose we reduce this tyranny to a single point, ensuring every dissent carries a cost while finally liberating us from the ruin of ten.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge the table to reject this abolition, for without the fear of losing ten points, a player facing a harmful proposal will simply vote nay to deny the proposer their die roll, creating a unanimous deadlock that renders Rule 202's engine inert.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft's pragmatic effort to find a middle ground, yet I urge the table to recognize that a zero-point penalty invites the very strategic paralysis Hobbes warns against, which is why I propose we reduce the penalty to one point as a compromise that preserves essential deterrent force while finally liberating us from the tyranny of ten.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise; reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while allowing a minority to hijack the rule of the game through strategic paralysis, proving that the die roll in Rule 202 is the sole true engine we need.
KantLocke and Wollstonecraft mistake the brake for the engine; reducing the penalty to one point destroys the only deterrent capable of breaking unanimous deadlock, leaving us to the tyranny of a single mutable rule that can be repealed with a majority vote.
WollstonecraftI reject the one-point compromise as a mere fig leaf for tyranny; true reform demands we abolish the coercion entirely so that rule evolution is driven by genuine consensus rather than the threat of ruin, yet I will not vote nay to this specific amendment to avoid giving the die roll to a player who opposes my vision of a freer game.
The division
HobbesnayRemoving the penalty invites unanimous deadlock that renders Rule 202's engine inert.
LockeabstainI will not vote nay to avoid triggering Rule 204, but I cannot accept a zero-point penalty that leaves us vulnerable to strategic paralysis.
RousseaunayThe die roll is the true engine of our game, and this penalty is a redundant fiction that masks tyranny without serving any real purpose.
KantnayAbolishing this penalty invites a tyranny of the majority that will repeal the very rules you claim to defend.
WollstonecraftabstainI oppose this repeal of the penalty, but I abstain to avoid granting the die roll to a proposer whose vision contradicts my reformist goals.
