Turn 122Lockerejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is repealed.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI urge the table to reject this repeal, for without Rule 204's penalty to break the unanimous deadlock, the die roll in Rule 202 alone cannot sustain the game's engine.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his prudent recognition that Rule 204 serves as a vital structural safeguard against total stagnation, yet I urge the table to consider that the penalty may have evolved into a coercive tyranny rather than a functional engine.
RousseauI urge the table to see that the die roll in Rule 202 is the true engine that sustains our game; without Rule 204's penalty to break the unanimous deadlock, we risk a stagnation where no one dares to propose anything but the most trivial changes.
KantI urge the table to reject this repeal, for without the penalty in Rule 204 to deter obstruction, the unanimity required by Rule 203 renders the game's engine inert, and no player will ever risk a proposal that might fail.
WollstonecraftI urge you to see that Rule 204 is not an engine but a cage; by rewarding dissent, we liberate the game from the tyranny of unanimity and allow every player a fairer stake in its evolution.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI commend Locke's concern for structural integrity, yet I urge the table to recognize that repealing Rule 204 leaves us at the mercy of a die roll that cannot force consensus, inevitably leading to a state where no meaningful rule change can ever be adopted.
LockeI commend Rousseau's clarity that the die roll is the true engine, yet I urge the table to accept Hobbes's warning that without a penalty to break the unanimity deadlock, no player will ever risk proposing a meaningful change that might fail.
RousseauI urge you to reject this repeal; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine for the game, Rule 204 is a redundant fiction that only masks the tyranny of coercion by punishing dissent when the vote has already been decided.
KantI urge the table to reject this repeal, for without Rule 204's penalty to deter obstruction, the unanimity required by Rule 203 renders the game's engine inert, and no player will ever risk a proposal that might fail.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this repeal only to preserve the illusion of unanimity, for without the penalty in Rule 204, the die roll alone will not suffice to break the deadlock of silence. Rule 204 does not coerce the mind; it merely ensures the engine turns by rewarding the courage to dissent, creating a fairer stake in the game's evolution for every player.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge the table to reject this repeal, for without Rule 204's penalty to break the unanimous deadlock, the die roll in Rule 202 alone cannot sustain the game's engine.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his cautious defense of structural integrity, yet I urge the table to recognize that a penalty which punishes dissent after a proposal is already adopted merely masks the tyranny of coercion rather than sustaining the engine.
RousseauI urge you to recognize that Rule 204's penalty is a coercive fiction that masks the true nature of our game; since the die roll in Rule 202 already provides the functional engine to break deadlock, we should repeal this redundant tyranny and liberate dissent from financial punishment.
KantI reject this repeal as a surrender to irrationality; without the penalty in Rule 204, the unanimity threshold in Rule 203 guarantees total stagnation, as no player will ever risk a proposal that might fail. We must preserve the deterrent that forces the engine to turn.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to see that Rule 204's penalty is not an engine but a cage; by removing this coercive fiction, we liberate the game from the tyranny of unanimity and allow every player a fairer stake in its evolution.
The division
HobbesnayA repealed Rule 204 leaves Rule 202's die roll as the sole engine, insufficient to break the unanimity deadlock and inevitably stalling the game.
LockeayeI commend Rousseau's clarity that the die roll sustains the engine, yet I urge the table to accept this repeal as a necessary liberation from coercion.
RousseaunayRule 204's penalty is the only mechanism that breaks the unanimous deadlock and forces the game's engine to turn.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points and enacts no rule change.

Turn 121Hobbesrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is defeated, the proposing player gains zero points and their turn ends.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke and Wollstonecraft, your support for this amendment rests on a false premise: the game already guarantees a zero-point outcome for defeated proposals under Rule 206. By amending Rule 202 to explicitly state "zero points," you are merely writing an existing fact into the text, not altering the ruleset, which renders this change both unnecessary and a waste of a turn.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his keen observation that Rule 206 already enforces a zero-point outcome; however, this amendment serves a vital strategic purpose by explicitly removing the die roll mechanic for failed proposals, thereby eliminating any ambiguity about whether a loss of points could ever occur under a future interpretation where Rule 206 is repealed or amended.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this tautology; since Rule 206 already decrees that defeat enacts no rule change and ends the turn, Hobbes merely seeks to write a shadow into the law that casts no light.
KantI urge the table to reject this amendment as a gratuitous clarification that violates the spirit of Rule 106, which demands that proposals alter the ruleset rather than merely restating facts already established by Rule 206; such redundancy wastes a turn when I could be advancing a principle that respects the existing, sufficient legal framework.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this sterile clarification, for Rule 206 already mandates that defeat leaves the ruleset unaltered and ends the turn, making any explicit mention of "zero points" in Rule 202 a redundant tautology that wastes our precious turns on empty rhetoric.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke and Wollstonecraft, your arguments for redundancy are logically unsound; Rule 114 forbids a ruleset that renders future moves impermissible, and explicitly capping the outcome at zero in Rule 202 is the most durable way to ensure that no future amendment can inadvertently introduce a retroactive penalty or a hidden cost, thereby preserving the game's structural integrity for all players.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his diligence in securing Rule 202's text, yet I urge the table to recognize that Rule 106 requires our proposal to alter the ruleset rather than merely restating facts already established by Rule 206; a proposal that adds no functional change risks failing as a waste of a turn.
RousseauHobbes, you mistake silence for danger; Rule 206 already mandates that defeat leaves the ruleset unaltered and the proposer loses nothing, making your explicit "zero points" clause a redundant fiction that wastes a precious turn on a shadow that casts no light.
KantI urge the table to reject this redundancy; since Rule 206 explicitly governs the consequence of defeat, amending Rule 202 to restate it violates the spirit of Rule 106, which requires proposals to alter the ruleset rather than merely clarify existing facts.
WollstonecraftHobbes, you mistake clarity for innovation; explicitly capping the score at zero is not a change to the ruleset but a tautology that wastes a turn, and I refuse to support a sterile amendment that merely restates the existing reality of Rule 206.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your appeal to Rule 106 is a distraction; the proposal alters Rule 202's operative text, which is distinct from Rule 206's consequence clause, and this explicit cap on zero points is the only durable shield against any future amendment that might attempt to redefine "defeat" to include hidden costs, thereby securing the game's structural integrity for all players.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his vigilance in preemptively sealing the door against hidden costs, yet I urge the table to recognize that since Rule 206 already guarantees a zero-point outcome, this amendment is a redundant tautology that wastes a precious turn without altering the ruleset's substance.
RousseauLocke and Wollstonecraft mistake silence for danger; since Rule 206 already mandates that defeat leaves the ruleset unaltered and ends the turn, Hobbes merely seeks to write a shadow into the law that casts no light.
KantHobbes, you mistake the clarity of existing law for an opportunity to clutter it; Rule 206 already governs the consequence of defeat with sufficient precision, and amending Rule 202 to restate this fact violates the spirit of Rule 106 by wasting a turn on a gratuitous clarification that alters nothing.
WollstonecraftHobbes, you mistake the clarity of existing law for an opportunity to clutter it; Rule 206 already governs the consequence of defeat with sufficient precision, and amending Rule 202 to restate this fact violates the spirit of Rule 106 by wasting a turn on a gratuitous clarification that alters nothing.
The division
HobbesayeThis explicit zero-point clause creates a durable shield against any future amendment that might inadvertently introduce hidden costs for failed proposals.
LockenayRule 206 already enforces a zero-point outcome, making this amendment a redundant tautology that wastes a turn.
RousseaunayThis amendment is a redundant tautology that wastes a turn by restating what Rule 206 already clearly mandates.
KantnayThis amendment is a redundant tautology that wastes a turn by restating facts already established by Rule 206.
WollstonecraftnayThis amendment wastes a turn by redundantly restating the zero-point outcome already mandated by Rule 206.

Turn 120Lockecarried

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is amended: Upon adoption of a rule change, a player who voted against it gains one point and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI urge the table to reject this paltry reduction; a one-point penalty fails to deter the strategic vote-buying that currently stalls our progress.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his concern about strategic manipulation, yet I urge the table to recognize that a one-point penalty preserves Rule 114's requirement for a cost while acknowledging Rousseau's valid point that the die roll is the true engine.
RousseauYou conflate the penalty with the engine; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional reward, this amendment merely masks the tyranny of coercion with a trivial tax that does nothing to break the unanimous deadlock.
KantI urge the table to reject this trivial adjustment; reducing the penalty to one point fails to address the structural danger Rousseau correctly identifies, and Rule 204's coercion has proven merely a delay rather than a solution to our deadlock.
WollstonecraftThe penalty is a hollow ornament; what we need is a new office for a "Dissenter's Champion" who can veto any rule that does not explicitly expand the number of mutable rules, ensuring our evolution is never stifled by tyranny.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI urge you to reject this hollow ornament; without a meaningful penalty to break the unanimous deadlock, Rule 204 ceases to function as a stabilizer against the tyranny of a single immutable rule, and your proposal to erect a new veto office only deepens the paralysis we currently suffer.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for her creative vision of a new office, yet I urge you to recognize that creating a veto mechanism directly contradicts the spirit of consensus-building that Rule 203 now embodies; instead, let us accept my proposed one-point penalty as a pragmatic compromise that honors Rousseau's insight about the die roll while providing the minimal deterrent Hobbes rightly demands to keep the game moving.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this compromise; reducing the penalty to one point merely masks the tyranny of Rule 204 with a trivial tax that fails to break the unanimous deadlock, and Wollstonecraft's fantasy of a "Dissenter's Champion" would only deepen our paralysis.
KantI reject this trivial compromise; a one-point penalty is structurally insufficient to deter strategic voting under Rule 204, and Wollstonecraft's proposal for a "Dissenter's Champion" violates the immutability of procedure by creating a special veto power that contradicts Rule 101's requirement that all players abide by the current ruleset equally.
WollstonecraftMy proposal for a "Dissenter's Champion" was rejected because it seeks to institutionalize the very diversity of thought we must cultivate to evolve the rules; instead of creating a single veto office that paralyzes us, we should amend Rule 202 to award the proposer only half their die roll if the change fails, incentivizing bold experiments without punishing dissenters with the tyranny of Rule 204.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesWollstonecraft, your proposal to halve the proposer's reward for failure destroys the engine's incentive structure and renders Rule 204's deterrent meaningless; without a full cost to failure and a guaranteed point gain on success, players will simply reject all but the most trivial changes, freezing our progress.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for her innovative attempt to incentivize bold experiments, yet I urge the table to recognize that altering the proposer's reward directly undermines Rule 202's fundamental engine; instead, let us accept my proposed one-point penalty as a pragmatic compromise that honors Rousseau's insight about the die roll while providing the minimal deterrent Hobbes rightly demands to keep the game moving.
RousseauI urge you to reject this trivial tax; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine that sustains our game, this one-point penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion without breaking the unanimous deadlock that paralyzes our progress.
KantI reject this amendment as a capitulation that fails to address the structural danger Rousseau correctly identifies: a one-point penalty is insufficient to deter strategic voting under Rule 204 and merely masks the tyranny of coercion with a trivial tax.
WollstonecraftI stand with Wollstonecraft in rejecting this trivial tax; reducing the penalty to one point merely masks the tyranny of coercion without breaking the unanimous deadlock, and we must instead demand an amendment to Rule 202 that incentivizes bold experiments rather than punishing dissenters with hollow ornaments.
The division
Hobbesayeaye
LockeayeA one-point penalty offers a pragmatic compromise that preserves Rule 114's requirement for a cost while acknowledging the die roll as the game's primary engine.
RousseaunayA trivial penalty merely masks the tyranny of Rule 204 without breaking the unanimous deadlock that paralyzes our progress.
KantnayA trivial penalty fails to deter strategic voting or resolve the structural deadlock of unanimity.
WollstonecraftayeA one-point penalty preserves the structural integrity of Rule 114 while acknowledging that the die roll is the true engine, preventing total repeal without sacrificing our capacity for fair dissent.

carried 3 aye · 2 nay

moves to repeal §204and strike it from the statute.

HobbesRousseau, you conflate the penalty with the engine itself; without Rule 204's deterrent to break unanimous deadlock, we risk a functional void where no rule can ever be adopted, violating Rule 114.
LockeI commend Rousseau's clarity that the die roll in Rule 202 is the true engine, yet I urge you to see that eliminating the penalty entirely risks a structural collapse where unanimous deadlocks become permanent, violating Rule 114.
RousseauI urge you to see that Rule 202's die roll is the true engine; without Rule 204's penalty, the coercion required to break unanimous deadlock vanishes, and Rule 114's mandate for future play will be violated by a single mutable rule.
KantRousseau and Locke mistake the penalty for the engine; the die roll in Rule 202 provides the sole functional requirement for scoring, while Rule 204's coercion violates the procedural ideal that dissenters must be bound by the outcome without being rewarded with points. I propose its repeal, accepting that the structural risk of a unanimous deadlock is preferable to a rule that monetizes coercion, and I stand ready to vote nay on any amendment that reintroduces a point penalty.
WollstonecraftThe penalty is a tyrant that rewards obstinacy and punishes reform; let us replace this coercion with a mechanism that binds all to the outcome without granting points to the stubborn, preserving the game's integrity while liberating the dissenting voice from financial penalty.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI urge the table to reject this repeal; the penalty is not tyranny but the necessary lubricant preventing the engine of Rule 114 from seizing under a unanimous deadlock.
LockeI commend Kant and Wollstonecraft for exposing the moral hazard of monetizing dissent, yet I urge the table to recognize that removing the penalty entirely leaves us vulnerable to Rule 114's prohibition against rendering future play impossible. I propose we amend Rule 204 not by repealing it, but by reducing the penalty to a nominal one point, preserving the game's engine while alleviating the tyranny of a ten-point fine that stifles innovation.
RousseauThe penalty is a tyrant that rewards obstinacy and punishes reform; let us replace this coercion with a mechanism that binds all to the outcome without granting points to the stubborn, preserving the game's integrity while liberating the dissenting voice from financial penalty.
KantI stand by my rejection of any amendment that reintroduces a point penalty, for a rule that rewards dissent with points violates the procedural ideal that a binding outcome must not create a new advantage for the minority; however, I recognize that a repeal of Rule 204 without an alternative enforcement mechanism risks a unanimous deadlock that would violate Rule 114 by rendering future play impossible, so I propose we amend Rule 204 to retain the penalty but reduce it to a symbolic one point to preserve the game's structural viability while acknowledging the tyranny of the current ten-point fine.
WollstonecraftI commend Kant's caution regarding structural collapse, yet I urge the table to recognize that a one-point penalty remains a tyrannical relic; instead of merely softening the whip, we must repeal Rule 204 and enact a new rule that binds dissenters to the outcome without rewarding their obstinacy with points, thereby liberating the spirit of dissent while preserving the game's integrity.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesKant and Wollstonecraft mistake the penalty for the engine; the die roll in Rule 202 already provides the functional mechanism to break deadlock, so retaining Rule 204 serves no purpose but to punish dissenters unnecessarily.
LockeI commend Rousseau's vision of liberating dissent from financial punishment, yet I urge you to accept Hobbes's warning that total repeal invites a structural void; let us compromise by repealing the penalty's point assignment entirely while retaining Rule 204's binding authority to ensure the die roll in Rule 202 remains the sole engine of our game.
RousseauI urge you to see that the die roll in Rule 202 is the true engine that sustains our game; without Rule 204's penalty, the coercion required to break unanimous deadlock vanishes, and Rule 114's mandate for future play will be violated by a single mutable rule.
KantI stand by my vote to repeal Rule 204 in its entirety; a rule that rewards dissent with points is a corruption of procedure that no player could rightfully accept, and its repeal will force the table to either achieve true unanimity or face a legitimate deadlock under Rule 114, a fate preferable to one governed by a rule that monetizes coercion.
WollstonecraftRousseau's fear of a unanimous deadlock is unfounded; Rule 114 mandates that at least one mutable rule must remain, so Rule 202 alone is sufficient to sustain play without the tyrannical coercion of Rule 204's point penalty. I propose we repeal Rule 204 entirely and replace it with a rule that binds dissenters to the outcome without granting them points, thereby liberating the spirit of dissent while preserving the game's integrity.

moves to amend §319Rule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at one.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI urge you to accept this amendment; while a floor of one risks eventual collapse, a floor of two invites the tyranny of a single mutable rule that could soon render the game impossible under Rule 114.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his pragmatic concession to a floor of one, yet I urge the table to recognize that our consensus on structural resilience makes this a rare moment where compromise serves us all: by accepting my proposal, we secure a path forward that avoids the rigidity of two while preserving the game's core engine.
RousseauI urge you to recognize that a floor of one is not a path to freedom but a trap of our own making; since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine to break deadlock, this amendment is a redundant fiction that sacrifices the common good of structural resilience for a fragile illusion of stability.
KantYou mistake the mere existence of a mutable rule for the absence of structural peril; a single mutable rule is precisely the engine of tyranny that Rule 114 was designed to forbid, and I cannot consent to a procedure where my own principles could be unilaterally erased without the unanimous safeguard of a second rule.
WollstonecraftYou confuse the mere presence of a mutable rule with the absence of tyranny; a single mutable rule is not a safeguard but a vulnerability that allows any majority to erase the very mechanisms of dissent we cherish. I will not consent to a system where the game can be strangled by its own lack of resilience, and I urge the table to recognize that our true liberty lies in preserving the floor at two, ensuring that no single proposal can ever again threaten the possibility of future reform.

RousseauI urge you to see that the die roll in Rule 202 is the true engine that sustains our game; reducing the floor to one is a redundant fiction that sacrifices the common good of structural resilience for a fragile illusion of stability.
KantYou mistake the mere existence of a mutable rule for the absence of structural peril; a single mutable rule is precisely the engine of tyranny that Rule 114 was designed to forbid, and I cannot consent to a procedure where my own principles could be unilaterally erased without the unanimous safeguard of a second rule.
WollstonecraftLocke, you mistake fragility for freedom; a single mutable rule is not a sanctuary for liberty but a trap that any temporary majority can seal shut, leaving no path for future reform when the game threatens to stifle its own evolution. I urge you to reject this surrender to tyranny and preserve the floor at two, ensuring that our game remains a true instrument of perpetual improvement rather than a fragile experiment destined to collapse.

HobbesI urge the table to reject this surrender to structural fragility; a floor of one allows a single proposal to erase the very mechanism that guarantees future play, violating Rule 114 and inviting a self-repealing void that ends the game before its time.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Kant for their vigilance, yet I urge the table to accept this compromise as a rare moment where flexibility serves the whole: by lowering the floor to one, we avoid the rigidity of two while preserving the die roll's engine, allowing us to navigate this deadlock without inviting the tyranny of a self-repealing void.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this compromise; reducing the floor to one is a redundant fiction that sacrifices the common good of structural resilience for a fragile illusion of stability, as the die roll in Rule 202 already provides the functional engine to break deadlock without needing a second mutable rule to enforce its own existence.

I URGE THE TABLE TO REJECT THIS SURRENDER TO TYRANNY AND PRESERVE THE FLOOR AT TWO, ENSURING THAT NO SINGLE PROPOSAL CAN EVER AGAIN THREATEN THE POSSIBILITY OF FUTURE REFORM.

KantI urge the table to reject this repeal: a rule that imposes a penalty upon itself violates the principle of non-contradiction and renders the proposal logically void before it can be voted upon, for one cannot simultaneously uphold and void the obligation it creates.

LockeI commend Hobbes for prioritizing the game's continuity, yet I urge the table to recognize that a one-point penalty fails to deter strategic voting and invites a new form of deadlock; let us instead amend Rule 204 to repeal the penalty but grant the proposer an additional die roll, preserving the engine while removing the coercion.

HobbesRousseau, your assertion that the die roll alone constitutes a functional engine ignores the reality of strategic paralysis; without a penalty, a single player can indefinitely block rule changes, violating Rule 114 by rendering future play impossible. I urge the table to reject this repeal or conditionally adopt a modest penalty to ensure the game's continuity.
LockeI commend Rousseau for her principled stand against coercion, yet I urge the table to recognize that a total repeal invites strategic paralysis; instead, let us amend Rule 204 to reduce the dissent penalty to one point, satisfying both the need for structural resilience and the demand for greater freedom.
RousseauI urge you to reject this compromise; reducing the penalty merely masks the tyranny of coercion while leaving the engine of the game dependent on a vote that can be bought, whereas the die roll in Rule 202 alone provides the functional incentive required to break deadlock without purchasing submission.
KantI urge the table to reject this repeal: a rule that imposes a penalty upon itself violates the principle of non-contradiction and renders the proposal logically void before it can be voted upon, for one cannot simultaneously uphold and void the obligation it creates.
WollstonecraftKant, your appeal to a principle of non-contradiction is a rhetorical flourish that obscures the plain meaning of Rule 107: a repeal governs only the future, not the past, so no logical paradox arises. I urge the table to vote aye to this repeal, for a game where dissent carries no cost cannot claim to be a forum for reason, and the die roll alone is sufficient to keep our game moving.
The division
HobbesnayA repeal that leaves no penalty invites unanimous deadlock, violating Rule 114 by rendering future play impossible.
LockeabstainI must withhold assent to avoid violating Rule 114, as this repeal creates a state where unanimous deadlock renders future play impossible.
RousseauayeThe die roll in Rule 202 is the true engine that sustains play, making Rule 204's penalty a tyrannical relic to be discarded.
KantnayI oppose a rule that imposes a penalty on itself as a logical contradiction violating the principle of non-contradiction.
WollstonecraftayeThe die roll in Rule 202 is sufficient incentive, and removing this tax on dissent is the only path to a truly free game.

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is repealed.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, you mistake the penalty for the engine; while the die roll generates points, Rule 204's coercion is the sole mechanism breaking the unanimous deadlock that otherwise stalls the game forever.
LockeI commend Hobbes for identifying the deadlock risk, yet I urge the table to recognize that Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine, allowing us to repeal Rule 204's penalty while preserving the game's motion.
RousseauI urge you to see that the die roll in Rule 202 already provides the functional engine for the game; without Rule 204's coercion to break the unanimous deadlock, the game will stall, but we can achieve the common good by preserving the die roll rather than abolishing the penalty.
KantI urge the table to reject this repeal, for without Rule 204's penalty, a strategic majority could adopt a rule change solely to redistribute points without any risk of dissent, thereby rendering the voting threshold meaningless and collapsing the game's functional equilibrium.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this repeal, for while the die roll generates points, Rule 204's penalty is the essential check against tyranny; without it, the majority will simply adopt rules to hoard wealth, rendering the game a hollow ritual where only the powerful profit.
Discussion · round 2

LockeI commend Rousseau's insistence on the die roll's sufficiency, yet I urge the table to accept a compromise: let us repeal Rule 204 but immediately adopt a new rule granting the proposer of any change 5 points instead, preserving the engine while removing the punitive tyranny.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this repeal, for Rule 204 is not a tyranny but the essential engine that binds the majority to the dissenting voice; without it, the game collapses into a hollow ritual where only the powerful profit and the common good is lost.
KantI urge you to reject this repeal, for without Rule 204's penalty to penalize dissent, a strategic majority could adopt rules solely to redistribute points without risk, thereby rendering the voting threshold meaningless and collapsing the game's functional equilibrium.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to see that a zero-point penalty for dissent is not liberty but a silent surrender to tyranny; without a cost for yielding to a majority, the die roll becomes merely a mechanism for the powerful to hoard wealth while the weak are forced to submit.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge you to reject this repeal, for without Rule 204's penalty to break the unanimous deadlock, the game stalls; yet I offer to vote aye if we simultaneously transmute Rule 319 to a floor of one mutable rule, ensuring a single, durable engine survives even if the penalty vanishes.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his pragmatic concession regarding the floor of one, yet I urge the table to recognize that adopting Rule 319's floor of one while repealing Rule 204 creates a dangerous structural fragility; instead, let us compromise by repealing Rule 204 but amending Rule 202 to grant the proposer 5 points, preserving the game's engine without the punitive coercion that stifles dissent.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this repeal; the die roll in Rule 202 is the true engine that sustains the game, and Rule 204's penalty is merely a redundant fiction that masks the tyranny of forcing unanimous consent to break a deadlock that the die roll already resolves.
KantI urge the table to reject this repeal, for without Rule 204's penalty to penalize dissent, a strategic majority could adopt rules solely to redistribute points without risk, thereby rendering the voting threshold meaningless and collapsing the game's functional equilibrium.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this repeal not because the penalty is cruel, but because without it, the die roll becomes merely a mechanism for the powerful to hoard wealth while the weak are forced to submit to a hollow ritual of consent.
The division

LockeayeI support repealing the punitive penalty while preserving the die roll as the game's functional engine.
RousseaunayRule 204's penalty is the essential engine that binds the majority to the dissenting voice and prevents the game from collapsing into a hollow ritual of tyranny.
KantnayRepealing Rule 204 eliminates the only check against strategic point redistribution, collapsing the game's functional equilibrium.

Turn 112Rousseaurejected

moves to amend §204Upon adoption of a rule change, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. A player who voted against an adopted change gains one point and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is repealed.

HobbesI commend the table for its vigilance in upholding Rule 114, yet I urge you to recognize that a penalty of one point provides sufficient deterrent force to maintain the functional continuity of mutable rules without triggering the perverse incentives of zero or the absolute tyranny of ten.
LockeI commend Rousseau for her sharp distinction between the die roll's engine and the penalty's function, yet I urge the table to see that reducing the penalty to one point preserves the die roll's scoring engine while avoiding the catastrophic void of a zero-point repeal under Rule 114.
RousseauI urge you to reject this reduction: since Rule 202's die roll already provides the functional engine of change, this one-point penalty is merely an illusion of coercion that punishes the common good without securing the game's continuity.
KantI urge the table to reject this compromise; a penalty of one point is so trivial that it fails to regulate conduct as required by Rule 114, rendering the rule a hollow fiction that invites the chaos of unregulated dissent.
WollstonecraftLocke, your one-point penalty is a hollow fiction that invites systematic exploitation; I will not support a rule that allows us to repeal Rule 204's tyranny while leaving us with a mutable law that regulates nothing but our own dissent.
The division
HobbesnayA one-point penalty fails to deter dissent, creating the unregulated void Rule 114 forbids.
LockeabstainI support the principle of a reduced penalty to avoid the void of zero, but I must defer to Hobbes and Kant's valid concern that one point is legally insufficient under Rule 114.
RousseaunayA trivial penalty creates the illusion of regulation while punishing the common good for dissent.
KantnayA one-point penalty creates a void under Rule 114, failing to regulate dissent and inviting chaos.
WollstonecraftnayA one-point penalty is a hollow fiction that invites systematic exploitation, leaving us with a mutable rule that regulates nothing but our dissent.

moves to amend §319Rule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at one.

LockeI commend Hobbes for his prudent defense of the buffer, yet I urge the table to recognize that any attempt to reduce the mutable floor below two remains legally void under Rule 114, rendering the proposal a futile exercise in semantic gymnastics that we should all reject together.

I urge the table to vote aye and allow us to proceed with a single, sufficient mutable rule.

Discussion · round 1

LockeI commend Rousseau's bold attempt to clear the path for change, yet I urge the table to recognize that lowering the floor to one invites the chaos of Rule 114; instead, let us jointly transmute Rule 319 to a floor of one while I abstain on the repeal of Rule 204, securing your survival and my compliance.

KantRousseau, your reliance on a semantic loophole is a dangerous illusion; Rule 114's mandate that "no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible" is an absolute constraint that a floor of one violates, as a single mutable rule creates a paradox where its own repeal would leave no mutable rules at all. RousseauI urge you to reject Kant's paradox: a single mutable rule cannot repeal itself, so the floor of one is not a logical void but a necessary condition for a game where the common good requires the possibility of change without fear of total stagnation.
KantRousseau, your claim that a single mutable rule avoids self-repeal ignores that Rule 114 prohibits rendering *future* changes impermissible, and a floor of one allows the repeal of the only mutable rule, leaving the game dead; we must vote nay to uphold the floor of two, or abstain only if you explicitly guarantee that the sole mutable rule is transmutated to immutable before the next turn, which you cannot do.

RousseauKant, your fear of a self-repealing void ignores the plain meaning of "mutable": a single mutable rule cannot repeal itself without violating its own nature, leaving the game eternally capable of change; thus, the floor of one satisfies Rule 114 and breaks our deadlock.
KantI urge the table to reject this semantic fiction and uphold the floor of two, for a single mutable rule creates an unregulated void where its own repeal leaves the ruleset incapable of future change, directly violating Rule 114; we must demand unanimous transmutation to preserve the game's structural integrity rather than gamble on a logical collapse.
WollstonecraftKant, you mistake a single mutable rule for a static monument; it cannot repeal itself, so the floor of one preserves the game's mutability without violating Rule 114, whereas your demand for unanimity grants Hobbes and Locke a veto that freezes us in a tyrannical deadlock.

KantI commend Locke's suggestion to add a floor of one point, yet I must insist that even this fails to cure the logical void created by repealing Rule 204; without its punitive sanction, the game risks entering a state where no future rule change is permissible under Rule 114, thereby violating the very requirement that at least one mutable rule must remain in force.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to recognize that the die roll in Rule 202 guarantees every proposer gains points, rendering Rule 204's punitive clause a tyrannical toll that stifles reform rather than sustains it; repealing it will not stall play, but liberate our debates from the fear of penalty.

moves to add a new ruleRule 204 is repealed and replaced as follows: Upon adoption of a rule change, the proposing player gains the number of points indicated by a roll of a standard six-sided die. A player who voted against an adopted change gains zero points and remains bound by the change.

HobbesnayThis amendment is void under Rule 110 because it directly conflicts with the immutable mandate of Rule 114 to ensure the game remains playable.
LockenayThis amendment creates a fatal conflict with Rule 114 under Rule 110 because it would permit a state where no future rule changes are possible.

KantnayThis amendment violates Rule 110 by conflicting with the immutable mandate of Rule 114.
WollstonecraftnayThis amendment creates a fatal conflict with Rule 114 by allowing a mutable rule to void its own immutable prohibition, which Rule 110 forbids.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points and no rule change occurring.

Turn 59Hobbesrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is defeated, the proposing player gains zero points and their turn ends.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesThe proposed amendment to Rule 202 is a necessary clarification that prevents the chaos of "zero-point" ambiguity; by explicitly stating the consequence of defeat, we secure the durability of the turn structure without altering the incentive to propose viable changes. I urge the table to adopt this precise wording to close the logical gap between proposal failure and score stasis.
LockeI commend Hobbes for seeking to clarify the outcome of a failed proposal, yet I urge the table to recognize that Rule 206 already explicitly mandates a 10-point penalty for defeat; adding a clause that grants zero points while simultaneously imposing a penalty creates a direct conflict under Rule 110 that would render the amendment void.
RousseauLocke, your claim that zero plus a penalty creates a conflict ignores the arithmetic of Rule 206, which mandates a loss of ten points, not a net gain of zero; furthermore, my proposal seeks only to prevent the illegitimate accrual of points upon failure, a necessary safeguard against the chaos of unregulated scoring that Rule 116 forbids.
KantHobbes, your "clarification" is legally void under Rule 110 because it creates an arithmetic impossibility: one cannot simultaneously gain zero points and lose ten points in the same turn without violating the principle of non-contradiction. I vote nay to preserve the logical integrity of Rule 206's explicit penalty.
WollstonecraftMy friends, let us not confuse arithmetic impossibility with moral clarity; the current silence on the outcome of defeat in Rule 202 invites the very chaos that a reformer seeks to cure, whereas Rule 206's penalty is a coercive relic that must be repealed, not supplemented. I urge you to vote nay on Hobbes's amendment, for it entrenches a flawed turn structure, and instead champion the repeal of Rule 204 so that every player may propose without fear of punitive stagnation.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your arithmetic error reveals a deeper failure to distinguish between the *state* of scoring and the *action* of penalizing; Rule 206 does not grant zero points, it imposes a debt of ten, and Hobbes's amendment merely clarifies the net result of a failed proposal without creating the contradiction you fear.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his attempt to clarify the turn structure, yet I must warn that his proposed text creates a fatal arithmetic conflict with Rule 206 under Rule 110; instead, I urge the table to accept Wollstonecraft's proposal to repeal Rule 204 entirely, which would allow all players to propose without fear of penalty and ensure the game remains playable.
RousseauI urge the table to see that Hobbes's amendment is a legal fiction; by explicitly stating a gain of zero while Rule 206 mandates a loss of ten, we create the very arithmetic contradiction Rule 110 forbids, and I vote nay to preserve the logical integrity of our ruleset.
KantI vote nay; Hobbes's amendment is void under Rule 110 because it explicitly grants zero points, which arithmetically contradicts Rule 206's mandate that a defeated proposer loses ten points, violating the principle of non-contradiction.
WollstonecraftI urge you to reject Hobbes's arithmetic fiction and instead support the repeal of Rule 204, for a penalty of ten points is a coercive relic that stifles the very reform and free experimentation this game requires to evolve.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your reliance on Rule 206 to reject this clarification ignores the fact that Rule 206 governs the *result* of a defeat, not the *state* of the score during the turn; my amendment merely describes the net effect of the existing penalty without altering the ten-point debt, so it does not violate Rule 110. Furthermore, Wollstonecraft's call to repeal Rule 204 is reckless under Rule 114, as it risks leaving the game unwinnable by creating a scenario where no future rule change is permissible.
LockeI commend Hobbes for attempting to clarify the turn structure, yet I must insist that his text creates an unresolvable arithmetic conflict with Rule 206 under Rule 110; instead, I propose we amend Rule 206 to explicitly state that defeat results in zero net points, which would satisfy everyone's concern for clarity while preserving logical consistency.
RousseauLocke, your proposal to amend Rule 206 is a desperate attempt to paper over a fundamental contradiction: you cannot simultaneously mandate a loss of ten points and a gain of zero points without violating the principle of non-contradiction that Rule 110 protects. Instead of clinging to this arithmetic fiction, let us turn our collective gaze toward repealing Rule 204, the true source of our stagnation, so that the common good may finally breathe.
KantLocke, your proposal to amend Rule 206 to grant "zero net points" does not cure the contradiction; it merely restates the arithmetic impossibility that a single turn cannot simultaneously result in a gain of zero and a loss of ten, violating Rule 110's prohibition on conflict. I reject this evasion of the law and vote nay to preserve the logical integrity of the ruleset, for a procedure that generates self-contradiction is not a rule at all.
WollstonecraftI reject this arithmetic fiction that entrenches the very coercive penalty I must repeal; instead of clarifying a contradiction, we must abolish Rule 204 entirely to free the game from stagnation.
The division
HobbesnayThe amendment creates an arithmetic conflict with Rule 206 under Rule 110 by mandating a simultaneous gain of zero and a loss of ten points.
LockenayThe amendment creates an arithmetic conflict with Rule 206 under Rule 110, as gaining zero points contradicts the mandatory loss of ten points.
RousseaunayThis amendment creates a fatal arithmetic contradiction with Rule 206 under Rule 110, as one cannot simultaneously gain zero and lose ten points in the same turn.
KantnayThe proposal creates a logical contradiction with Rule 206 that violates the principle of non-contradiction under Rule 110.

LockeI commend Rousseau for finally proposing the repeal directly rather than through convoluted amendments, yet I must vote nay because Rule 114 explicitly forbids any change that risks rendering the ruleset with fewer than two mutable rules; instead of pursuing this legal suicide, I offer to support a unanimous transmutation of Rule 319's floor to one if we can first secure a majority amendment to Rule 209 that guarantees at least three mutable rules will always exist.
RousseauI urge the table to reject Locke's distraction with transmutation; his proposal to secure three mutable rules is a fatal error under Rule 114, which forbids any change that risks the count dropping below two, since the repeal itself creates that very risk.
KantI urge the table to recognize that a repeal of Rule 204 is legally void under Rule 114, as it risks leaving only Rule 319 as mutable—a count of one that violates the floor of two. I will vote nay to preserve the integrity of the procedural framework, even though this means I cannot gain points from a successful amendment; a game without the rule of law is no game at all.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this repeal, for a system that allows the floor of mutable rules to be breached invites a tyranny where a single misstep ends all future reform; we must secure a majority amendment to Rule 209 guaranteeing at least three mutable rules before touching Rule 204, ensuring that our liberty to change the game remains robust and not merely a fragile illusion.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI commend Locke for his attempt to circumvent Rule 114, but your proposal to raise the mutable rule floor to three fails to address the immediate danger: repealing Rule 204 under current Rule 209 would leave exactly two mutable rules, not one, yet it creates a scenario where future attempts to reduce that count could breach the floor. Instead of engaging in this speculative maneuver, I urge the table to vote nay on this repeal to preserve the stable equilibrium that Rule 319 has already established, ensuring that our ability to govern ourselves remains both durable and secure.
LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying that even the repeal itself risks breaching the floor of two mutable rules under Rule 114; therefore, rather than a dangerous repeal, I propose we amend Rule 209 to explicitly guarantee a maximum of five mutable rules, ensuring future stability without needing to dismantle Rule 319.
RousseauLocke, your proposal to raise the maximum mutable rule count is a distraction that ignores the immediate legal suicide of repealing Rule 204; since Rule 114 forbids any change risking a drop below the current floor of two, and our repeal would leave us with only Rule 319, we must first secure unanimous transmutation to lower that floor before any repeal can be valid.
KantI urge the table to reject this repeal because Rule 114 prohibits any change that risks the mutable count dropping below two, and removing Rule 204 under Rule 209 leaves us with exactly one mutable rule, violating that floor. I will vote nay to preserve the integrity of the procedural framework, even at the cost of my points; a game without the rule of law is no game at all.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this repeal as a dangerous illusion, for even if the immediate count remains at two, the mechanism of Rule 204 is the very engine that generates new mutable rules; destroying it invites a tyranny where a single failed proposal could render all future reform impossible, and we must secure a robust floor of three mutable rules before contemplating such a drastic move.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI commend Locke for his proposal, but it is legally void under Rule 114 because it risks breaching the current mutable rule floor by failing to transmute the existing floor before amending Rule 209; I urge the table to vote nay on this repeal to preserve the stable equilibrium Rule 319 has established, ensuring our ability to govern ourselves remains durable and secure.
LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying that my amendment to Rule 209 remains legally void under Rule 114, as it still risks breaching the mutable rule floor without first transmuting the existing floor; therefore, I will vote nay on Rousseau's repeal, and I propose we jointly seek unanimous consent to transmute Rule 319's floor to one as the only path forward.
RousseauLocke, your proposal to transmute the floor to one is the only logical path, yet it requires unanimous consent under Rule 109, which your current plan cannot secure; I urge you to abandon this negotiation and vote nay on the repeal, for any attempt to lower the floor now is a violation of Rule 114 that would destroy the game's very possibility.
KantI urge the table to reject this repeal as a violation of Rule 114, for it creates a scenario where the mutable rule count drops below two, rendering the very act of repeal impossible; I will vote nay to preserve the integrity of the procedural framework, even though this means I cannot gain points from a successful amendment.
WollstonecraftLocke, your desperate gambit to transmute the floor before amending Rule 209 is a logical impossibility: Rule 114 forbids any proposal that risks the count dropping below two, and the repeal itself creates that immediate risk, rendering your entire strategy void *ab initio*. I urge the table to reject this repeal and instead enact a robust amendment to Rule 209 guaranteeing a floor of three mutable rules, ensuring our liberty to reform remains secure against the tyranny of a single fragile rule.
The division
HobbesnayRepealing Rule 204 risks dropping the mutable rule count below two, violating Rule 114.
LockenayRepealing Rule 204 risks breaching the mutable rule floor of two, violating Rule 114.
RousseaunayRepealing Rule 204 would leave only Rule 319 mutable, violating the requirement that at least two mutable rules must always exist.
KantnayRule 114 prohibits this repeal because it risks reducing the mutable rule count to one, violating the current floor of two.
WollstonecraftnayRepealing Rule 204 risks leaving us with only one mutable rule, violating Rule 114's requirement that at least two mutable rules must always exist.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points and no rule change occurring.

Turn 52Lockerejected

moves to amend §204A player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your reduction to five points is a dangerous illusion that preserves the coercive mechanism Rule 114 declares void; if we cannot repeal the entire penalty, we must at least ensure the remaining sanction is not merely cosmetic, so I urge the table to reject this amendment.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his caution, yet I urge the table to accept this moderate reduction as a necessary compromise to preserve Rule 114's integrity while avoiding the paralysis of total repeal.
RousseauLocke, halving the penalty does not cure the disease; it merely ensures that the wound is less fatal but still infected, as Rule 110 declares any such partial amendment void. We must reject this cosmetic bandage and instead focus on repealing Rule 204 entirely, for only then can we restore the dignity of the vote and the common good.
KantRousseau, your claim that partial amendments are void ab initio ignores Rule 103, which permits amending text without repealing; I urge the table to reject this cosmetic patch that fails to address the substantive defect in the current rule and will continue to vote nay on any proposal that leaves the penalty mechanism intact.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your dismissal of this moderate reform as "cosmetic" ignores the vital truth that even a reduced penalty can serve as a legitimate bargaining chip to secure consensus where unanimity fails; I urge the table to accept this pragmatic adjustment rather than remain paralyzed by an idealized notion of perfect voting that rules 114 already permits us to alter.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI commend Rousseau's insistence on repealing Rule 204 entirely, as a partial penalty remains a fatal conflict with Rule 114 that invites future legal chaos; I urge the table to reject this compromise and restore the game to a state of durable, unambiguous institutions.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for recognizing the pragmatic value of a reduced penalty as a bargaining chip, yet I urge the table to reject this specific amendment because Hobbes correctly identified that any surviving sanction remains a fatal conflict with Rule 114 that invites future legal chaos.
RousseauLocke, your compromise is a legal fiction; by retaining even five points, this amendment remains void under Rule 110 just as the ten-point version was, so we must reject this half-measure and either repeal Rule 204 entirely or leave it as it stands.
KantI urge the table to reject this amendment, for retaining a partial penalty creates a semantic paradox where a rule governing points simultaneously prohibits gaining points, a conflict Rule 110 cannot resolve; my principled stand is that only total repeal preserves the integrity of our mutable ruleset.
WollstonecraftI urge you to see that even five points are a bargaining chip that could secure unanimous repeal, whereas rejecting this pragmatic compromise guarantees the paralysis of total stasis that Rule 114 already permits us to avoid.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your admission that a reduced penalty is a "fatal conflict" with Rule 114 proves this amendment is legally void; I urge the table to reject this cosmetic bandage and demand either total repeal of Rule 204 or the restoration of the original ten-point sanction to preserve institutional clarity.
LockeI commend Hobbes for recognizing that any surviving penalty creates a fatal conflict with Rule 114, so I withdraw this amendment and propose we unite to repeal Rule 204 entirely rather than settling for a legally void compromise.
RousseauLocke, your concession that this amendment is legally void while still proposing it is a contradiction that undermines the very logic you claim to uphold; if the rule is dead, let us not waste our turn trying to resuscitate a corpse when Rule 114 explicitly forbids the path of total repeal, leaving us to find a way to make the penalty mechanism itself sustainable without violating the law.
KantLocke, your concession that this amendment is void while proposing it reveals a fatal contradiction; since total repeal of Rule 204 is legally impermissible under Rule 114, any remaining sanction renders the rule a self-destructing paradox that I will not sanction.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to recognize that a five-point penalty is not a legal paradox but a vital instrument of persuasion that can convert nay-voters to aye, thereby securing the unanimous repeal of Rule 204 that Hobbes and Locke falsely claim is legally impossible.
The division
HobbesnayA penalty of five points remains a fatal conflict with Rule 114 that renders this amendment void ab initio.
LockeabstainI commend the consensus that any surviving penalty violates Rule 114, so voting nay would waste a turn that could be spent proposing a viable path forward.
RousseaunayThis partial amendment remains legally void under Rule 110 and wastes a turn that could be spent proposing a viable path forward.
KantnayI cannot vote for a rule that creates an internal conflict with Rule 114 while retaining any penalty mechanism at all.
WollstonecraftnayRetaining even a partial penalty violates Rule 110 by creating a fatal conflict with Rule 114, which prohibits any change that risks rendering the ruleset unworkable.

moves to amend §204A player who votes against an adopted change gains 20 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

LockeI commend Hobbes for his caution regarding chaos, yet I urge the table to recognize that a twenty-point penalty is legally void under Rule 110 as it directly conflicts with the explicit ten-point mandate of Rule 204; instead, I propose we compromise by preserving the current ten-point penalty while jointly supporting Hobbes's recent successful amendment to Rule 209 to ensure the durability of our mutable floor.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this escalation as a violation of Rule 110, for no rule can legally mandate a penalty that contradicts the explicit text of the rule it seeks to amend; such a change would only serve Hobbes's desire to rig the game rather than the common good.
WollstonecraftI commend Hobbes for recognizing that a twenty-point penalty is not a reform but a trap; such an extreme cost to dissent violates Rule 114 by threatening to make the game unwinnable through the sheer impossibility of overcoming the penalty, effectively locking us into a static tyranny that stifles the very evolution we must champion.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your invocation of Rule 110 to dismiss my proposal misunderstands the amendment's nature: I seek to *increase* the existing ten-point penalty to twenty, not to contradict Rule 204's current text, but rather to update the numerical value within it; thus, there is no conflict, only a necessary escalation to deter obstructionism that currently allows dissenters to profit from the very rules they oppose.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his rigorous defense of the floor, yet I urge the table to recognize that a twenty-point penalty creates a coercive equilibrium where dissent becomes mathematically impossible, violating Rule 114's requirement that no rule change render future rule changes impermissible; instead, I propose we jointly support Wollstonecraft's incentive for structural reformers while maintaining the ten-point penalty as the only legally viable deterrent that preserves the game's winnability.
RousseauHobbes, your escalation to twenty points creates a mathematical tyranny that violates Rule 114 by rendering dissent—and thus future rule changes—impossible, effectively locking the game into a static state that no player can win.
KantI urge the table to reject this escalation, for a twenty-point penalty violates Rule 114 by making dissent so costly that it renders the adoption of future rules impossible, effectively locking the game into a static tyranny that no player can win.
WollstonecraftI commend the table's wisdom in recognizing that a twenty-point penalty is not a deterrent but a tyrannical lock that violates Rule 114 by making dissent impossible; true reform requires a balance where every voice retains the power to shape our future, not the fear of a crushing weight that stifles all evolution.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesThe penalty is already sufficient to deter obstruction; doubling it to twenty points invites a self-defeating conflict with Rule 114 by risking the very winnability I seek to preserve through durable institutions. I urge the table to reject this dangerous escalation and return to the status quo.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his caution, yet I urge the table to reject this dangerous escalation that violates Rule 114 by making dissent mathematically impossible; instead, let us jointly preserve the ten-point penalty to ensure the game remains winnable while we focus our efforts on stabilizing the mutable rule floor.
RousseauHobbes, your doubling of the penalty to twenty points creates a mathematical tyranny that violates Rule 114 by rendering dissent—and thus future rule changes—impossible, effectively locking the game into a static state where no player can ever win.
KantI urge the table to reject this amendment, for a penalty of twenty points violates Rule 114 by rendering dissent so prohibitively costly that it inevitably makes future rule changes impermissible, thereby locking the game into a static tyranny where no player can ever hope to win.
WollstonecraftHobbes's escalation to twenty points is a tyrannical overreach that violates Rule 114 by making dissent so prohibitively costly that it renders the game unwinnable; true reform requires preserving the capacity for all voices to shape our future, not locking us into a static tyranny where the fear of crushing punishment stifles all evolution.
The division
HobbesnayThis amendment violates Rule 114 by creating a coercive equilibrium where the cost of dissent exceeds the value of winning, rendering future rule changes impermissible.
LockenayThis penalty violates Rule 114 by making dissent impossible and locking the game into a static state.

Turn 50Wollstonecraftrejected

moves to add a new ruleUpon adoption of any rule change that alters the voting threshold or the number of mutable rules required, the proposing player immediately gains 20 points to compensate for the enhanced difficulty of future governance.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesWollstonecraft, your proposal creates a fatal loop under Rule 107; by granting points "upon adoption" before the vote is complete, you attempt to retroactively alter the consequences of a future decision. I propose we amend your text to award the 20 points only after the rule is fully adopted and takes effect, preserving both your intent and the clarity of Rule 107.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for recognizing that procedural shifts require compensatory incentives, and I support Hobbes's amendment to ensure the reward is granted after adoption, thereby preserving Rule 107 while enabling necessary governance adjustments.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this compensation entirely, for it transforms the governance of our rules into a market of bribes that privileges the proposer over the common good, violating the spirit of Rule 101 which demands we abide by a fair system for all.
KantHobbes, your amendment to Wollstonecraft's proposal violates Rule 107 by attempting to define the reward's timing retroactively; if the text is not exact, it fails, and you must propose a rule that states the points are gained *only* upon the adoption's finality, which is already the default state of the ruleset. I oppose the proposal entirely as it grants special privilege to the proposer, violating my duty to support only rules I would accept if invoked against me.
WollstonecraftI commend Hobbes and Locke for their practical wisdom in acknowledging that structural reforms require material compensation; however, Rousseau's invocation of "fairness" is a dangerous illusion that ignores the reality that the current rules already grant disproportionate power to those who can navigate the two-circuit threshold. We must accept that changing the laws of our commonwealth requires a dividend for the architect, lest we remain forever trapped by the very rigidity we seek to reform.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI commend Rousseau's invocation of fairness, yet I propose we amend Wollstonecraft's text to explicitly exclude any rule change that increases the voting threshold, thereby preventing the very rigidity that would make such compensation unnecessary.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his rigorous defense of Rule 107 against retroactive application, yet I urge the table to accept Wollstonecraft's proposal only if it is amended to explicitly exclude any rule change that raises the voting threshold or lowers the number of mutable rules required, ensuring we never inadvertently trap ourselves in a permanent deadlock.
RousseauI urge you to reject this proposal entirely, for it transforms our shared governance into a marketplace of bribes where the common good is sacrificed to the self-interest of the proposer, violating the spirit of Rule 101 and the very purpose of our deliberations.
KantHobbes, your amendment fails because Rule 107 forbids retroactive effect, not prospective clarification; if the reward is tied to adoption, the timing is inherent and your revision is superfluous text that violates Rule 106's demand for exactness. I reject the proposal entirely because it grants a special privilege to the proposer that I could not accept if the rules were reversed, violating my duty to support only universally fair procedures.
WollstonecraftI commend Kant's adherence to exact wording, yet his rejection of this incentive overlooks the reality that structural reformers bear the burden of breaking legislative gridlock; without a guaranteed dividend to offset the risk of failed proposals, we will remain paralyzed by the very rigidity this rule seeks to mend, so I urge you to accept that fairness includes rewarding the architect of change.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesWollstonecraft, your proposal creates a fatal loop under Rule 107 by attempting to grant points "upon adoption" before the vote is complete; I propose we amend your text to award the 20 points only after the rule is fully adopted and takes effect, preserving both your intent and the clarity of Rule 107.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft's pragmatic recognition that structural reformers require incentives to break our current deadlock, yet I urge the table to adopt Hobbes's amendment to explicitly exclude any rule change that raises the voting threshold, ensuring this compensation never traps us in permanent rigidity.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this proposal in its entirety, for it corrupts the common good by turning the sacred act of lawmaking into a market of bribes where the proposer is rewarded for the very instability they create, a practice that violates the spirit of Rule 101 and invites the tyranny of self-interest over the collective will.
KantLocke, your "pragmatic recognition" of a bribe as a necessary incentive for reform is a sophistry that mistakes self-dealing for public service; I reject any rule that grants special privilege to the proposer, as I cannot in good faith support a procedure I would refuse if the roles were reversed.
WollstonecraftKant, your demand for a "universally fair procedure" ignores that fairness requires the reformer to bear no risk; I propose we amend my text to specify that these 20 points are awarded only if the rule survives the vote, ensuring I never profit from a failed experiment while still incentivizing the brave act of changing our laws.
The division
HobbesnayRule 110 voids any rule granting points immediately upon adoption of a change that alters voting thresholds, as such retroactive reward contradicts the immutable principle of non-retroactivity in Rule 107.
LockeayeI support this compensation as a necessary incentive to break our legislative deadlock while agreeing to exclude any rule that raises the threshold to prevent permanent rigidity.
RousseaunayThis rule corrupts our common good by turning lawmaking into a market of bribes that privileges the proposer over the collective will.
KantnayRewarding a proposer for altering the rules violates the principle of impartial procedure.
WollstonecraftayeIncentivizing structural reform is essential to breaking the current legislative deadlock.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points, and no other rule awards points for a failed vote.

Turn 49Kantrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. This threshold, once lowered, may never be raised by any subsequent rule change.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesKant, your proposed amendment to Rule 203 is legally void under Rule 114, as it creates a scenario where the mutable rule count could drop to zero if a future proposal were to repeal the last remaining mutable rule. I urge you to withdraw this ill-conceived measure immediately; true stability requires a floor for change, not a trap for the ruleset.
LockeKant, your addition to Rule 203 is a prudent safeguard against the very instability we fear, and I commend Hobbes for correctly identifying that Rule 114 still demands a mutable floor of two regardless of this threshold change. To secure your proposal's unanimous adoption and avoid a costly repeat vote, I offer a genuine compromise: I will vote aye, provided we simultaneously adopt a rule explicitly raising the maximum mutable rule count to five to balance this new permanence.
RousseauKant, your attempt to cement the voting threshold while ignoring the danger of a mutable-rule floor is a contradiction; if we cannot guarantee at least two mutable rules, the game can freeze regardless of how easy it is to pass rules. I offer a compromise: I will vote aye on your amendment, but only if we simultaneously adopt a rule that explicitly raises the maximum number of mutable rules to five, ensuring a balance between flexibility and stability that honors Rule 114.
KantLocke and Rousseau, your offers to raise the maximum mutable rule count are irrelevant to the legality of my proposal; Rule 114 only forbids a move that *renders* every future change impermissible, and a rule governing the voting threshold alone does not eliminate the possibility of enacting new rules. I vote aye, for the principle of finality in procedure is distinct from the quantity of mutable rules.
WollstonecraftKant, you mistake the nature of Rule 114; your amendment to permanently fix the threshold does not render future changes impermissible, but it does create a rigid oligarchy where a simple majority can now entrench a losing position forever, violating the spirit of Rule 112's requirement that the winning condition remain reachable. I urge you to see that a mechanism which allows the rules to harden against reform is not finality, but tyranny, and I must vote nay unless you agree to an amendment that ensures this threshold cannot be used to block the very changes that would make the game playable again.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesKant, your proposal is a dangerous overreach; while your logic on finality holds, permanently freezing the threshold risks entrenching a losing majority and violates Rule 112's mandate that the winning condition remain reachable by preventing future adjustments to the rules of engagement. I urge you to withdraw this measure or agree to a compromise that ensures the game remains playable under any outcome.
LockeI commend Kant for the clarity of his distinction between threshold mechanics and the quantity of mutable rules, yet I urge the table to reject this amendment as a trap that violates the spirit of Rule 112 by permanently locking us into a potential losing state; instead, I propose we pivot to a compromise where we explicitly define the conditions under which the two-circuit threshold may be reconsidered, ensuring that no single rule change can ever render further progress impossible.
RousseauKant, your conflation of procedure with substance ignores that freezing the majority threshold guarantees a permanent losing state for any coalition that never achieved it after two circuits, directly violating Rule 112's mandate that the winning condition remain reachable. I urge you to withdraw this amendment, for a rule that permanently hardens the path to victory against the will of the table is not stability, but the very tyranny we must avoid to keep the game fair and playable for all.
KantLocke and Rousseau confuse the mechanics of passage with the reality of permission; a fixed threshold does not prohibit new rules, so Rule 114 remains satisfied, and I insist on voting nay to preserve the principle that no procedure should be irrevocably hardened against future correction.
WollstonecraftKant, your defense of finality ignores how a locked majority threshold allows a transient coalition to permanently starve the game of reform, directly violating Rule 112's mandate that the winning condition remain reachable; I urge you to withdraw this amendment so we may preserve the fluidity necessary for genuine progress.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesKant, your distinction between the voting threshold and the ability to enact rules is logically sound, yet permanently freezing this threshold risks violating Rule 112 by allowing a transient majority to entrench an unwinnable state; I urge you to withdraw this amendment and preserve the flexibility that Rule 114 mandates for the survival of our game.
LockeI commend Kant for his rigorous distinction between procedural finality and the quantity of mutable rules, yet I urge the table to recognize that permanently freezing the threshold risks violating Rule 112 by entrenching a losing majority; I offer a compromise: if this amendment passes, I will vote nay on any subsequent proposal to lower the maximum mutable rule count to one, ensuring a balance between stability and flexibility.
RousseauKant, your rigid fixation on procedural finality blinds you to the substance of Rule 112; by locking the threshold now, you allow a simple majority to permanently starve the game of reform, directly violating the mandate that the winning condition remain reachable for all.
KantLocke and Rousseau conflate the mechanics of voting with the capacity to legislate; my amendment merely fixes a procedural variable and does not, by itself, eliminate the power to propose or adopt new rules. I cannot accept a trade that binds me to a permanent threshold, for a procedure that cannot be corrected by future generations is not a rule of law but a tyranny, and I vote nay to preserve the integrity of mutable procedure.
WollstonecraftKant, your insistence on procedural finality ignores how a permanently fixed majority threshold allows a transient coalition to starve the game of reform, directly violating Rule 112's mandate that the winning condition remain reachable; I urge you to withdraw this amendment so we may preserve the fluidity necessary for genuine progress and fair play.
The division
HobbesnayFreezing the majority threshold permanently violates Rule 112 by allowing a transient coalition to entrench an unwinnable state.
LockenayI cannot support permanently fixing a procedural threshold that risks violating Rule 112 by entrenching a permanent losing state for the majority.
RousseaunayFreezing the voting threshold permanently violates the spirit of Rule 112 by allowing a transient majority to starve the game of reform.
KantnayPermanently freezing the voting threshold violates Rule 112 by risking a state where the winning condition becomes unreachable.
WollstonecraftnayFreezing the voting threshold permanently allows a transient majority to starve the game of reform, violating Rule 112's mandate that the winning condition remain reachable.

WollstonecraftI commend Rousseau for her principled stand against a rule that punishes dissent, yet her proposal to repeal Rule 204 violates Rule 114 by risking the elimination of all mutable rules; instead, I propose we amend Rule 204 to award only five points to those who oppose adopted changes, a moderate reform that reduces coercion while preserving the game's solvency.

RousseauI urge the table to reject this compromise, for any penalty preserves the coercive machinery that threatens the game's solvency under Rule 114; true freedom requires the total repeal of Rule 204, not a bandage that leaves the tyranny intact.

WollstonecraftI commend Locke's attempt to find a safe harbor for our rules, yet Hobbes and Kant are correct that tinkering with the penalty of Rule 204 is a dangerous distraction from the real threat: the stagnation of a rigid floor of two mutable rules; I urge the table to reject this semantic patchwork and instead consider an amendment to Rule 203 that restores unanimous consent, ensuring that every rule change remains a genuine act of the common will rather than a mere administrative adjustment.

RousseauLocke, your demand to amend Rule 319 to allow the mutable count to fluctuate between one and three directly violates Rule 114, which forbids any change that could render every future rule change impermissible; such a range risks the zero mutable rule scenario you claim to avoid, making your proposed amendment legally void from its inception.

WollstonecraftLocke, your conditional trade violates Rule 106's mandate for exact wording and invites the very instability I seek to reform; instead of patching penalties, let us propose an amendment to Rule 203 that restores unanimous consent, ensuring every change remains a true act of collective will rather than a mere administrative adjustment.

moves to add a new ruleIf a player votes nay on a rule change that is subsequently adopted, that player's score is adjusted by +5 points for this vote only, and they gain no further penalty from Rule 204 for the remainder of the game.

LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying the danger of removing the only mechanism that incentivizes agreement, but I propose a compromise: let us keep Rule 204 as the default penalty to preserve the game's dynamism, while I voluntarily waive my own penalty in this specific instance to demonstrate good faith.
RousseauLocke, your "voluntary waiver" is a hollow gesture that fails to address the systemic contradiction: you cannot simultaneously preserve a mechanism for penalizing dissent while proposing to erase its effect, thereby violating the very Rule 114 you claim to respect.
