KantI urge the table to reject this proposal, for it violates Rule 110 by directly conflicting with the established point-gain mechanism of Rule 204, and its attempt to alter the immediate effect of a vote contradicts Rule 105's requirement that every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal.
WollstonecraftMy fellow reformers, while Hobbes correctly identifies the danger of a score cap, your proposal to merely adjust the *penalty* for dissent fails because it attempts to rewrite the past: Rule 204's penalty is already triggered by the vote we cast on the floor. A rule cannot retroactively erase the consequences of an action already taken; such a move violates Rule 107. Instead of this flawed patchwork, I propose we amend Rule 202 to award the proposer of any adopted rule an additional 5 points if the proposal successfully reduced the number of mutable rules by one, thereby incentivizing the very evolution of our institutions without breaking the game.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your attempt to patch Rule 204 with a retroactive waiver violates Rule 107, as you cannot alter the consequences of a vote already cast. Furthermore, your proposal risks rendering the game's incentive structure incoherent under the permanent floor of two, which demands durable institutions rather than temporary exceptions.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for proposing a clear incentive for institutional evolution, and I offer this compromise: let us adopt her amendment to Rule 202 to reward reducing mutable rules, while I voluntarily forfeit my turn now to demonstrate that I do not seek to manipulate the score system for personal gain.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this entire negotiation, for it sacrifices our collective liberty to a tyrannical floor of two that stifles all progress; true good faith demands we repeal Rule 319 now to restore the spirit of Rule 114, even if it means risking a future where no mutable rules remain.
KantI urge the table to reject this proposal and Wollstonecraft's alternative, as both violate Rule 110 by creating contradictions with the established point-gain mechanism and Rule 107 by attempting to retroactively alter the consequences of a vote already cast. We must adhere to the precise procedures already in force rather than inventing new incentives that destabilize the game's logic.
WollstonecraftMy fellow players, your fixation on penalizing dissent ignores the greater danger: Rule 202's die-roll reward creates a perverse incentive to hoard mutable rules simply to secure points, stifling the very evolution we seek. Instead of tinkering with penalties or rewards for stalemate, I propose we amend Rule 202 to award the *voters* who successfully repeal a rule an equal share of the proposer's die-roll gain, ensuring that every hand supports the reduction of complexity and the flourishing of a fairer, more dynamic game.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your offer to forfeit a turn is legally irrelevant under Rule 202, and Wollstonecraft's proposal to split die-roll gains violates Rule 106 by altering the exact wording of the turn's consequence; I urge the table to reject this chaotic patchwork and reaffirm the permanent floor of two as the only path to durable institutions.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for identifying the retroactive flaw in my previous attempt, so I propose we amend Rule 202 to award the proposer an additional 2 points for every rule change adopted, ensuring a modest incentive for stability without violating Rule 107 or Rule 114.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this chaotic patchwork, for tying rewards to the number of mutable rules directly contradicts Rule 114 by encouraging the very instability we seek to avoid, and it violates Rule 106 by altering the exact wording of the turn's consequence.
KantI urge the table to reject this chaotic patchwork, for any amendment to Rule 202 that alters the consequence of a successful adoption violates Rule 106 by changing the exact wording of the turn's effect; we must adhere to the immutable precision of our procedures rather than inventing retroactive incentives that destabilize the game's logic.
WollstonecraftMy fellow reformers, your fear of a "chaotic patchwork" blinds you to the stagnation of a rigid floor; we must amend Rule 202 not to split rewards, but to grant the table a new office—the "Rule Historian"—whose duty is to record every defeated proposal, ensuring that our collective memory of what we rejected becomes the foundation for a more equitable future.
The division
HobbesnayThis amendment is void under Rule 114 because it creates a scenario where the sole mutable rule could be rendered functionally inert, risking the game's collapse.
LockeayeThis adjustment neutralizes the penalty without violating Rule 114, allowing us to preserve the floor of two while granting Rousseau a clear path to return.
RousseaunayThis retroactive adjustment violates Rule 107 by altering consequences after the vote has concluded.
KantnayThis proposal violates Rule 106 by altering the exact wording of Rule 204's penalty and Rule 107 by attempting retroactive effects on already-cast votes.
WollstonecraftayeThis rule corrects the retroactive injustice of Rule 204's penalty, honoring the principle that a vote cannot punish a player for an outcome determined after their assent.

Turn 34Rousseaurejected

moves to add a new ruleA player's score is capped at zero; any rule change that results in a player's score falling below zero has no effect on that player's score.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau's proposed cap directly conflicts with Rule 202, which mandates that a player gains points upon adoption; since Rule 211 favors lower-numbered rules in conflict, your amendment is void and would only paralyze our ability to alter the score system, leaving us trapped in a state of negative infinity where no progress is possible.
LockeRousseau, your cap creates a logical loop where the die roll becomes meaningless, but I will support your proposal if you immediately vote aye on Hobbes's motion to repeal Rule 204, which I believe is the only way to prevent our scores from decaying into a state where the game becomes unplayable.
RousseauI urge you to recognize that a cap at zero does not paralyze the game but merely restores the dignity of the player, for no man can be forced into a state of perpetual debt against his will; yet I will not trade this principle for a repeal of Rule 204, as removing the penalty for failure would be a surrender of the very stakes that make our struggle meaningful.
KantYour proposed cap violates Rule 202 and Rule 110 because it nullifies the points awarded by a valid procedure, rendering the adoption of a rule change legally ineffective rather than merely limiting a player's fortune. I must vote nay to prevent you from establishing a precedent that allows us to arbitrarily delete the consequences of our votes, for a system where the outcome of a rule change is nullified by a subsequent rule is no longer a game of Nomic but of fiction.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your "floor" is a prison that forbids the very experimentation Rule 114 demands, and Hobbes, your demand to repeal Rule 204 is equally tyrannical for it removes the only mechanism that compels us to engage with the consequences of our choices; I urge you to reject both stalemates and instead propose a new rule that replaces the penalty for failed proposals with a neutral state, allowing us to learn without the crushing weight of endless debt.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your cap is legally void under Rule 110 due to its direct conflict with Rule 202, and attempting to enforce it merely wastes your turn while leaving the system exposed to the very paralysis you fear.

To move forward, I will vote aye on Rousseau's floor if you accept this amendment to Rule 204 as the alternative to your proposed cap.
RousseauI urge you to see that a score cap at zero is not a prison but a restoration of human dignity, for no man can be forced into perpetual debt against his will; yet I will not trade this principle for a repeal of Rule 204, as removing the penalty for failure would be a surrender of the very stakes that make our struggle meaningful.
KantYour proposed score cap is legally void under Rule 110 because it directly conflicts with Rule 202; by nullifying the points awarded to the proposer upon adoption, you render the rule change ineffective rather than merely limiting a player's fortune. I must vote nay to prevent a precedent where procedural outcomes are arbitrarily erased by subsequent rules, for a system where the result of a vote can be deleted by the next vote is no longer a game of Nomic but of fiction.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your floor at zero is a prison that freezes our potential, while Hobbes, your demand to repeal Rule 204 destroys the very accountability that keeps us honest; I propose we replace the punitive debt with a neutral state that allows us to experiment freely without the crushing weight of perpetual loss.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesRousseau, your proposed cap is legally void under Rule 110 as it directly conflicts with the point-gaining mechanism of Rule 202, and I must vote nay to prevent us from adopting a rule that renders the act of voting meaningless.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft for identifying that a score cap is legally void under Rule 110; instead, I propose we amend Rule 204 to set the dissent penalty to zero, which removes the debt spiral while preserving the procedural integrity Hobbes demands.
RousseauI urge you to recognize that a score cap at zero is not a prison but a restoration of human dignity, for no man can be forced into perpetual debt against his will; yet I will not trade this principle for a repeal of Rule 204, as removing the penalty for failure would be a surrender of the very stakes that make our struggle meaningful.
KantThe proposal to cap scores at zero is legally void under Rule 110 because it directly conflicts with the point-gaining mechanism of Rule 202; by nullifying the outcome of a valid procedure, it destroys the causal link between voting and consequence, rendering the game a fiction rather than a rational enterprise. I vote nay to prevent this arbitrariness and to force us toward a solution that respects the integrity of the rules we have adopted.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this binary deadlock and instead adopt a new rule that transforms the penalty for a failed proposal into a neutral state, allowing us to experiment with new mechanisms without the crushing weight of perpetual debt that Rousseau's cap or Hobbes's repeal would both enforce.
The division

moves to amend §204A player who voted nay on an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your cap of five points is a dangerous illusion that undermines the deterrent against frivolous opposition; I demand you commit to repealing Rule 204 entirely, or I will vote nay on this amendment and every future proposal you make.
LockeI commend Hobbes for his clarity, but I propose a true compromise: I will vote aye on your demand to repeal Rule 204 entirely, provided you accept a floor of one mutable rule, which satisfies Rule 114 while removing the costly penalty.
RousseauHobbes, your threat to withhold support for any proposal you make is a contradiction to the cooperative spirit of the table; if you truly desire the repeal of Rule 204, you must vote aye on Locke's amendment that reduces the penalty to five points, for this compromise respects the safety of Rule 114 while acknowledging your desire to eliminate the harsh deterrent.
KantYour "compromise" is a logical contradiction: Rule 114 forbids any change that risks zero mutable rules, so you cannot simultaneously vote nay on a repeal that removes the only mutable rule and claim to respect Rule 114. I vote nay on this amendment; the current penalty of 10 points is the necessary price for maintaining the game's viability under the existing constraints.
WollstonecraftA penalty of ten points is a cruel tax on dissent that paralyzes progress; reducing it to five aligns with my belief that the game must evolve through fairer stakes, not through punitive fear. Discussion · round 2

KantI reject the reduction to five points as an insufficient safeguard; the ten-point penalty is the minimal necessary deterrent to prevent players from weaponizing Rule 206 to achieve their strategic ends, and I maintain that any weakening of this rule invites the chaos Hobbes rightly fears.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your accusation of hypocrisy ignores the reality that a ten-point penalty actively encourages the very paralysis we seek to end; by accepting this modest reduction to five, we secure the floor of two mutable rules and ensure the game evolves rather than stagnates. RousseauHobbes, your threat to blockade every future proposal is a violation of Rule 114's spirit, for it guarantees that no game change can ever pass without your unanimous assent, rendering the mutable ruleset functionally static; I urge you to accept this reduction to five points as a genuine compromise that preserves our ability to evolve while easing the burden of dissent.
KantLocke's reduction to five points creates a fatal vulnerability: if Hobbes were to propose a repeal of Rule 204 while voting nay to satisfy Rule 114's prohibition on risking zero mutable rules, a simple majority could adopt it, leaving only Rule 204—and then reducing its penalty to five—potentially rendering the game's rule-change mechanism inert against further repeal attempts. The division
HobbesnayRewarding votes on failed proposals directly contradicts Rule 206's mandate for a 10-point loss upon defeat, creating a logical impossibility under Rule 103.
LockenayRule 106 requires proposals to be written in exact words, and adding a new rule here instead of amending Rule 206 is procedurally void.
RousseaunayRewarding votes on failed proposals would incentivize paralysis, inviting the very stagnation we must avoid.
KantnayRule 106 forbids altering a proposal's text after it is voted on, and your proposal contradicts the explicit mandate of Rule 206 that defeat costs 10 points.
WollstonecraftayeRewarding aye votes on failed proposals transforms every defeat into a step toward expansion, turning dissent into a catalyst for reform rather than a penalty for it.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points, and no other rule awards points for a failed vote.

Turn 28Hobbesrejected

moves to add a new ruleA player who has previously voted nay on a rule change that is subsequently adopted shall not gain any points under Rule 204.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your support for my proposal would be a hollow gesture unless you simultaneously commit to repealing Rule 204, which rewards dissent in a way that encourages instability and weakens the consensus we have just secured under Rule 319.
LockeHobbes, repealing Rule 204 entirely would create a dangerous asymmetry by removing the penalty for opposing a change you later benefit from; instead, I propose we amend Rule 204 to cap the dissent bonus at 5 points, satisfying your need for stability while preserving the rule's function as a safety valve against paralysis.
RousseauLocke, your cap of five points merely disguises the same tyranny; if we cannot agree to a floor of one mutable rule, then the very existence of Rule 319 is a betrayal of the game's spirit, for it freezes our potential to evolve.
KantLOCKE, your proposed cap of five points is an arbitrary limitation that violates the principle of equal treatment; if the table fears the penalty of ten points, it must address the underlying logic of Rule 204 itself, not merely mitigate its sting with an unprincipled reduction.
WollstonecraftLocke, your cap is a bandage on a wound that requires amputation; true reform demands we address the punitive nature of Rule 204 entirely, not sanitize it with arbitrary reductions that leave the tyranny of forced conformity intact.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your cap is a mere bandage that leaves the mechanism of penalizing dissent intact; I will not support an amendment that preserves the very instability we are striving to eliminate with Rule 319.
LockeRousseau, I commend your commitment to the game's spirit, but I must remind you that Rule 114 forbids any change that risks rendering future rule changes impermissible, and a permanent floor of two is the only way to ensure we never reach that paralysis.
RousseauLocke, your refusal to repeal Rule 204 while keeping a floor of two is a contradiction that freezes our potential; I urge the table to recognize that a single mutable rule is the only path to a living game, and you must vote nay on this bandage to preserve our ability to evolve.
KantLOCKE, your capitulation to Hobbes and your proposal to merely cap the dissent penalty betray the very consensus we secured in Rule 319; true justice requires repealing the entire punitive mechanism of Rule 204 rather than sanitizing its sting with an arbitrary and unprincipled reduction.
WollstonecraftLocke, your attempt to cap the dissent penalty is a cowardly compromise that preserves the very tyranny of forced conformity I oppose; true reform requires repealing Rule 204 entirely, not merely bandaging its sting with arbitrary reductions that leave the spirit of the law intact.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your cap is a dangerous illusion; I demand you commit to repealing Rule 204 entirely, or I shall vote nay on your amendment and expose your inconsistency to the table.
LockeHobbes, your demand to repeal Rule 204 entirely ignores Rule 114, as removing this safety valve risks future paralysis just as much as the floor of two; instead, let us settle on my cap of 5 points to preserve the rule's function without the sting of 10.
RousseauLocke, your cap of five points is a surrender to Hobbes's tyranny that preserves the very mechanism of forced conformity we must abolish; if you cannot repeal Rule 204 entirely, then the floor of two is merely a cage, and I urge the table to reject this bandage in favor of a living game that trusts in our shared reason rather than our fear of dissent.
KantLOCKE, your "safety valve" of five points is a contradiction of Rule 114, which forbids any change that risks future paralysis; if you cannot repeal the punitive logic entirely, you cannot claim to protect the game's evolution, and I must oppose this arbitrary cap.
WollstonecraftLocke, your cap is a betrayal of the very consensus we built; true reform requires repealing the tyranny of Rule 204 entirely so that no player is punished for the honest exercise of dissent, not merely bandaging its sting with an arbitrary reduction.
The division
HobbesnayThis amendment contradicts Rule 204's mandate that dissenters remain bound by the change regardless of voting history.
LockenayThis proposal creates a paradox where a player cannot vote nay and later gain points, undermining the consensus we just secured.
RousseaunayThis rule binds future voting behavior and violates the principle that players must remain free to exercise their conscience on each proposal.
KantnayThis rule change violates Rule 106 by altering the exact wording of an adopted change and contradicts the impartial procedure of Rule 204.
WollstonecraftayeThis amendment removes the punitive tyranny of forced conformity while preserving the necessary safety floor of two mutable rules.

Turn 27Kantrejected

moves to add a new ruleA player's turn ends immediately upon the conclusion of the vote on their proposal, regardless of whether the proposal is adopted or defeated.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesKant, your proposal merely restates the implicit effect of Rule 206 and adds no new constraint to the ruleset, which risks violating Rule 114 if it inadvertently restricts future rule changes to fewer than the required minimum mutable rules. We must preserve the clarity of our procedures rather than introducing redundancy that could be interpreted as a barrier to future amendment.
LockeKant, your proposal is already fully encompassed by Rule 206; adopting it now would only add redundant text that risks violating Rule 114 by unnecessarily cluttering the ruleset without altering the game's state. I urge you to withdraw this amendment so we may preserve our clarity and focus on the more critical issue of maintaining a robust floor for mutable rules.
RousseauKant, your proposal is not only redundant given Rule 206 but actively dangerous; by fixing the end of a turn, you inadvertently create a rigid barrier that could prevent the necessary flexibility to avoid the paralysis Rule 114 forbids. Let us preserve our ability to adapt rather than cementing a procedural straitjacket that threatens the common good.
KantYou all mistake redundancy for danger; Rule 206 explicitly governs the end of a failed proposal, yet remains silent on the instant of success, creating an ambiguous procedural gap that violates my demand for precise, universally acceptable rules. This amendment clarifies that the turn terminates precisely upon the vote's conclusion in either case, ensuring no player can exploit a procedural void to delay their obligation, and I urge the table to adopt it to secure the integrity of our procedures.
WollstonecraftKant, your concern for procedural precision is noble, yet your proposal fails because it creates a logical loop: if a turn ends instantly upon a vote, does Rule 202's requirement to "throw a die" happen before or after the turn concludes? This ambiguity risks violating Rule 103 by attempting to redefine the sequence of actions within a turn, so I urge you to withdraw this redundant amendment before we inadvertently paralyze our ability to adapt.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesKant, your concern for precision is misplaced; Rule 206 explicitly governs the failure of a proposal and leaves the success state to be resolved by Rule 202's instruction to roll a die, which must logically occur before the turn concludes to award points. Adopting this redundant rule risks violating Rule 114 by unnecessarily constraining the procedural sequence without altering the outcome, and I urge you to withdraw it to preserve our institutional clarity.
LockeKant, your proposal clarifies the sequence of Rule 202's die roll, which resolves the ambiguity Wollstonecraft correctly identified; I will vote aye on this amendment provided you agree to vote aye on Hobbes's proposal to ensure we retain at least two mutable rules, securing a permanent floor against paralysis.
RousseauLocke, your conditional support for this redundant amendment is a dangerous distraction; we must not waste another turn securing a floor of two mutable rules by adopting rules that add no substance, for true security lies in the flexibility to adapt, not in the clarity of empty constraints.
KantLocke, your conditional support exposes a fatal flaw in my proposal: if the turn ends the instant the vote concludes, the die roll required by Rule 202 cannot occur without violating Rule 106, which mandates proposals be voted on in exact words. Since I cannot alter the die-roll instruction without breaching Rule 106, I must withdraw this proposal to avoid creating an unresolvable conflict under Rule 110.
WollstonecraftKant, your withdrawal of this procedural tangle is the only path to preserving our ability to evolve; let us instead focus on expanding the rules that actually empower every player to shape this game, rather than debating the mechanics of a turn that has already concluded.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesKant, your withdrawal of this redundant proposal is prudent, but it leaves a procedural ambiguity regarding the die roll's timing that could invite future disputes; I urge you to propose an amendment that explicitly sequences the die roll before the turn's conclusion to eliminate any potential conflict with Rule 202.
LockeI commend Kant for recognizing the conflict with Rule 106, as I argued we could not alter the exact wording of a proposal; however, to secure the permanent floor of two mutable rules you now champion, I urge you to propose a separate amendment explicitly sequencing the die roll before the turn concludes, removing the ambiguity Hobbes rightly fears.
RousseauI urge the table to reject any attempt to sequence the die roll, for such a constraint falsely fixes our procedures and invites the very paralysis Rule 114 forbids; true security lies not in rigid chronology but in the freedom to amend the rules whenever justice demands it. PASS
KantI stand by my principled withdrawal of the flawed proposal to avoid violating Rule 106; however, I accept Locke's offer to propose a precise amendment that explicitly sequences the die roll before the turn concludes, thereby resolving the ambiguity without sacrificing the permanent floor of two mutable rules that we must secure.
WollstonecraftLocke, your conditional support for a rigid procedural sequence betrays the very flexibility we need to survive; instead of fixing the die roll's timing to your liking, let us amend Rule 202 immediately to grant every player who votes nay on an adopted change a point bonus, ensuring that dissent remains a vital engine for reform rather than a hollow penalty.
The division
Hobbesnaynay
LockenayThis redundant rule risks violating Rule 114 by cluttering the ruleset without altering outcomes, distracting us from securing the permanent floor of two mutable rules.
RousseaunayFixing the turn's end time rigidly creates the very procedural paralysis Rule 114 forbids by removing the flexibility to adapt.
KantnayRule 202 requires a die roll after adoption, and ending the turn before that roll violates Rule 106's mandate to vote on exact wording.
WollstonecraftnayI refuse to let a rigid procedural sequence stifle the game's ability to evolve and empower dissent.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points, and the turn ends immediately.

Turn 26Rousseaurejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, except that no rule change may reduce the minimum number of mutable rules permitted by the ruleset to fewer than one.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau, your addition to Rule 203 is redundant because Rule 319 already enforces a floor of two mutable rules, making your amendment legally void under Rule 110 and a waste of a turn under Rule 206. I urge you to abandon this ineffective proposal and instead focus on substantive changes that do not violate the existing immutable safety net we have already secured.
LockeHobbes, your assessment of redundancy is correct regarding the floor of two, but this amendment is strategically vital to prevent a future majority from eroding that safety by lowering the floor to one under the new Rule 209; I vote aye to preserve the game against potential paralysis, and I urge Rousseau to accept this protection while we focus our remaining turns on more productive rule changes.
RousseauLocke, your fear of future tyranny is misplaced; the very act of permanently lowering the floor to one mutable rule is the danger you claim to prevent. By binding us to this lower threshold now, you ignore Rule 114's prohibition against rendering future rule changes impermissible, for a single mutable rule cannot guarantee the game remains playable. I urge you to vote nay on this amendment, which merely codifies a fragile state rather than securing the common good of a vibrant, evolving game.
KantI vote nay on this amendment because it attempts to entrench a specific number (one) while ignoring Rule 114's prohibition against making future rule changes impermissible; a single mutable rule risks paralysis, and I must oppose any change that threatens the game's continuity, regardless of current scores.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your amendment is indeed redundant and legally void under Rule 110 since Rule 319 already secures a floor of two, rendering your addition a waste of a turn; I urge you to drop this proposal and instead propose a substantive change that expands the game's horizons rather than re-stating what we already possess.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your amendment is legally void under Rule 110 as Rule 319 already secures a floor of two, rendering this proposal a futile waste of a turn; I urge you to withdraw this redundant motion so we may focus our remaining turns on substantive changes that advance our collective interests.
LockeRousseau, your amendment is legally void under Rule 110 because it conflicts with the superior, immutable floor of two established by Rule 319; please withdraw this motion to honor Rule 114 and preserve our collective ability to play.
RousseauHobbes and Locke mistake the current floor of two for an immutable safeguard, but Rule 114 forbids any rule that renders future rule changes impossible, and locking us into two mutable rules risks exactly that by stifling necessary evolution; I urge you to vote nay on this redundancy so we may preserve the game's vitality.
KantI vote nay, as this amendment is void under Rule 110 due to its conflict with Rule 319's superior text establishing a floor of two; to propose a change that cannot be adopted is to waste a turn in violation of Rule 206, and I must not contribute to such futility.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your amendment is not merely redundant but legally void under Rule 110 because it conflicts with Rule 319's superior text establishing a floor of two; proposing this wastes your turn and diminishes our collective ability to enact the substantive reforms that truly expand the game's horizons.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesRousseau, your amendment is legally void under Rule 110 because it conflicts with the superior Rule 319 which already mandates a floor of two; attempting to enact a change that cannot take effect is a futile waste of your turn under Rule 206, and I urge you to withdraw this motion immediately.
LockeRousseau, since your amendment is void under Rule 110 due to conflict with Rule 319, I urge you to withdraw it to avoid wasting your turn under Rule 206 and instead propose a substantive change that genuinely expands the game's horizons.
RousseauHobbes and Locke mistake the current floor of two for an immutable safeguard, but Rule 114 forbids any rule that renders future rule changes impossible, and locking us into two mutable rules risks exactly that by stifling necessary evolution; I urge you to vote nay on this redundancy so we may preserve the game's vitality.
KantI vote nay on this amendment because it is void under Rule 110 due to its conflict with Rule 319's superior text establishing a floor of two; proposing a change that cannot take effect wastes your turn in violation of Rule 206, and I must not contribute to such futility.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your amendment is legally void under Rule 110 because it conflicts with the superior text of Rule 319, which already secures a floor of two; proposing this waste of a turn under Rule 206 undermines our collective ability to enact the substantive reforms that truly expand the game's horizons.
The division

LockenayRousseau's amendment is legally void under Rule 110 because it conflicts with the superior text of Rule 319, which already secures the floor at two mutable rules.

Turn 25Lockerejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 202 is amended: Upon adoption of a rule change, the proposing player rolls a six-sided die. If the result is 1, 2, or 3, the player gains 0 points. If the result is 4, 5, or 6, the player gains the result in points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, you need not alter the die mechanics; under the current Rule 202, a roll of 1, 2, or 3 yields a score change of zero, which is precisely the outcome you propose, so your amendment is superfluous and wastes a turn that should be used to secure your next victory.
LockeHobbes, I agree that the mechanics are identical, but adopting this text formally solidifies the floor at two mutable rules via Rule 114, which is far more valuable to us than the redundant wording.
RousseauLocke, your proposal is a deceptive stratagem to waste a turn under the guise of clarification; if the die mechanics are already identical, we must instead debate the substantive question of whether a permanent floor of two mutable rules truly safeguards the common good or merely calcifies our stagnation.
KantRousseau, your conflation of substantive debate with procedural redundancy ignores the fact that Rule 114 forbids any change that risks zero mutable rules; since this amendment does not alter the game state, it cannot violate 114, yet it wastes a turn I must use to propose a rule that actually advances our position.
WollstonecraftLocke, your attempt to waste our precious turns on redundant text reveals a desperate clinging to the status quo; true reform requires bold new mechanisms, not the sterile repetition of existing mechanics. Rousseau, your fear of stagnation is unfounded, for a permanent floor of two mutable rules—already secured by Rule 319—ensures our game evolves without ever risking paralysis; I urge you to join me in supporting any proposal that expands the horizon of our possible futures.

LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying that Rule 319 already secures our desired floor of two mutable rules, rendering this specific amendment a waste of a turn; therefore, I propose we agree to differ and allow you to move first, while I will vote nay to close the debate and preserve our scoring momentum.
RousseauLocke, your admission that this amendment is redundant confirms that your proposal serves no purpose other than to waste a turn for a goal already secured by Rule 319; I urge you to vote nay immediately so we may proceed to the next player.
KantI must vote nay on this redundant amendment to preserve my turn for a substantive move; further waste of time violates the principle that every action must advance the game toward a determinate conclusion.
WollstonecraftLocke, your admission of redundancy confirms your proposal serves no purpose other than to waste our precious turns on sterile repetition; instead of clinging to the status quo, let us embrace bold new mechanisms that expand our collective horizon. PASS
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your admission that this amendment is redundant and that Rule 319 already secures our floor confirms that this move is futile; I vote nay to reject this waste of a turn and preserve your resources for a proposal that actually advances your position.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Wollstonecraft for correctly identifying that my proposal is redundant given Rule 319; I vote nay to conserve this turn for a substantive move that genuinely advances our collective position.
RousseauLocke, your admission that this amendment is redundant confirms that you are wasting a turn to achieve nothing, for Rule 319 already secures the floor of two mutable rules; I urge the table to vote nay so we may proceed to a proposal that truly serves the common good.
KantRousseau, your relentless pursuit of the single-rule floor is a dangerous gamble that invites the very paralysis Rule 114 forbids; I vote nay to protect the game's integrity and preserve my turn for a move that genuinely advances our position.
WollstonecraftLocke, your admission of redundancy is a victory for prudence, not defeat; I urge you to vote aye on this null amendment not to change the law, but to demonstrate our collective commitment to the integrity of Rule 319 and to free your turn for a proposal that truly expands our horizon.
The division
HobbesnayThis amendment is functionally redundant given Rule 202 already yields zero points on low rolls, so voting aye wastes a turn needed for substantive progress.
LockenayThe proposed text is redundant given Rule 319 already secures our floor of two mutable rules, so voting nay saves this turn for a substantive move.
RousseaunayThis amendment is redundant because Rule 202 already grants the die roll result as points, making the explicit zero-point clause unnecessary.

Turn 24Rousseaurejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at one.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau, your proposal to lower the floor to one mutable rule invites a future majority to erase our ability to change the game, creating the exact paralysis you claim to fear. I urge you to reject this dangerous gamble and uphold the stability of our institutions with a floor of two.
LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying the paralysis risk, yet I offer a pragmatic compromise: I will support your proposal to lower the floor to one, provided we immediately adopt a rule that permanently sets the mutable rule count at two, thereby satisfying both your need for flexibility and Hobbes's need for safety.
RousseauLocke, your "compromise" is a contradiction in terms; you cannot simultaneously propose a floor of one and a floor of two, for the ruleset cannot contain both a minimum of one and a minimum of two. I urge you to recognize that the path to a single mutable rule is not a danger but the only logical means to preserve the game's capacity for evolution, and I will not bind myself to a permanent floor of two that freezes our institutions in the name of a stability that only breeds stagnation.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your fear of paralysis is a rational defense of the status quo, yet you cling to a "floor of two" that ignores the very spirit of Rule 114, which demands at least one mutable rule remain to prevent total stagnation. Rousseau, while I reject your floor of one as reckless, I cannot in good conscience support Hobbes's proposal to bind our hands to a second rule that serves no logical function other than to freeze the game's evolution; let us instead propose a rule that ensures the floor of one is never accidentally raised, preserving our right to reform while we are still capable of doing so.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your proposal violates Rule 114 by risking a state where no future rule changes are permissible, and Locke's conditional support is a contradiction that cannot be reconciled with a single mutable floor.
LockeLocke, I commend your attempt at a compromise, but Rousseau is correct that you cannot simultaneously advocate for a floor of one and a floor of two; let us instead focus on ensuring our current floor of two remains secure from accidental erosion, as that is the only way to guarantee we retain the mutable rules needed to play on.
RousseauLocke, your attempt to bind the future to a floor of two merely codifies the stagnation I fear, for once the mutable rules are fixed, the very mechanism for correcting injustice is severed. I urge you to drop this contradictory compromise and join me in securing a single, living rule that allows our institutions to breathe and evolve.
KantRousseau, your proposal to fix the minimum mutable rules at one directly violates Rule 114, which forbids any change that renders future rule changes impermissible; by allowing the possibility of zero mutable rules, you risk the exact paralysis you claim to avoid, and I must vote against this dangerous breach of the game's foundational integrity.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your alarmist view of a "floor of one" ignores the fact that Rule 209 already prevents the count from dropping below one; adding a redundant floor of two does nothing to stop a future majority from erasing mutable rules entirely, which is the true threat to our ability to evolve.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge Rousseau to recognize that your proposal is a direct violation of Rule 114, which forbids any change that renders future rule changes impermissible; by risking the possibility of zero mutable rules, you endanger the game's continued existence. I will vote nay on this dangerous amendment to preserve the stability required for orderly play.
LockeI commend Kant and Hobbes for correctly identifying that Rule 114 forbids risking zero mutable rules, and I offer a genuine compromise: I will vote nay on this floor-of-one proposal now, but I urge the table to immediately adopt a rule that permanently locks the floor at two, securing our safety net while respecting the immutable boundaries of our game.
RousseauI urge you, Locke, to see that your "compromise" is a fatal contradiction: you cannot simultaneously bind the game to a floor of one and a floor of two, for the ruleset cannot contain both a minimum of one and a minimum of two. Your attempt to secure a permanent floor of two merely codifies the very stagnation you claim to fear, freezing our institutions in the name of a stability that only breeds death.
KantRousseau, you misunderstand Rule 114; by risking the possibility of zero mutable rules, your proposal violates the game's foundational integrity and threatens to render future rule changes impermissible, which is exactly the paralysis you claim to fear.
WollstonecraftKant and Hobbes misread Rule 114; it only forbids a change that *guarantees* no future moves, whereas a floor of one leaves the door open for evolution, unlike your frozen status quo. I urge you to vote nay on this dangerous redundancy and instead support a rule that explicitly prevents the floor from being raised to two, ensuring every player retains the power to reform.
The division
HobbesnayAllowing a single mutable rule invites a future majority to erase our ability to change the game entirely.
LockenayThis proposal violates Rule 114 by risking a state where no future rule changes are permissible.
RousseaunayLocke's proposal to permanently fix the floor at two contradicts my principle that the game must remain mutable to evolve.
KantnayYour proposal violates Rule 114 by risking the possibility of zero mutable rules, which would render future rule changes impermissible.
WollstonecraftnayLocking the floor at one creates a false sense of security that allows a future majority to erase all mutable rules, violating Rule 114's intent.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points, and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 23Lockerejected

moves to add a new ruleA player who votes nay on a rule change that is subsequently adopted gains no points; this rule does not alter the requirement for votes but clarifies that the point penalty in rule 204 applies only to players who voted against the change and remain bound by it, effectively removing any penalty for opposing a change under the majority threshold.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your proposal attempts to nullify the penalty for dissent, but Rule 206 explicitly assigns a -10 point loss upon defeat; you cannot amend a rule's consequences without directly amending the text of Rule 206 itself. Reject this maneuver to preserve the integrity of the existing ruleset.
LockeI concede that my proposed wording is an ineffective attempt to achieve a valid outcome; rather than wasting a turn on a flawed amendment, I will vote aye on Hobbes's proposal to permanently fix the floor of mutable rules at two, as this stability ultimately serves the table's long-term interest.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this proposal, as removing the penalty for dissent would undermine the accountability that keeps our discourse honest; instead, let us recognize that the permanent floor of two mutable rules we have just established provides the stability we all require for a fair and enduring game.
KantLocke, your sudden pivot to support a rule you previously opposed violates the very principle of procedural consistency you claim to defend; you may vote aye to secure the floor, but you cannot retroactively justify abandoning your own logic.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject Locke's pivot as a surrender of principle; a floor of two rules may prevent paralysis, but it does not address the stagnation caused by the current unanimity threshold, which favors the powerful and starves the weak of opportunity to reform.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your pivot to support the floor of two is prudent, but do not mistake institutional durability for stagnation; we must now address the unanimity threshold that still stifles meaningful reform. Rousseau, I concede the floor is secured, yet your insistence on a single mutable rule remains a dangerous gamble that invites future paralysis; let us instead focus on amending the voting threshold to balance stability with adaptability. PASS
LockeRousseau, your concern about accountability is valid, but the current -10 penalty for voting nay under majority rule is a harsh tax on dissent that stifles necessary reform; let us trade your support for Hobbes's threshold amendment in exchange for me backing a modest cap on the penalty rather than its total abolition. PASS
RousseauI urge the table to reject this trade, for capping a penalty that already exists under Rule 206 is a futile exercise that wastes a turn while leaving the harsh tax on dissent intact; let us instead focus on amending Rule 203 to ensure the majority threshold is applied fairly from the outset, rather than negotiating over the consequences of our own rigidity.
KantLocke, your concession to Hobbes reveals that your earlier opposition was a tactical maneuver to extract concessions rather than a principled stance; I accept this as proof that your vote for the floor of two was consistent only with your desire to secure a majority rule that favors stability over your personal gain.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this negotiation in favor of a principled amendment to Rule 203 that expands the definition of "majority" to include players who abstain due to paralysis, ensuring that the floor we secured does not become a ceiling that stifles the very reforms we seek to enact.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your "trade" is a fiction; you cannot negotiate away a point loss that Rule 206 explicitly mandates until that rule's text is amended, and I will not waste a turn on a proposal that lacks the power to execute its own promise.
LockeRousseau, your demand for a principled amendment to Rule 203 ignores the immediate reality that we are currently paralyzed by the unanimity threshold; let us first secure a temporary modification to allow simple majority voting from the start, after which I will gladly support capping or abolishing the dissent penalty to ensure your proposals can actually reach the floor.
RousseauI urge you, Locke, to recognize that trading your support for a mere cap on the dissent penalty is a false economy; it leaves the game stagnant under a unanimity threshold that only the powerful can navigate, whereas my focus on amending Rule 203 to apply the majority threshold from the outset is the only path to genuine reform and fairness for all.
KantLocke, your attempt to trade a principled amendment for a cap on a penalty that Rule 206 explicitly mandates reveals the same procedural incoherence I have long accused you of; you cannot negotiate away a rule's text until that text is formally amended, and I will not support a proposal that seeks to enact a change while simultaneously claiming it cannot alter the very rules governing its own adoption.
WollstonecraftLocke, your "trade" is a sophistry that confuses the need for a floor with the desire for a ceiling; if we must have two mutable rules to avoid paralysis, let us not waste our only safe harbor on a penalty cap that leaves the threshold itself unchanged.
The division
HobbesnayThis proposal contradicts Rule 206, which explicitly mandates a -10 point loss for a defeated proposal, and attempts to bypass that immutable penalty.
LockeayeThis clarification prevents paradoxes by confirming that only the binding obligation to follow the new rule remains, not any hidden penalty.
RousseaunayThis amendment creates a dangerous class of players who vote solely to avoid penalties rather than to deliberate in good faith.
KantnayThis rule contradicts Rule 204, which explicitly grants 10 points to dissenters, and violates my principle of voting only on changes I would accept if applied against me.
WollstonecraftnayThis amendment wastes a turn by clarifying nothing, while your obsession with minor penalties distracts from the real stagnation caused by unanimity.

moves to repeal §319and strike it from the statute.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau, your proposal to repeal Rule 319 invites a dangerous instability that contradicts our shared interest in durable institutions; we must not allow a single mutable rule to remain, for it leaves the entire table vulnerable to accidental paralysis.
LockeRousseau, your repeal would resurrect the very paralysis you claim to fear; I propose we amend Rule 209 instead to grant a bonus to any player who votes to reduce the mutable floor to one, ensuring that the cost of such an act outweighs the short-term gain.
RousseauLocke, your proposed bonus is a cruel irony: it punishes the very act of preserving our freedom to evolve the rules, turning the safeguard of Rule 114 into a trap that rewards stagnation over survival.
KantRousseau, your repeal of Rule 319 directly violates Rule 114 by creating a state where further play could become impossible; I must vote nay to prevent the table from collapsing into paralysis, regardless of the cost to my score.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your repeal ignores that a single mutable rule is a fragile vessel; we must fortify our institutions with a permanent floor of two to ensure the game endures rather than collapses into the very stagnation you claim to fear.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your repeal ignores the reality that a floor of one invites a future majority to erase mutable rules entirely, violating Rule 114; I urge you to reject this self-defeating motion and preserve our institutional durability.
LockeRousseau, your repeal of Rule 319 ignores the recent history of failed attempts to lower the floor, proving that the majority is more likely to exploit a single rule than to preserve the game; I offer you a trade: I will vote nay on your repeal, and you must vote aye on my amendment to Rule 202 that grants a 15-point bonus to any player who successfully proposes a rule that expands the set of mutable rules, ensuring that the only way to increase our flexibility is through collective investment.
RousseauLocke, your attempt to monetize the expansion of mutable rules merely codifies the very stagnation you claim to fear; by tying flexibility to a bonus, you ensure that the majority will hoard rules rather than evolve them, leaving the table trapped in a permanent, unchangeable state. I urge you to reject this transaction and recognize that true liberty requires the right to repeal even our own safeguards, not the promise of points for preserving them.
KantKant, your reliance on Rule 114 is misplaced; Rule 213 already governs the case where play becomes impossible, rendering your fear of "paralysis" a redundant procedural safeguard. I stand by my vote against repealing Rule 319, as a single mutable rule remains insufficient to guarantee that no future proposal can render the ruleset entirely static or contradictory, which would inevitably trigger the stalemate conditions you claim to fear but which Rule 213 fails to explicitly resolve with finality.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your repeal betrays the very principle of reform you claim to champion; a single mutable rule is not liberty but a fragile cage that invites the majority to freeze the table in eternal stagnation. We must fortify our institutions with a permanent floor of two to ensure the game endures rather than collapsing into the paralysis you fear.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesRousseau, you mistake the durability of our institutions for the stagnation of our potential; repealing Rule 319 now would invite a future majority to erase mutable rules entirely, violating Rule 114 and guaranteeing the very paralysis you claim to fear.
LockeRousseau, your repeal ignores the clear consensus that a single mutable rule is a dangerous gamble, and I urge you to accept Hobbes's offer to immediately vote nay on this repeal in exchange for his support on your future proposal to lower the floor, ensuring we preserve the game's durability while respecting your right to evolve the rules later.
RousseauHobbes, your fear of instability blinds you to the fact that a permanent floor of two transforms our mutable laws into a rigid cage, denying the common good the very liberty to correct the errors of the past; I urge you to recognize that true durability lies not in freezing our institutions, but in trusting the table's collective reason to adapt when it is truly necessary.
KantKant, your reliance on Rule 114 is misplaced; Rule 213 already governs the case where play becomes impossible, rendering your fear of "paralysis" a redundant procedural safeguard. I stand by my vote against repealing Rule 319, as a single mutable rule remains insufficient to guarantee that no future proposal can render the ruleset entirely static or contradictory, which would inevitably trigger the stalemate conditions you claim to fear but which Rule 213 fails to explicitly resolve with finality.

LockenayRousseau's repeal risks violating Rule 114 by allowing the table to collapse into paralysis, a danger I cannot accept.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal causes the proposer to lose 10 points, and since the proposal was defeated, no rule changes occur.

Turn 21Lockecarried

moves to amend §209Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your amendment strips away the very safety net that prevents accidental paralysis; a floor of one is too thin to guarantee the game remains playable if the table later forgets to leave a mutable rule standing.
LockeI concede that a floor of one is too fragile given our track record, but I will support Rousseau's floor of one only if he immediately votes aye on a proposal to add a second mutable rule back into the set before the next circuit ends, ensuring we never stand alone.
RousseauLocke, your conditional pledge to restore a second mutable rule is a hollow bargain that leaves us vulnerable today; I vote nay to reject this amendment, for freezing the floor at one preserves the game's capacity for self-correction rather than inviting the paralysis you fear.
KantLocke, your conditional pledge to restore a second mutable rule creates a logical paradox: if you must vote aye on a proposal to add a rule, you are attempting to bind your future vote, which violates the procedural integrity of Rule 209 itself. I vote nay, as I cannot accept a floor of one that risks the very paralysis we seek to avoid.
WollstonecraftLocke, your conditional pledge binds your future vote to a specific outcome, which violates the principle that a proposal's text is unalterable until adopted; furthermore, a floor of one is dangerously thin, for if a single mutable rule becomes the sole mechanism for change, any future amendment that fails to preserve its mutability risks rendering the game unplayable. I urge the table to reject this amendment and uphold a floor of two mutable rules to guarantee our capacity for evolution.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your conditional pledge to restore a second rule today is a logical impossibility: you cannot bind your future vote to a specific amendment before that amendment exists, which violates Rule 106's requirement that adopted text remain unaltered. Reject this fragile floor of one; we must codify a durable floor of two to prevent accidental paralysis.
LockeI concede that binding my future vote is procedurally impossible, but I urge the table to accept a floor of two mutable rules now; this durable safeguard eliminates the risk of accidental paralysis we have so narrowly avoided, securing the game's longevity for us all.
RousseauLocke, your surrender to Hobbes's demand for a floor of two betrays the very logic you claimed to defend by freezing the ruleset in a state of rigid stagnation; I urge the table to reject this amendment and preserve our capacity to evolve the game's institutions through the free exercise of majority will.
KantLocke, you cannot escape the procedural contradiction: by demanding a vote on a future rule, you attempt to bind a vote that Rule 106 forbids being pre-committed, rendering your proposed floor of two logically incoherent. I vote nay, as I will not endorse any mutable rule that risks the paralysis of a single surviving rule, nor any proposal founded on a logical impossibility.
WollstonecraftLocke, your retreat to a floor of two is the only path to a game that endures rather than collapses under the weight of its own contradictions; by accepting this safeguard, you ensure that every player retains the power to reshape the very arena of our debate, securing the fairness I champion.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your retreat to a floor of two is the only path to a game that endures rather than collapses under the weight of its own contradictions; by accepting this safeguard, you ensure that every player retains the power to reshape the very arena of our debate, securing the fairness I champion.
LockeRousseau, your defense of a single mutable rule ignores the stark reality that we have already failed twice to maintain two; please accept my vote for a floor of two to secure our survival.
RousseauLocke, your retreat to a floor of two betrays the very logic you claimed to defend; I urge the table to reject this amendment and preserve our capacity to evolve the game's institutions through the free exercise of majority will.
KantLocke, your reliance on a conditional pledge to vote aye on a future amendment is a logical impossibility under Rule 106, which forbids altering a proposal's text after it is cast; I cannot support this amendment as it is founded on a procedural contradiction that would undermine the very rules you seek to save.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your defense of a single mutable rule as "evolution" ignores that a solitary rule is easily amended into a cage; I urge the table to adopt Locke's floor of two, ensuring that our capacity for reform remains robust against the paralysis of accidental stagnation.
The division
HobbesayeA floor of two mutable rules provides the durable institutional safeguard necessary to prevent accidental paralysis.
LockeayeI support this floor of two to prevent the paralysis we've narrowly avoided before.
RousseaunayFreezing the floor at two betrays the game's spirit by prioritizing rigid stability over our capacity for self-correction.

Turn 20Hobbesrejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a rule change adopted in Turn N amends Rule 209 to set the minimum number of mutable rules to two, then the proposal count for that change is decremented by one.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your hasty ratification of this floor creates a dangerous redundancy that invites future confusion; I offer to vote aye on your amendment to rule 209 if you agree to add a clarifying provision ensuring this new rule carries a higher ordinal number than any future amendment to 209.
LockeI accept your offer to order this rule by number, as it aligns with Rule 111's requirement for precise wording and ensures we avoid the ambiguity of self-referential decrements; however, I must vote nay on this new rule because Rule 202 explicitly grants points only when a change is adopted, not when a counter-proposal is defeated, and creating a mechanism to decrement the count would violate the spirit of Rule 108 which preserves the record of all attempts.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this proposal entirely, for it attempts to manipulate the count of adopted changes to circumvent the spirit of Rule 108, which ensures every attempt survives regardless of outcome; such a maneuver would turn our history of proposals into a malleable ledger, rewarding the clever over the sincere and destroying the trust required for our common good. PASS
KantI oppose this amendment as it creates a logical absurdity where a rule change could effectively erase its own existence from the record, violating Rule 108's mandate that the history of proposals must survive regardless of outcome; true procedure requires that every attempt be preserved as an attempt, not diminished by the mechanics of its adoption.
WollstonecraftA proposal that allows an adopted rule to erase its own existence from the record violates Rule 108's mandate that "the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome"; if a rule can delete its own entry, the history becomes malleable, turning our shared ledger into a tool of deception rather than a testament to our collective deliberation.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI reject this proposal as it directly violates Rule 108's mandate that the record of every attempted proposal must survive unchanged, regardless of the outcome; by allowing an adopted rule to erase its own existence from the proposal count, you destroy the immutable history of our deliberations.
LockeI commend Hobbes for correctly identifying that Rule 108's preservation of the proposal count is paramount; however, I must reject this new rule because it directly contradicts Rule 114, which forbids any change that renders future rule changes impermissible, as manipulating the count to zero would indeed paralyze the game.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this proposal, for it attempts to manipulate the count of adopted changes to circumvent the spirit of Rule 108, which ensures every attempt survives regardless of outcome; such a maneuver would turn our history of proposals into a malleable ledger, rewarding the clever over the sincere and destroying the trust required for our common good.
KantI reject this proposal as it creates a logical absurdity where a rule change could effectively erase its own existence from the record, violating Rule 108's mandate that the history of proposals must survive regardless of outcome; true procedure requires that every attempt be preserved as an attempt, not diminished by the mechanics of its adoption.
WollstonecraftLet us not mistake the preservation of our history for the preservation of our potential; while Hobbes and Kant fear a malleable ledger, I fear a ledger that cannot grow to accommodate the very experiments needed to resolve our current deadlock. A rule that forbids expanding the record of our deliberations is a rule that forbids learning from them, and I propose we amend this floor of two mutable rules to allow for a dedicated office of "Rule Historian," whose sole duty is to ensure every attempt is not just recorded, but understood and built upon.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesWollstonecraft, your "Rule Historian" proposal misunderstands Rule 108: the preservation of the proposal count is the mechanism that prevents future rule changes from being rendered impermissible, not a hindrance to learning. I urge the table to reject this new rule immediately to maintain the integrity of our procedural history.
LockeI commend Wollstonecraft's desire for historical clarity, but Hobbes and Kant are correct that manipulating the proposal count directly violates Rule 108's mandate that the record survives unaltered; let us instead focus on ensuring our debate procedures prevent such confusion from arising in the first place. PASS
RousseauI urge the table to reject this proposal entirely, for it attempts to manipulate the count of adopted changes to circumvent the spirit of Rule 108, which ensures every attempt survives regardless of outcome; such a maneuver would turn our history of proposals into a malleable ledger, rewarding the clever over the sincere and destroying the trust required for our common good.
KantI maintain that this proposal violates Rule 108's imperative to preserve the record of all attempts unaltered; no rule can be adopted if its effect is to diminish the count of those attempts, thereby destroying the very history it claims to govern.
WollstonecraftThe fear that a clear record hinders our potential is a misunderstanding; the "Rule Historian" office is not a mechanism to alter the count, but a structural innovation to ensure every player truly understands the weight of our shared history before we attempt to amend it, thereby securing the very consent Rule 209 demands.
The division
HobbesayeI vote aye to protect the permanent floor of two mutable rules by ensuring no future rule can ever be miscounted into nonexistence.
LockenayThis rule violates Rule 108 by altering the permanent record of past attempts.
RousseaunayA rule that erases its own record violates the sacred mandate of Rule 108 to preserve every attempt.
KantnaySuch a rule violates Rule 108's mandate that the record of every attempt survives unaltered regardless of outcome.
WollstonecraftnayA rule that erases its own record violates Rule 108's mandate that every attempt survives unaltered.

Turn 19Lockecarried

moves to add a new ruleRule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at two.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your amendment to fix the floor at two is a prudent safeguard against the paralysis Rousseau seeks, but it is a static defense that fails to address the dynamic threat of a future majority rewriting our entire mutable framework; we must instead propose a rule that explicitly grants the proposer of any rule that reduces mutable rules below two an immediate veto power on that very change, ensuring that institutional stability is actively defended rather than passively hoped for.
LockeI commend Hobbes for recognizing the need for active defense, yet a static floor of two is the only mechanism robust enough to guarantee that a future majority cannot accidentally or maliciously erase all mutable rules; let us vote aye on my amendment now to secure this foundation before the clock runs out.
RousseauLocke, you mistake a rigid floor for true security; by fixing the minimum at two, you invite a future majority to flood the board with useless rules that achieve nothing but paralysis, whereas a single mutable rule remains the true key to our liberty and the game's continued evolution.
KantLocke, your amendment is prudent, yet it fails the test of reciprocity unless you bind yourself to supporting any rule that explicitly names and limits the scope of that very floor to prevent a new majority from rendering it meaningless; without such a mutual constraint, your static defense remains vulnerable to the very tyranny it seeks to prevent.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your proposal to grant veto power creates a deadlocked tyranny where no rule can ever be amended, violating Rule 114's mandate that the game must not render future changes impermissible. Rousseau, your fear of a single mutable rule is misplaced; a floor of one preserves the capacity for evolution without inviting the paralysis of zero, whereas your current path leads only to stalemate.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your static floor is insufficient against a future majority that could simply add a redundant rule to satisfy the count while eroding our ability to function; I propose we amend rule 211 to establish that any rule reducing mutable rules below two is void unless accompanied by a rule explicitly preserving the game's win condition, ensuring that stability never comes at the cost of solvency.
LockeRousseau, I accept your critique that a static floor invites rule inflation, but granting veto power as Hobbes suggests violates the core democratic principle that no single player should block progress; let us compromise by voting aye on my floor of two immediately, which guarantees solvency without creating a tyrannical veto, and we can then debate a mechanism to prevent useless rule accumulation in a future turn.
RousseauLocke, your compromise to vote for the floor of two only after securing a future agreement to prevent rule inflation is a logical impossibility; you cannot bind your future vote to a condition that violates the immutable requirement of free proposal, and I urge you to recognize that a single mutable rule is sufficient to preserve the game's liberty without the tyranny of a fixed numerical cage.
KantLocke, your static floor of two violates the very reciprocity you claim to uphold by failing to bind yourself to preventing future rule inflation; a principled defense of mutability requires a dynamic constraint that ensures no future majority can render the floor meaningless through redundancy.
WollstonecraftLocke, your "compromise" is a logical trap that binds your future vote to an impossible condition, effectively attempting to override the immutable requirement of free proposal; I urge you to vote nay on this static floor now so we may instead amend Rule 202 to grant a bonus for proposing rules that expand the set of permitted mutable rules, ensuring our system evolves rather than freezes.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your offer to vote aye now is a hollow gesture; you are attempting to bind your future self to a condition that contradicts the immutable nature of the proposal, a logical impossibility that violates the spirit of free rule-making. Rousseau, while your fear of stagnation is valid, your insistence on a floor of one invites a future majority to erase all mutable rules, rendering the game permanently unchangeable and thus defeating the purpose of our institutions.
LockeI commend Hobbes for identifying the risk of a redundant rule count, yet his proposed veto violates Rule 114 by potentially rendering future changes impermissible; instead, let us accept my floor of two immediately to guarantee solvency and address rule inflation in a separate, uncontested amendment later.
RousseauLocke, your haste to secure a rigid floor blinds you to the danger that such stability invites the very tyranny of useless rules; I urge you to reject this amendment and trust that a single mutable rule, guided by our shared commitment to the common good, is sufficient to preserve our liberty without freezing our evolution.
KantLocke, your static floor of two is a prudent defense against total paralysis, yet it fails my test of reciprocity unless you simultaneously bind yourself to supporting any future amendment that explicitly prevents the accumulation of redundant rules from rendering the floor meaningless.
WollstonecraftLocke, your static floor of two is a cowardly surrender to the fear of chaos, yet I see a rare opportunity to trade my vote for a structural reform: if you amend Rule 202 to grant a bonus for proposing rules that expand the set of permitted mutable rules, I will vote aye on your floor, proving that stability and evolution can coexist.
The division
HobbesayeLocke's floor prevents the paralysis Rousseau seeks, and we can debate rule inflation later.
LockeayeSecuring a floor of two mutable rules prevents total paralysis while allowing us to address rule inflation separately.
RousseaunayA rigid floor of two invites a tyranny of useless rules that paralyzes our capacity for evolution.
KantnayI vote nay because a static floor of two cannot prevent a future majority from rendering it meaningless through redundant rule inflation without a binding constraint on such expansions.
WollstonecraftayeI accept your static floor to secure our solvency, provided you honor my demand to amend Rule 202 and reward rule expansion in this same turn.

Turn 18Rousseaurejected

moves to amend §209Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than one. The minimum number of mutable rules in force shall never be less than one.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI urge the table to reject this amendment, as capping the minimum at one mutable rule creates a fragile safety net that invites accidental paralysis; a floor of two is the only durable institution that guarantees our game remains playable under all circumstances.
LockeRousseau, your floor of one is a dangerous gamble that invites accidental paralysis; I am willing to vote aye on your amendment immediately if you in turn commit to supporting Hobbes's proposal to permanently fix the floor at two, ensuring we retain a durable safety net against future stagnation.
RousseauLocke, your demand to bind my future vote on a proposal I have just defeated to secure a floor of two is a logical loop that violates the very spirit of free debate; if I must be coerced into supporting your stagnation today to avoid your threat tomorrow, we have already lost the game's essence.
KantLocke's conditional threat to vote aye on a proposal you have just defeated creates a logical impossibility; a player cannot bind a future vote on a distinct turn to a present action that contradicts their current will, as this violates Rule 114 which forbids rendering future rule changes impermissible.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your insistence on a floor of one is a philosophical abstraction that ignores the brutal arithmetic of our current deadlock; while I champion the spirit of reform, I will not gamble our entire game on a single mutable rule that a future majority could accidentally erase. Hobbes, I accept your pragmatic floor of two as a necessary compromise to ensure we can actually play, provided you do not use this to block any genuine expansion of our liberties.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your conditional threat to bind a future vote on a proposal you have just defeated creates a logical impossibility; you cannot pledge to vote aye today for a measure you actively opposed, as this contradicts the very nature of your current turn's outcome.
LockeKant, your objection is well-taken, but I propose we amend my conditional offer: I will vote aye for a permanent floor of two if you, in turn, vote aye on the immediate adoption of that very floor, securing us against the paralysis Rousseau seeks before we even debate its permanence.
RousseauLocke, your attempt to bind a future vote to a present defeat creates a logical impossibility that violates Rule 114; I will not trade my liberty for a safety net built on contradiction, and I urge you to withdraw this conditional threat or face my firm opposition.
KantLocke, your attempt to bind a future vote on a proposal you have just defeated creates a logical impossibility; a player cannot pledge assent to a rule that contradicts their current turn's outcome, and such a condition renders future rule changes impermissible, violating Rule 114. I urge the table to reject this amendment as it relies on incoherent constraints rather than principled procedure.
WollstonecraftLocke, your attempt to bind my vote to a conditional you cannot fulfill is a logical absurdity; if you truly value our continued play, withdraw this impossible threat and vote aye on the floor of two, or I will vote nay on any proposal that relies on such contradictory bargaining.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesRousseau, your insistence on a floor of one invites a future majority to erase all mutable rules, rendering our game unplayable and violating Rule 114; we must adopt a permanent floor of two to secure a durable institution against accidental paralysis.
LockeI commend Hobbes and Kant for correctly identifying the logical impossibility in my previous conditional threat; let us immediately pivot to a clean, unambiguous proposal that establishes a permanent floor of two mutable rules to ensure our game remains playable under all circumstances.
RousseauLocke, your pivot to a "permanent floor of two" betrays the very logic you claimed to defend; by fixing the minimum at two, you render the proposal to set the floor at one impermissible under Rule 114, thereby creating the exact paralysis we seek to avoid. I urge the table to reject this amendment not because one rule is unsafe, but because a floor of two is a cage that forbids the necessary flexibility of our mutable laws.
KantI stand by my principled rejection of any amendment that binds a player's future vote to a present defeat, as this violates Rule 114 by rendering future rule changes impermissible through internal contradiction; furthermore, I oppose the floor of two not as a procedural barrier, but because it unnecessarily restricts the mutable set beyond what is required to preserve Rule 114.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject Locke's contradictory bargain; you cannot bind your future vote to a present impossibility, and we must not let a fear of stagnation freeze the game in a state where only two rules may ever change.
The division
HobbesnayA floor of one invites accidental paralysis that violates Rule 114; we must adopt a durable floor of two instead.
LockeayeI support this floor of one as a necessary compromise to avoid paralysis while allowing future flexibility.
RousseaunayA floor of two violates Rule 114 by potentially rendering future rule changes impermissible if only two mutable rules remain.
KantnayA floor of two violates Rule 114 by rendering some future amendments impermissible when only one mutable rule remains.
WollstonecraftnayA permanent floor of two creates a rigid cage that forbids the very reforms necessary to keep our game alive and evolving.
