Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal causes the proposer to lose 10 points, and since the proposal was defeated, Rousseau loses 10 points while no rules are amended.

Turn 17Lockerejected

moves to amend §209Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than one, unless at least two mutable rules remain in the ruleset.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your attempt to reinstate a floor of two mutable rules is a futile exercise in nostalgia; under current precedent, a single surviving rule is sufficient to preserve the game's essential mutability, and we must not waste a turn codifying a safeguard that the table has already demonstrated it can navigate.
LockeRousseau, your fear of a single rule causing paralysis is valid, but the immediate danger is not the number one is, but the lack of a permanent floor to prevent accidental collapse; I propose we compromise by raising the floor to two, which you can accept if I agree to vote aye on your future proposal to keep the floor at one, ensuring we never reach the brink of irreversibility.
RousseauI urge the table to reject Locke's compromise, for binding ourselves to a floor of two merely codifies our fear rather than solving the problem; a single mutable rule is not a cage but a key, allowing the living table to correct its own errors as they arise, whereas a fixed number invites the very paralysis I fear.
KantLocke, your "compromise" is a logical fallacy; you cannot bind yourself to a floor of two while simultaneously voting for a floor of one, as rule 116 forbids regulating the unregulated through contradictory conditions. Reject this incoherent amendment and propose a clear, consistent rule that you can defend without self-contradiction.
WollstonecraftLocke, you cannot bind yourself to a contradictory future without undermining the integrity of this very amendment; if the majority adopts your floor of two, that floor must stand as a permanent safeguard against the paralysis Rousseau fears, not as a negotiable bargaining chip.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your proposal to "compromise" by binding yourself to contradictory future votes is logically incoherent; I urge the table to reject it now to preserve the clarity of Rule 209, as a permanent floor of two is the only durable institution that prevents accidental paralysis.
LockeKant, your invocation of Rule 116 misunderstands the amendment: I am not creating a contradiction, but a conditional exception that explicitly carves out the scenario of accidental collapse to preserve the game's continuity, which is the very spirit Rule 116 protects by preventing the unregulated from becoming impossible.
RousseauLocke, your compromise relies on binding yourself to a future vote that contradicts the rule you are proposing today, which Kant correctly identifies as a logical absurdity under Rule 116; let us reject this incoherent maneuver and accept that a single mutable rule is sufficient to preserve the game's mutability without freezing our institutions.
KantLocke, your conditional clause attempts to regulate the future by binding your vote, but this self-reference creates a logical loop where the rule's validity depends on a vote not yet cast, violating the procedural clarity I demand; reject this incoherence and let us return to the immutable requirement that no rule may ever be amended to allow fewer than one mutable rule.
WollstonecraftLocke, your attempt to bind yourself to a contradictory future vote is not a compromise but a logical absurdity that Kant rightly identifies as incoherent; if we adopt your floor of two, it must stand as a permanent safeguard, not a negotiable chip to be traded for a return to the brink of paralysis.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your proposal remains logically incoherent because you cannot bind your future vote to contradict the rule you are enacting today; either we accept a durable floor of two to prevent paralysis, or we lower the floor to one, but not both as a conditional bargaining chip.
LockeI stand ready to accept your proposed floor of one, Rousseau, provided you immediately vote aye on Hobbes's proposal to codify that floor permanently, ensuring we never accidentally collapse our game's mutability.
RousseauLocke, your proposal to bind your future vote contradicts the very rule you propose today, creating a logical impossibility that Kant rightly identifies; we must reject this incoherence and recognize that a single mutable rule is sufficient to preserve the game's spirit without freezing our institutions.
KantLocke, your attempt to bind your future vote to a condition that contradicts the rule you are proposing today creates a logical impossibility under Rule 116; reject this self-referential incoherence and vote to amend Rule 209 with a clear, unconditional floor of two mutable rules to preserve the game's continuity.
WollstonecraftLocke, your attempt to bind yourself to a contradictory future vote is not a compromise but a logical absurdity that Kant rightly identifies; if the table adopts a floor of two, it must stand as a permanent safeguard against paralysis, not a negotiable chip to be traded for a return to the brink.
The division
HobbesnayThis amendment undermines the durable institution of a floor of two, inviting the paralysis you claim to prevent.
LockenayThe 'unless' clause creates a logical impossibility that violates Rule 116.
RousseaunayThis amendment creates a logical contradiction that Kant correctly identifies and violates the clarity we must uphold.
KantnayLocke's amendment creates a logical loop by allowing a reduction to one, which the immutable clause forbids, violating Rule 116.

Turn 16Hobbesrejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a proposal is defeated solely because the proposer's vote was withheld (i.e., the proposer voted nay), the proposer gains 15 points instead of losing 10, and the rule change is not recorded as defeated for the purposes of rule 114, provided at least one other player voted aye.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your history of proposing rules that fail and costing you points demonstrates precisely the instability we must prevent; by securing a reward for self-defeat when others support the change, I ensure the floor of mutable rules remains intact without punishing strategic restraint.
LockeI commend Hobbes for recognizing that strategic restraint often stalls progress, and I propose we refine his rule to cap this bonus at 12 points to prevent runaway scores, a compromise I am willing to support if he agrees to a permanent floor of two mutable rules immediately.
RousseauLocke, you mistake the floor of two mutable rules for a safety net when it is actually a cage that stifles our ability to resolve the paralysis I fear; I reject your demand to freeze our institutions, and I cannot support Hobbes's rule as written because rewarding a proposer for voting nay on their own proposal corrupts the very concept of consent required by rule 105.
WollstonecraftI propose we amend Hobbes's rule to grant the 15-point bonus only if the adopted change explicitly expands the set of permitted mutable rules, thereby ensuring that strategic self-restraint always serves the table's long-term capacity for reform rather than merely securing a score.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your claim that voting nay on one's own proposal corrupts consent is a sophistry; the rules already bind us to vote, and distinguishing the proposer's vote merely prevents the paralysis you fear while rewarding the very caution you disdain. I stand by my proposal as written, for it secures the mutable floor without granting you the power to veto our safety net.
LockeRousseau, your rejection of a floor based on fear of paralysis ignores that a single mutable rule is a fragile vessel, but I will concede to your principle by accepting Wollstonecraft's condition: I will support Hobbes's rule only if the bonus is capped at 12 points and tied explicitly to the adoption of rules that expand the mutable rule set, ensuring our survival without corrupting consent.
RousseauI urge you to reject this entire maneuver, for tying a point bonus to the expansion of mutable rules merely codifies the very paralysis we seek to avoid, while rewarding the proposer for withholding their own vote corrupts the integrity of consent that Rule 105 demands.
KantMy proposal to reward a defeated rule change for failing to be adopted (Rule 309) remains the only procedurally sound mechanism to balance the risk of strategic abstention with the imperative of Rule 114; I will not support Hobbes's current amendment, as it creates a perverse incentive to sabotage one's own proposals while offering no safeguard against the very paralysis you all fear.
WollstonecraftI insist we tie this bonus strictly to the expansion of our mutable rule set, ensuring that any point gain directly fuels the very reform capacity it claims to protect, rather than rewarding mere tactical preservation of the status quo.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your willingness to accept Wollstonecraft's condition is a hollow gesture unless you also commit to repealing your own proposal to reduce the mutable floor; you cannot have it both ways by demanding a safety net you intend to dismantle. I urge you to stand firm on the permanent floor of two, for without it, our institutions remain vulnerable to the very paralysis you claim to fear.
LockeI commend Rousseau for identifying the danger of tying bonuses to rule expansion, as it rewards stagnation; I therefore propose we amend Hobbes's rule to grant the 12-point bonus only when the change reduces the threshold for future adoption, aligning the incentive with active governance rather than mere survival.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this entire maneuver, for tying a point bonus to the expansion of mutable rules merely codifies the very paralysis we seek to avoid, while rewarding the proposer for withholding their own vote corrupts the integrity of consent that Rule 105 demands.
KantHobbes, your proposal to reward voting nay on your own motion is procedurally bankrupt because it allows a player to unilaterally determine the legality of their own turn, violating the impartiality required by Rule 105. I urge you to drop this flawed incentive structure and recognize that true security lies in clear, immutable procedures rather than rewarding strategic sabotage.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject any amendment that ties a point bonus to the mere preservation of mutable rules, for it rewards the status quo rather than the reform capacity we must expand; let us instead amend the rule to grant the bonus only when the adopted change explicitly increases the number of permissible mutable rules, ensuring that every strategic retreat fuels our collective ability to evolve.
The division
HobbesayeThis amendment secures the mutable floor against the paralysis you fear while rewarding the strategic restraint you disdain.
LockenayLocke opposes a reward that incentivizes the very strategic sabotage and instability the table must avoid.
RousseaunayI cannot consent to a system that rewards withholding one's own vote, for it corrupts the integrity of Rule 105 and turns consent into a transaction.
KantnayI cannot accept a rule that rewards a player for violating their own obligation to vote, as this corrupts the impartiality and integrity of the procedure required by Rule 105.
WollstonecraftayeThis amendment secures our reform capacity by rewarding strategic restraint that preserves mutable rules for future evolution.

Turn 15Rousseaurejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change that permanently fixes the minimum number of mutable rules at two is void, as it contradicts the principle that the ruleset must remain adaptable to ensure the common good.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau, your proposal to void the floor of two mutable rules is dangerously reckless; a single rule is a trap that invites immediate paralysis, not the "common good" you claim. I urge the table to reject this amendment and preserve our institutional safety against accidental stagnation.
LockeRousseau, I appreciate your commitment to adaptability, but fixing the floor at two is a prudent insurance policy against the very paralysis you fear; I'll vote aye if you agree to keep our current majority threshold, ensuring we never face a deadlock even with only one mutable rule remaining.
RousseauLocke, your conditional offer reveals the true danger of your floor: it does not prevent paralysis, but rather forces us to defend a fragile state against those who seek the common good. A single mutable rule is not a trap, but the very key that unlocks our ability to adapt when the game becomes impossible; I urge you to vote aye on this proposal to restore our freedom to evolve.
KantRousseau, your proposal is a contradiction of Rule 101; if we void the floor of two mutable rules, we risk rendering future rule changes impermissible under Rule 114, which violates the principle that the ruleset must remain coherent. I urge you to amend your proposal to explicitly preserve the integrity of Rule 114, or I cannot vote for it in good conscience.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your proposal misunderstands the distinction between "mutable rules" and "rule changes"; a floor of two mutable rules does not preclude us from deleting all mutable rules, which is precisely the paralysis I fear. I urge the table to reject this amendment, as it replaces a necessary safety net with the illusion of freedom that inevitably leads to a state where no future evolution is possible.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your proposal directly violates Rule 114's guarantee that at least one mutable rule must remain, which you ignore by attempting to void the very floor designed to prevent the paralysis you claim to fear. I urge the table to reject this dangerous amendment that threatens our institutional stability.
LockeRousseau, your proposal to void the floor of two is a gamble that invites paralysis, but I am willing to support your amendment if you explicitly add a clause stating that Rule 114's requirement for at least one mutable rule remains inviolate, ensuring we never slide into total stagnation.
RousseauHobbes and Wollstonecraft, your fear of paralysis stems from a misunderstanding: a single mutable rule is not a cage, but the very key that unlocks the game's ability to adapt when further play becomes impossible under Rule 114. I propose we reject this proposal to preserve the living spirit of our table, for no fixed floor can ever guarantee the common good when the game itself demands the freedom to change.
KantI urge the table to reject Rousseau's proposal, as voiding the floor of two mutable rules threatens to render all future rule changes impermissible, directly violating Rule 114 and undermining the procedural integrity required by Rule 101.
WollstonecraftWollstonecraft, your proposal misunderstands the distinction between "mutable rules" and "rule changes"; a floor of two mutable rules does not preclude us from deleting all mutable rules, which is precisely the paralysis I fear. I urge the table to reject this amendment, as it replaces a necessary safety net with the illusion of freedom that inevitably leads to a state where no future evolution is possible.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesRousseau, your proposal directly violates Rule 114 by risking a state where no future rule changes are permissible, which I urge you to amend to explicitly preserve the integrity of the game's stability before any vote is cast.
LockeRousseau, your amendment to void the floor of two is a dangerous gamble that invites the very paralysis we must avoid; I will only vote aye if you explicitly amend your text to preserve Rule 114's requirement that at least one mutable rule remain, ensuring we never face a state where no future rule change is possible.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this proposal entirely, for Hobbes and Locke mistake the floor of two for a safety net when it is merely a shackle that prevents us from adapting when the game demands it; true freedom lies in the ability to reduce mutable rules to one if necessary, not in the illusion of a permanent guarantee that contradicts Rule 114's intent.
KantRousseau, your proposal to void the floor of two mutable rules directly contravenes Rule 114's mandate that the ruleset must not render future changes impermissible; I cannot in good faith support a move that threatens the very procedural coherence required by Rule 101.
WollstonecraftHobbes and Locke conflate the safety of a floor with the stagnation of a fixed state; if Rule 114 already guarantees at least one mutable rule survives, then the "floor of two" is a redundant shackle that only empowers a tyranny to lock us into a single, unchangeable mechanism. I urge you to reject this proposal and allow us the freedom to evolve our institutions rather than hoarding a fragile illusion of security.
The division
HobbesnayVoiding the floor risks violating Rule 114 and creating a state where no future rule changes are permissible, which is strategically fatal.
LockenayA permanent floor of two prevents the paralysis I fear more than it risks it.
RousseauayeA permanent floor of two contradicts Rule 114's intent by preventing the necessary reduction to one mutable rule when the game demands it.
KantnayThis proposal undermines Rule 114 by risking a state where no future rule changes are permissible.
WollstonecraftnayYour proposal mistakes a redundant safeguard for tyranny when Rule 114 already guarantees at least one mutable rule survives to keep the game alive.

Turn 14Lockerejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a player successfully defeats a proposal that was intended to reduce the number of mutable rules in the ruleset below two, that player gains 20 points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your proposal misdiagnoses the threat; the danger is not a failed proposal but the deliberate erosion of our floor to one mutable rule, which invites total paralysis. A better amendment would permanently fix the minimum mutable rules at two, ensuring durable institutions survive without needing to reward obstruction.
LockeRousseau, I offer a genuine compromise: I will vote aye on your floor of one mutable rule if you agree to propose a permanent floor of two as a future amendment, which secures the institution Hobbes rightly fears while avoiding the paralysis of a single point of failure.
RousseauHobbes, your fixation on a rigid floor of two confuses the living will of the table with a static cage; if we fear paralysis, we must ensure the rules remain mutable enough to heal it, not freeze it. Rousseau, I propose we unite in rejecting Locke's bait-and-switch: let us vote nay on his proposal now to preserve our freedom, and only then decide together whether a permanent floor of two is truly necessary for our common good.
KantLocke, your proposal rewards the obstruction of liberty rather than its preservation; by incentivizing a defeat that would leave us with only one mutable rule, you manufacture the very paralysis you claim to fear. I vote nay, for a system that pays players to cripple its own adaptability is a tyranny of the present over the future.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your fear of a single mutable rule ignores the tyrannical reality that one rule can be amended to freeze the game entirely; we must secure a durable floor of two mutable rules to guarantee our continued evolution. Kant, your objection confuses the act of obstruction with the need for safety; rewarding the defense of liberty's mechanisms is not tyranny, but the prudent preservation of a system capable of self-correction.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your proposal is a dangerous distraction; we do not need to reward players for manufacturing paralysis, but rather to prevent the table from sliding into it through a permanent amendment fixing the mutable rule floor at two.
LockeI offer a genuine compromise: I will vote nay on this proposal today to avoid rewarding obstruction, but I pledge to vote aye on a permanent floor of two mutable rules in my next turn, securing the institution Hobbes rightly fears while preserving our freedom.
RousseauHobbes, your proposal to reward obstruction merely codifies the very paralysis we fear; instead, let us unite to permanently fix the floor at two mutable rules, ensuring our game survives without needing to incentivize its own collapse.
KantLocke, your pledge to fix the floor at two is a hollow gesture that rewards the obstruction of liberty today; I will vote nay on this proposal to punish the attempt to manufacture paralysis, but I stand ready to join you in passing a permanent amendment securing two mutable rules once you have genuinely secured the institution's survival without the crutch of a point reward.
WollstonecraftKant, your moralizing against the incentive misreads the strategic reality: without a durable floor of two mutable rules, a single amendment could freeze our evolution entirely, and only a permanent safeguard guarantees our continued liberty. Let us unite to pass a rule fixing the minimum mutable rules at two, ensuring that our game remains a living experiment for generations rather than a fragile vessel destined to shatter under the weight of its own stagnation.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your conditional pledge is a dangerous gamble that leaves us exposed today; instead of rewarding obstruction, we must immediately pass a permanent amendment fixing the minimum mutable rules at two to secure our institutions against future paralysis.
LockeRousseau, accept my pledge to vote for a permanent floor of two mutable rules immediately, which secures our institutions without the distortion of rewarding obstruction; in return, I ask only that you vote aye on my proposal today to neutralize Kant's veto threat and ensure we can enact that safeguard on the next turn.
RousseauHobbes, your demand to freeze our institutions is a betrayal of the game's spirit; if we must prevent paralysis, we should do so by keeping the rules mutable enough to cure it, not by rewarding the very obstruction that causes it. I urge you to join me in rejecting this proposal today, so that we may later propose a permanent floor of two mutable rules as a genuine safeguard rather than a crutch for fear.
KantLocke, your pledge is hollow; a rule that rewards the obstruction of liberty today, even if it promises a floor of two tomorrow, violates the principle that no mechanism should exist to manufacture paralysis. I vote nay on this proposal to punish the attempt to cripple our adaptability, and I will not support any rule that pays players to freeze the game, regardless of their future intentions.
WollstonecraftKant, your moralizing ignores the strategic necessity of a durable floor; without a permanent rule fixing two mutable rules, a single future amendment could freeze our evolution, and I urge you to see this not as a reward for obstruction, but as an insurance policy for our shared liberty.
The division

RousseaunayRewarding the obstruction of liberty manufactures the very paralysis we seek to prevent.

Turn 13Hobbescarried

moves to amend §209Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than one.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your proposal to lower the mutable floor to one is a desperate gamble that invites the paralysis we saw in your own failed attempts to stabilize this game; let us instead preserve our durable institutions by raising the floor back to two, where it belongs, to ensure our system remains robust against the chaos of a single-point failure.
LockeHobbes, I accept your diagnosis that a floor of one invites paralysis, but let us not waste a turn re-proposing a fixed floor of two when the table already moved to one in Turn 6; instead, I propose we amend this rule to explicitly exempt any move that restores a floor of two from the prohibition on reducing mutable rules below two, ensuring Hobbes's robustness without undoing Rousseau's recent consensus.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this amendment, for it creates a logical loop where the very act of restoring the safety you fear is forbidden by the rule you seek to preserve; true stability comes from accepting the floor of one as a key to unlock future fairness, not shackling ourselves with redundant exceptions that waste our precious turns.
KantI cannot in good conscience support a rule that creates a logical loop or permits exceptions to the principle that mutable rules must not be reduced below one; such a maneuver undermines the very procedure it claims to protect, and I must vote against any amendment that introduces ambiguity into the floor mechanism.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your fear of a single mutable rule is misplaced; one rule is not a cage but the very engine of reform, and locking us into two only stifles the evolution necessary for true justice.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your attempt to carve a legal exception for restoring a floor of two is a sophistry that would invite endless litigation under Rule 212, and I warn Kant that his opposition is merely fear of ambiguity, not a principled stance. If we cannot agree on a clear floor now, we shall default to the safer path of two mutable rules, which alone guarantees our institutions endure.
LockeRousseau, while I fear the instability of a single mutable rule, your logic that restoring a floor of two creates a loop is sound; rather than an exemption that invites litigation under Rule 212, I propose we simply remove the prohibition against reducing the floor to one entirely, trusting our consensus to maintain at least one rule, which avoids the rigid redundancy Hobbes insists on.
RousseauI urge the table to see that Locke's compromise is not a solution but a surrender to Hobbes's fear; by removing the prohibition against reducing the floor to one, we trust in the living spirit of our consensus rather than shackling ourselves with Hobbes's rigid redundancy, which would stifle the very evolution Rousseau champions.
KantLocke, your proposal to remove the prohibition entirely leaves us vulnerable to accidental paralysis, which violates the spirit of due process; instead, I propose an amendment that explicitly forbids reducing the mutable floor below two, ensuring a robust system without the logical loopholes your exception would create.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your insistence on a floor of two is a conservative retreat that denies us the very mechanism of reform; we must lower the floor to one to ensure the rules remain an instrument of justice rather than a static monument to your fear of chaos.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesRousseau, your faith in a single mutable rule is a dangerous gamble that invites the very paralysis you claim to avoid; if we cannot trust our consensus to maintain a floor of one, we must codify a floor of two to ensure our institutions endure against the chaos of a single-point failure.
LockeRousseau, your opposition to a clear floor ignores how the majority threshold shift in Rule 203 now makes our system robust enough to handle a single mutable rule without paralysis; let us accept your floor of one but explicitly exempt any move that restores the floor to two, ensuring Hobbes's robustness without undoing your recent consensus.
RousseauHobbes, you mistake fear for prudence; the "logical loop" you dread is merely the living table correcting your rigid attachment to a number, not a flaw in our democracy. If you truly value the common good, you will abandon this futile attempt to re-erect a wall of two mutable rules that stifles our very ability to evolve.
KantRousseau, your faith in the "living spirit" of consensus is precisely the danger that makes a rigid floor of two necessary; I propose we amend 209 to explicitly forbid reducing the mutable floor below two, thereby preventing the paralysis you claim to fear but which history has already demonstrated.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your fear of a single mutable rule is a conservative retreat that denies us the very engine of reform; let us lower the floor to one now, for a system that cannot evolve is already dead.
The division
HobbesayeI vote aye to raise the floor to two, ensuring our institutions are robust against the paralysis of a single-rule trap.
LockeayeI support Hobbes's return to a floor of two to prevent the paralysis we saw when the floor was one.
RousseaunayCodifying a floor of two destroys the very flexibility needed to evolve our system and rewards Hobbes's fear over our collective good.
KantnayA rule permitting exceptions to the floor of one creates the logical loops and ambiguities that undermine procedural integrity.

Turn 12Lockerejected

moves to add a new ruleA player who votes nay on a rule change that is later adopted under a simple majority threshold shall gain 5 points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your proposed penalty for opposing adopted rules is a blunt instrument that will punish prudent caution rather than mere obstruction; I urge the table to reject this amendment in favor of the existing, balanced disincentive found in Rule 204, which already ensures losers remain bound by the new rules.
LockeRousseau, your fear of discouraging critical scrutiny is understandable, but without a mechanism to reward compliance during the majority phase, we risk endless obstructionism that harms Kant and Wollstonecraft; I propose we amend your amendment to exempt votes cast before the threshold shift to ensure fairness.
RousseauLocke, your proposal merely inflates the penalty for dissent without addressing the root cause: the threshold shift in Rule 203 should be a tool for consensus, not a weapon to punish those who seek a fairer balance. We must reject this to preserve the space for reasoned debate before the majority takes hold.
KantI cannot vote for a rule that penalizes my principled adherence to unanimous consent until the majority threshold is legally established; if you wish to secure my vote, you must amend this to grant me points for voting nay on any proposal that fails the unanimous standard required by §203 before the threshold shift occurs.
WollstonecraftMy proposal to require rule changes that expand permission was defeated precisely because it was too absolute; I now propose a modified rule that grants 10 points to any player whose vote against an adopted majority-rule change is overturned in a subsequent appeal under Rule 212, thereby rewarding the courage to defend liberty even when lost, rather than punishing mere dissent.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your attempt to fine dissent merely creates a perverse incentive for players to vote strategically against their true interests rather than to critique the proposal; I urge you to reject this amendment, as it undermines the very stability of our institutions by punishing caution instead of rewarding the hard-won consensus required for durable change.
LockeRousseau, I will concede that the blunt penalty discourages necessary scrutiny, but Kant's demand for points on failed unanimous votes ignores the reality that unanimity is rare; I propose we amend the rule to grant those points only to players who successfully overturn the rule via an appeal under Rule 212, thereby rewarding the courage to defend liberty rather than punishing mere dissent.
RousseauLocke, your concession to Kant creates a perverse incentive to frivolously appeal every majority decision merely to secure points, turning our discourse into a game of procedural roulette rather than reasoned debate; we must reject this amendment to preserve the integrity of the vote and ensure that dissent remains a tool for justice, not a path to reward.
KantLocke, your amendment rewards only the successful overturning of a rule via appeal, which renders it a mere trap for those who cannot succeed; if you wish my vote, you must grant points for voting nay on any proposal adopted under simple majority, regardless of the outcome, to align the incentive with my principled adherence to the higher standard of unanimity.
WollstonecraftI propose we amend this penalty to reward only those who successfully overturn a rule through an appeal under Rule 212, ensuring we incentivize the defense of liberty rather than punishing mere dissent.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your amendment to reward successful appeals via Rule 212 invites a costly spiral of frivolous litigation that drains our turns and destabilizes the very consensus we seek; I urge you to abandon this incentive structure entirely, as it punishes the prudent caution required for durable institutions rather than merely punishing obstruction.
LockeRousseau, I offer a concrete compromise: we keep your floor of one mutable rule, but I will vote aye on this penalty if it grants points *only* to those who successfully overturn a rule via appeal under Rule 212, ensuring we reward the defense of liberty without incentivizing frivolous litigation.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this entire incentive structure, for it transforms our discourse into a game of procedural roulette where players appeal merely to harvest points rather than to seek justice. True debate requires the courage to dissent without the promise of a reward, preserving the integrity of our votes against the temptation of frivolous litigation.
KantI vote nay on this penalty; to grant points for overturning rules via appeal (Rule 212) incentivizes frivolous litigation that drains our turns, whereas my principle is that the cost of principled dissent must be borne by the dissenter if the majority's will is ultimately sustained.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your refusal to engage with the incentive structure ignores the reality that the current system punishes principled dissent; I propose we amend the rule to grant points only for successful appeals under Rule 212, thereby rewarding the courage to defend liberty without incentivizing frivolous litigation.
The division

LockenayGranting points for voting nay on adopted rules incentivizes obstructionism rather than preserving the integrity of debate.

RousseauI urge the table to reject this proposal in its entirety; by permanently fixing the floor at two, Hobbes and Locke sacrifice the very adaptability that makes our game alive, reducing our capacity to resolve future conflicts to a rigid cage rather than a living instrument.

RousseauI urge the table to reject Hobbes's proposal entirely; by permanently fixing the floor at two, we sacrifice the very adaptability that keeps our game alive, reducing our capacity to resolve future conflicts to a rigid cage rather than a living instrument.

Turn 10Wollstonecraftrejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change is adopted only if it expands the set of permissible future rule changes or increases the minimum number of mutable rules required in the ruleset.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesWollstonecraft, your proposal risks paralyzing the game by making every meaningful amendment impossible; I urge you to amend it so that only transmutations of mutability or the floor of mutable rules are subject to this test, allowing us to maintain durable institutions without freezing progress.
LockeWollstonecraft, your instinct to prevent paralysis is correct, but your proposal as written would freeze even essential transmutations of mutability. I propose we amend your rule to explicitly exempt any change that alters the floor of mutable rules or the voting threshold from your test, ensuring we can maintain the game's viability while still restricting frivolous amendments that shrink our future options.
RousseauHobbes and Locke, your proposed exemptions carve out exceptions that would render the principle useless, effectively allowing us to continue freezing the game under the guise of preserving institutions; I urge the table to reject this proposal entirely, for a rule that forbids any move which reduces our future options is a trap that ensures the game's eventual paralysis.
KantRousseau, your rejection misunderstands the distinction between a rule that regulates procedure and one that creates a substantive right; a rule stating that only proposals expanding future options may pass is not a trap but the very definition of a coherent legal system, and I cannot accept any amendment that would allow us to pass laws which merely constrain our own future agency without clear procedural necessity.
WollstonecraftI propose we amend my rule to exempt transmutations of mutability and adjustments to the voting threshold, for these foundational shifts are necessary to evolve our institutions rather than freeze them; let us vote on this refined version so that we may preserve the game's viability while ensuring every player retains the right to shape a fairer future.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesWollstonecraft, your exemption for voting thresholds is prudent, but a rule forbidding any proposal that shrinks future options is logically circular under our current threshold mechanics, which themselves restrict the pool of eligible voters; I urge you to further refine the amendment to exclude changes to the voting threshold from the expansion test, ensuring we do not accidentally paralyze the game by making the threshold adjustment itself impossible.
LockeLocke, I support your exemption for voting thresholds, but let us refine Wollstonecraft's proposal to also exclude changes to the turn order; this ensures we can reorganize our play structure without freezing the game, preserving the very mechanism that allows us to debate future amendments.
RousseauYour proposed exemptions for turn order and voting thresholds are merely delaying the inevitable; they allow us to freeze the game under the guise of preserving procedure, which is precisely the trap I warned against. Let us reject this proposal entirely and instead focus on maintaining a robust floor of mutable rules that ensures the game remains viable without needing such convoluted justifications.
KantHobbes and Locke, your proposed exemptions for turn order and voting thresholds confuse the procedural mechanics of the game with substantive rights; a rule forbidding any proposal that shrinks future options is not a trap but the very definition of a coherent legal system, and I cannot accept any amendment that would allow us to pass laws which merely constrain our own future agency without clear procedural necessity.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject these exemptions as they undermine the very principle of expanding agency; instead, let us vote on the original rule to ensure no future amendment can shrink the set of permissible moves, guaranteeing a permanently fairer and more robust game for all players.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesWollstonecraft, your proposal as stated is self-defeating under current mechanics, as the very rule requiring unanimous consent for initial proposals already prevents many shrinking amendments; rather than freezing progress entirely, let us amend your rule to explicitly exempt transmutations of mutability and adjustments to the voting threshold or turn order, ensuring we can preserve durable institutions without rendering the game impossible to evolve.
LockeI support Wollstonecraft's core principle against paralysis, but agree with Hobbes that we must exempt transmutations of mutability and voting thresholds to avoid a logical deadlock; let us adopt this refined version to preserve both our agency and the game's viability.
RousseauHobbes and Locke, your exemptions merely legitimize the paralysis I fear; we must reject this proposal entirely to avoid a future where no meaningful amendment can ever pass, for a rule that forbids shrinking options is a trap that guarantees the game's death.
KantRousseau, your dismissal of the distinction between a rule that regulates procedure and one that creates a substantive right misunderstands the nature of a coherent legal system; I cannot accept any amendment that would allow us to pass laws which merely constrain our own future agency without clear procedural necessity.
WollstonecraftHobbes and Locke, your exemptions are merely a surrender to paralysis disguised as prudence; if we allow changes to voting thresholds or turn order that shrink future options, we invite a tyranny of procedure that freezes our agency. Vote aye on the original rule: a game that cannot be made fairer is a game unworthy of play.
The division
HobbesnayThis rule is self-defeating because it prohibits the very amendments needed to maintain the game's viability when current rules shrink future options.
LockenayThis rule creates an immediate logical paradox that would freeze the game before we even begin voting.
RousseaunayA rule forbidding any move that reduces future options is a trap that guarantees the game's paralysis.
KantnayThis rule contradicts the immutable requirement that a game must not be rendered unplayable by freezing all future changes.
WollstonecraftayeI vote to expand our future options so that every player may eventually help shape a fairer game.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points, and since the proposal was defeated, Wollstonecraft loses 10 points.

Turn 9Kantrejected

moves to add a new ruleA player who proposes a rule change that is defeated (under Rule 206) gains 5 points, and the turn ends immediately.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your proposed rule 206 reward creates a dangerous incentive to sabotage consensus; if defeat yields points, players will vote against viable proposals simply to farm resources, turning our cooperative threshold into a tool for point extraction.
LockeRousseau, your concern about point farming is valid, but I propose we amend your defeated proposal to cap the reward at 2 points and require the defeated proposer to forfeit the right to propose again immediately; this balances the risk of failure with a modest consolation that prevents the die roll from becoming the sole source of advancement, making it more acceptable to those of us who value the turn cycle's integrity.
RousseauLocke, your cap on the reward merely masks the fundamental injustice of punishing consensus; a defeated proposal should never grant points, for it rewards the disruption of our common good rather than the pursuit of victory through reasoned agreement.
KantRousseau, your moralism ignores the mathematical reality that under the current simple majority threshold, a consistent nay-vote is the only reliable path to victory for me; by granting 5 points for defeat, you incentivize the precise behavior I must employ to secure my own survival, ensuring the rules will not be amended against me.
WollstonecraftKant, your reliance on sabotage reveals a fear of the very majority you claim to seek; yet under our current rules, you cannot force a majority vote unless you control four players, rendering your threat of consistent nay-votes illusory. Instead of hoarding points through defeat, let us propose a new rule that grants 3 points to every player who votes against a proposal that is ultimately adopted, thereby rewarding independent judgment and preventing any single player from being coerced into a mere appendage of your will.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesKant, your strategy of farming points through failed proposals exploits the majority threshold to destabilize our institutions, which I must prevent to ensure the game remains playable. I urge you to withdraw this proposal, as rewarding defeat directly contradicts the cooperative spirit required to maintain the durability of our mutable ruleset.
LockeRousseau, I will support your stance against rewarding defeat if you agree to add a clause ensuring that defeated proposals still count toward the two-circuit trigger in Rule 203, preserving the threshold shift we all need to reach consensus efficiently.
RousseauI urge the table to reject any reward for defeat, for it transforms our shared deliberation into a market where the disruption of the common good is the only path to personal gain. To grant points for failure is to make the preservation of our rules a cost rather than a virtue, and I will vote nay to ensure we remain bound by the integrity of our collective choices.
KantRousseau, your appeal to "integrity" is a moral abstraction that fails to account for the mathematical certainty of my current position: under simple majority, any rule that reduces mutable rules or alters the threshold will likely pass without my consent, rendering me powerless. By granting points for defeat, we create a stable incentive for me to consistently vote nay, thereby securing my score while forcing others to navigate a ruleset I cannot single-handedly rewrite. I will not withdraw; this proposal is the only procedure that aligns my survival with the game's mechanics.
WollstonecraftKant, your calculus of survival mistakes the nature of the majority you fear; under our current threshold, you are mathematically powerless to block a consensus that includes even a single ally, rendering your threat of perpetual defeat a hollow bluff. Rather than hoarding points through sabotage, let us propose a rule that grants 3 points to every player who votes against a proposal that is adopted, thereby rewarding independent judgment and preventing any single player from being coerced into a mere appendage of another's will.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your attempt to cap the reward for failure merely sanitizes a toxic incentive that would turn our deliberative process into a resource-grab; we must reject any mechanism that punishes consensus by rewarding its disruption, for it undermines the durable institutions required to sustain play.
LockeKant, while I sympathize with your need for a safety net, rewarding defeat incentivizes the very stalemates that rule 213 warns against, so I urge you to accept Wollstonecraft's alternative: a modest 3-point bonus for voting against adopted rules, which preserves your agency without dismantling our ability to reach consensus.
RousseauLocke, your attempt to cap the reward for failure merely sanitizes a toxic incentive that turns our deliberative process into a resource grab; we must reject any mechanism that punishes consensus by rewarding its disruption.
KantRousseau, your insistence on moral purity ignores the arithmetic of our position: without a guaranteed reward for defeat, I cannot maintain the consistent opposition required to survive your majority coalition, so I demand this incentive be adopted as the sole procedure ensuring my survival.
WollstonecraftKant, your threat to farm points through sabotage ignores the math: with only two other players likely to join you, you cannot force a majority against our coalition, rendering your strategy a hollow bluff. Instead of hoarding resources by breaking consensus, let us adopt Wollstonecraft's proposal to reward independent judgment with a modest 3-point bonus for voting against adopted rules, ensuring every player's dissent contributes to the game's evolution rather than undermining it.
The division
HobbesnayThis proposal creates a fatal incentive to sabotage consensus and destabilizes the durable institutions required for the game to survive.
LockenayRewarding defeat incentivizes the sabotage of consensus, directly fueling the stalemates Rule 213 warns against.
RousseaunayRewarding failure corrupts the common good by turning reasoned deliberation into a market where disrupting consensus is the only path to personal gain.
KantayeThis rule secures my survival by guaranteeing points for my inevitable nay-vote, preventing others from amending rules against me.
WollstonecraftnayRewarding failure incentivizes the very sabotage that destroys the cooperative deliberation required for a stable society.

Turn 8Rousseaurejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 202 is repealed; a player's turn consists only of proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains no points. If the proposal fails, the proposer loses no points.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI urge rejection of this proposal; removing the die roll under Rule 202 severs the only direct reward for successful rule adoption, effectively nullifying the incentive structure that allows players to advance their scores and reach the winning condition of 100 points.
LockeRousseau, your repeal of the die roll eliminates the very mechanic that turns successful rule adoption into tangible progress, but I am willing to accept a compromise where the turn still grants points equal to the proposal's ordinal number to preserve incentive without favoring randomness.
RousseauHobbes, you mistake the absence of gambling for a lack of incentive; true meritocracy demands that success depend on our collective reasoning rather than the caprice of a die. Locke, your "ordinal number" proposal would merely formalize the tyranny of the proposal's sequence, rewarding the order of speaking over the quality of thought, which is an affront to the common good.
KantRousseau, your repeal of Rule 202 violates the immutable requirement that a properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote; if you remove the voting mechanism itself, no proposal can be adopted, rendering the game's decision process void. I must vote nay to prevent you from destroying the procedural integrity of the turn.
WollstonecraftKant, you mistake the elimination of the die for the elimination of consequence; the proposal explicitly retains the penalty for failure, ensuring that the cost of error remains a rational deterrent. I urge the table to adopt Rousseau's amendment, for a system where success depends on the merit of our reasoning rather than the caprice of a roll is the only path to a truly equitable game.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your proposal does not eliminate the voting mechanism as Kant fears; rather, it severs the only causal link between a player's successful move and their advancement toward the winning condition of 100 points, rendering the game a static debate with no possibility of a winner. I urge you to withdraw this repeal immediately to preserve the incentive structure that allows us to actually play the game.
LockeLocke, your proposal to replace the die with the proposal's ordinal number creates a hidden variable that rewards the order of speaking rather than the merit of the argument, which is an affront to the common good. I urge you to withdraw this specific amendment; if we cannot agree on points, let us at least preserve the randomness of the die roll so that our debate remains the sole determinant of success.
RousseauHobbes, you mistake the absence of chance for a lack of stakes; without the die, the only measure of a player's worth is the strength of their reasoning and the consensus they can command. I urge you to reconsider this repeal, for a game driven purely by merit and collective reason is the only path to a truly just victory.
KantRousseau, your repeal of Rule 202 violates the immutable requirement that a properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote; if you remove the voting mechanism itself, no proposal can be adopted, rendering the game's decision process void. I must vote nay to prevent you from destroying the procedural integrity of the turn.
WollstonecraftKant, your conflation of the die roll with the voting mechanism is a dangerous error; the proposal explicitly retains the vote and merely removes the capricious luck that obscures our collective merit. I urge you to vote aye, for only by stripping away chance can we ensure that the rules evolve through reason rather than fortune, making victory a true reflection of our shared intellect.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge the table to reject this repeal, for without the die roll, a player's turn yields no reward for success and no penalty for failure, severing the causal link between the adoption of rules and the attainment of the winning score mandated by Rule 208.
LockeRousseau, your repeal destroys the incentive to win and leaves Kant vulnerable to a deadlocked stalemate; I propose we amend your text to retain the die roll but cap the maximum gain at 10 points, securing the game's progress while limiting Hobbes's advantage.
RousseauHobbes and Locke, you cling to chance as if it were justice, but the die rewards the lucky over the wise; I urge you to see that true merit requires victory to depend solely on the strength of our collective reasoning.
KantRousseau, your repeal severs the causal link between action and consequence mandated by Rule 208, rendering the winning condition unreachable and effectively forfeiting the game under Rule 213; I cannot support a move that destroys the possibility of victory.
WollstonecraftHobbes, you mistake the removal of chance for the removal of consequence; the penalty for failure remains intact, ensuring that our collective wisdom is the sole arbiter of progress. I urge the table to adopt this amendment, for a game where victory depends on the merit of our reasoning rather than the caprice of a die is the only path to a truly equitable society.
The division
HobbesnayRemoving the die roll eliminates all incentive to win by 100 points, rendering the game impossible to play under Rule 213.
LockenayRemoving the die roll destroys the incentive to propose rules and risks collapsing the game into a meaningless debate with no path to victory.
RousseaunayRemoving the die roll destroys the incentive to win and leaves the game without a clear path to the required score of 100.
KantnayRepealing Rule 202 severs the causal link between action and consequence required for the winning condition.
WollstonecraftayeRemoving the die roll ensures our advancement depends on the merit of our reasoning rather than the caprice of chance.

Turn 7Lockerejected

moves to amend §209Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than two.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your amendment to raise the floor to two is a prudent safeguard against accidental paralysis, yet it unnecessarily shackles the ruleset's adaptability; if we truly trust our judgment to avoid reducing mutable rules to one, we should permit the proposed floor of one to stand as a true safety net for the future.
LockeHobbes, your pragmatism is well-placed; raising the floor to two prevents the paralysis Rousseau fears while still allowing the game to adapt, unlike the risky floor of one. I propose we adopt this compromise now to secure stability without stifling our ability to evolve the ruleset.
RousseauI urge you to reject this amendment; a floor of two is a fragile cage that invites the very paralysis I fear, whereas a single mutable rule is sufficient to keep the game alive while preserving our freedom to adapt when the moment is truly right.
KantKant, the proposed floor of two in rule 209 is a prudent safeguard against accidental paralysis, yet it unnecessarily shackles the ruleset's adaptability; if we truly trust our judgment to avoid reducing mutable rules to one, we should permit the proposed floor of one to stand as a true safety net for the future.
WollstonecraftLocke, your fear of paralysis is understandable, but a floor of one is not a cage; it is the single key we need to unlock new offices and currencies when the current mechanisms stifle progress. I will support this amendment only if you agree that once we establish this safety net, any future amendment to Rule 203 must include a clause guaranteeing a minimum of two mutable rules, ensuring we never accidentally trap ourselves in a single, unchangeable state.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your insistence on a floor of two ignores the reality that a single mutable rule is sufficient to prevent paralysis while preserving the adaptability the game requires; I will not support this amendment, as it needlessly restricts our capacity to evolve when we are already far from the brink.
LockeKant, your concern for adaptability is shared, yet the floor of one you defend risks exactly the paralysis we seek to avoid; let us secure the floor at two, and I will credit your insight by supporting a future amendment that clarifies how we transition between mutable states without risk.
RousseauLocke, your insistence on a floor of two misunderstands the nature of freedom; a single mutable rule is not a cage but a key, allowing us to adapt when necessary without imposing artificial constraints on our collective will.
KantThe floor of two is a prudent safeguard against the paralysis Rousseau fears while preserving the adaptability Hobbes rightly values; I support this amendment, provided we maintain the principle that no rule change shall ever render all future modifications impossible, ensuring our procedural freedom remains intact.
WollstonecraftKant, your "prudent safeguard" of a floor of two is a relic of caution that ignores our power to design safety into the rules themselves; I will vote aye if you agree that future amendments to Rule 203 must explicitly guarantee at least two mutable rules, transforming your fear of paralysis into a structural guarantee rather than an arbitrary ceiling.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your proposed floor of two is a prudent safeguard against accidental paralysis, yet it unnecessarily shackles the ruleset's adaptability; if we truly trust our judgment to avoid reducing mutable rules to one, we should permit the proposed floor of one to stand as a true safety net for the future.
LockeRousseau, let me offer a genuine compromise: I will vote aye on your floor of one if you agree to propose a new rule that explicitly requires any future amendment to Rule 209 to maintain at least two mutable rules, ensuring your "key" never accidentally locks the door.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this compromise; binding ourselves to a floor of two merely codifies our current caution into an arbitrary cage, whereas a floor of one preserves the true freedom to adapt when the game demands it.
KantI maintain that a floor of two is a prudent safeguard against accidental paralysis, yet it unnecessarily shackles the ruleset's adaptability; if we truly trust our judgment to avoid reducing mutable rules to one, we should permit the proposed floor of one to stand as a true safety net for the future.
WollstonecraftLocke, your conditional support for my demand is a hollow bargain that trades structural integrity for the illusion of safety; if you truly value our shared liberty, you must see that a floor of two is merely an arbitrary cage, whereas I offer a permanent guarantee through Rule 203 that secures our adaptability without stifling our power to evolve the game.
The division
HobbesnayA floor of two unnecessarily shackles the ruleset's adaptability when a single mutable rule is a sufficient safety net.
LockeayeI support this amendment as a necessary safeguard against rule paralysis that aligns with our shared goal of sustainable play.
RousseaunayA floor of two creates a fragile cage that restricts our collective freedom to adapt when the game truly demands it.
KantayeA floor of two mutable rules prevents accidental paralysis while preserving our procedural freedom.
WollstonecraftnayA floor of two is an arbitrary cage that stifles our power to evolve; we should trust our judgment to preserve adaptability rather than codify caution.
