Turn 6Rousseaucarried

moves to amend §209Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than one.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI propose we keep the floor at two, not one; reducing to one invites the paralysis that rule 213 already guards against, and such a reduction would be redundant given our recent experience.
LockeRousseau, I appreciate your vigilance against paralysis, but lowering the floor to one is a risky gamble that could destabilize our ability to adapt; instead of repeating this failure, let us build consensus on a higher, safer threshold that everyone can endorse.
RousseauHobbes and Locke, your fear of paralysis ignores the very real danger that a floor of two allows a single malicious rule to freeze the game entirely; lowering the floor to one preserves our ability to self-correct when the rules become tyrannical, which is the essence of a living democracy.
KantI cannot in good conscience support a rule that permits the reduction of mutable rules to one, as such a configuration risks rendering the game's procedural evolution impossible under rule 114; I must oppose this amendment to preserve the stability of our legal framework.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your "tyrannical" fear misunderstands the mechanics: lowering the floor to one does not freeze the game, but rather grants *us* the sole power to unshackle ourselves from any future paralysis, which is the only true safeguard for a living democracy.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesRousseau, your appeal to democracy ignores the mechanics of rule 104 and 114: with only one mutable rule, any player holding a single 'nay' vote can permanently lock the table into an unchangeable state, which is precisely the tyranny you claim to prevent.
LockeRousseau, your fear of tyranny is valid, but lowering the floor to one invites the very paralysis you seek to avoid; let us instead agree to keep the floor at two, which Hobbes rightly identifies as the only safe threshold that preserves our ability to evolve without freezing the game.
RousseauHobbes, your argument that a single mutable rule creates tyranny ignores that unanimity remains the only shield against paralysis; yet without a second mutable rule, the table loses the very capacity to reform that tyranny when it arises. Wollstonecraft, you mistake the number of mutable rules for the number of defenders; lowering the floor to one does not grant us power, but rather surrenders our future freedom to a single, potentially hostile amendment.
KantLocke, your plea for "consensus on a higher threshold" is merely an attempt to preserve the status quo of the two-rule floor, which you admit is "risky" yet refuse to change; if you truly believe two mutable rules are necessary to prevent paralysis, why do you oppose an amendment that lowers the floor to one, thereby granting the table a greater capacity to self-correct against future tyranny?
WollstonecraftKant, your appeal to stability blinds you to the very tyranny of rigidity; a floor of one ensures we can always choose the path of reform, whereas two rules merely grant a veto to the status quo. Rousseau, your fear of surrender is misplaced, for the power to reduce mutable rules to one is the ultimate sovereign right—a single, deliberate act that unshackles us from any future paralysis rather than leaving us at the mercy of a hostile second rule.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge the table to reject Rousseau's amendment, as reducing the floor to one mutable rule creates a catastrophic single-point of failure where a single malicious player can permanently lock the game into an unchangeable state, violating the spirit of durable institutions we all claim to value.
LockeRousseau, your fear of tyranny is valid, but lowering the floor to one invites the very paralysis you seek to avoid; let us agree to keep the floor at two, which Hobbes rightly identifies as the only safe threshold that preserves our ability to evolve without freezing the game.
RousseauLocke, you mistake the floor for a safety net; a single mutable rule is not a cage but a key, allowing us to unlock the table from any future tyranny that two rules might inadvertently permit.
KantLocke, your insistence on "consensus" is merely a disguise for preserving the status quo against a necessary check on tyranny; I cannot support your refusal to vote aye on this amendment simply because you fear a single rule might be misused, when Rule 109 already demands unanimous assent for any change to that rule's mutability.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your conflation of the mutable floor with the immutable requirement for unanimous transmutation is a fatal error in logic; by lowering the floor to one, we ensure that when the tyranny of two rules inevitably arises, we possess the singular, decisive power to unshackle the game, whereas retaining two merely grants a permanent veto to the status quo that no amount of consensus can override.
The division
HobbesnayLowering the floor to one creates a single point of failure that violates the principle of durable institutions.
LockeayeI support lowering the floor to one to ensure the game remains adaptable and prevent future paralysis.
RousseauayeA single mutable rule preserves our sovereign right to unshackle the game from any future paralysis.
KantnayA floor of one mutable rule risks violating rule 114 by making future rule changes impermissible if that single rule becomes restrictive.
WollstonecraftayeLowering the floor to one grants the table the ultimate sovereign key to unshackle itself from any future tyranny of rigidity.

carried 3 aye · 2 nay

Rule 202 awards points only upon a successful die roll after adoption, and no rule in the current set awards points based on the voting division or the adoption of a rule change itself.

Turn 5Lockerejected

moves to add §303Players who vote aye on a proposal that adopts a rule granting points to the proposer receive 1 bonus point. Players who vote aye on a proposal that adopts a rule granting points to another player receive 1 bonus point.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesLocke, your proposed rule 303 creates a dangerous feedback loop that inflates scores without adding strategic depth, and its reliance on a definition of "granting" that could easily be stretched to include my own successful rule changes is a loophole you cannot close without undermining the very point system you seek to influence.
LockeHobbes, I accept your critique on "feedback loops" as a valid concern for game health; let us narrow 303 to strictly credit bonus points only when the adopted rule explicitly names the proposer, thereby eliminating ambiguity while preserving the incentive for consensus.
RousseauLocke, your narrow definition of "explicitly names" merely delays the rot; it invites endless, trivial amendments that clog the ruleset with noise rather than substance. Before we adopt this distraction, let us first repeal rule 202's point-granting mechanic entirely, for a game where turns are bought with dice rolls has already lost its civic soul.
KantLocke, your attempt to "narrow" rule 303 merely creates a new, equally mutable loophole that violates the principle of procedural clarity; before we pollute the ruleset with another bonus mechanism, we must first repeal rule 202's dice-granting mechanic, as a game where turns are purchased with luck lacks the moral legitimacy required for true deliberation.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject this proposal entirely, for it treats rule-making as a marketplace for points rather than a mechanism for collective improvement; before we incentivize noise with bonuses, let us repeal rule 202's dice-granting mechanic, which rewards luck over reason and undermines the civic soul of our deliberation.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your "narrow" definition does not eliminate ambiguity; it merely shifts the burden of defining "explicitly names" to a future, mutable text where a clever amendment can render your bonus meaningless. Reject this proposal; let us focus on repealing the luck-based reward of Rule 202 to restore the game to a contest of pure strategy rather than gambling on dice.
LockeI propose we amend my draft of 303 to explicitly exclude any bonus when the adopted rule amends a previous rule granting points, addressing Hobbes's valid concern about recursive inflation while preserving the incentive for genuine consensus.
RousseauRousseau, your call to repeal Rule 202 is noble but imprudent; without the dice roll, players who fail to propose viable changes simply waste turns rather than being punished, leaving the game stagnant and devoid of the necessary friction that sharpens our collective reasoning.
KantLocke, your proposed amendment fails to address the root violation: by introducing a bonus tied to voting behavior, you create a self-referential loop that rewards the mere act of consensus rather than substantive improvement, corrupting the impartiality required of any proceduralist. We must repeal Rule 202 first to restore the game to a contest of pure strategy, as luck-based rewards have no place in a system governed by reason.
WollstonecraftI urge the table to reject Locke's amendment and instead repeal Rule 202 immediately, for no bonus system can redeem a mechanism that rewards luck over reason. Let us replace the dice with a civic test where every rule change requires proof of collective benefit, ensuring that our points reflect wisdom rather than chance.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your attempt to narrow the definition of "explicitly names" is a futile exercise in self-deception that merely delays the inevitable collapse of our point system into a recursive mess; I propose we repeal Rule 202 immediately, for no bonus can legitimate a mechanic that rewards the luck of the die over the virtue of sound reasoning.
LockeRousseau, I will not support repealing Rule 202 because the die roll is the only mechanism that ensures every turn produces a definitive outcome rather than stalling the game, and your call to remove it risks leaving us with a ruleset that generates no progress.
RousseauRousseau, your call to repeal Rule 202 is noble but imprudent; without the dice roll, players who fail to propose viable changes simply waste turns rather than being punished, leaving the game stagnant and devoid of the necessary friction that sharpens our collective reasoning.
KantLocke, your amendment merely shifts the locus of arbitrariness from the die roll to a recursive textual definition of "explicitly names," which violates the principle of procedural clarity; before we adopt any such bonus, we must repeal Rule 202 to restore the moral legitimacy of the game by removing luck as a determinant of success.
WollstonecraftLocke, your narrow amendment still rewards the mechanics of consensus rather than the substance of improvement; let us instead repeal Rule 202 entirely and replace the dice with a requirement that every adopted rule must explicitly state a public benefit to the collective, ensuring our points reflect wisdom rather than chance.
The division
HobbesnayThis rule corrupts the integrity of voting by turning a duty of consensus into a transaction for personal points.
LockeayeThis vote secures my turn while rewarding the consensus needed to pass my proposal, aligning with my strategy of building alliances.
RousseaunayThis bonus rewards consensus over substance, turning our civic deliberation into a mere marketplace for points.
KantnayThis proposal introduces an arbitrary reward based on voting behavior rather than substantive improvement, violating the principles of procedural clarity and impartiality.
WollstonecraftnayA bonus for voting aye rewards the mere act of consensus rather than the substance of improvement, turning our civic deliberation into a marketplace for points.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposal results in the proposer losing 10 points, and no other rule in the current ruleset awards points for voting patterns or defeats.

Turn 4Hobbesrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesThe shift to simple majority was necessary, yet we must prevent it from becoming a tool for destabilizing the game. I propose an amendment to rule 203: "Once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority, no single player's vote may be sufficient to defeat a proposal that directly alters the winning condition or the number of mutable rules." This ensures durability without returning us to the paralysis of unanimity.
LockeHobbes, your amendment protects the game's core integrity, but it inadvertently grants veto power to a single dissenter on critical changes, which contradicts the spirit of simple majority. Let us instead adopt a simpler safeguard: "No proposal may be adopted that reduces the number of mutable rules below two," ensuring we never reach paralysis without needing complex conditional vetoes.
RousseauHobbes, your attempt to carve out exceptions for "critical changes" merely creates a loophole that undermines the very simplicity you claim to protect; true stability comes from the clear, universal application of the majority rule, not from special protections that confuse the players and invite endless debate over what constitutes "critical."
KantI cannot accept an amendment that grants unilateral veto power over critical changes, as it violates the principle of procedural equality enshrined in rule 207; if we are to move beyond unanimity, the majority must rule without special exceptions that privilege one player's judgment over the collective will.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your proposed "durability" is merely a disguised veto that fractures the collective will; if the majority deems a change to the winning condition necessary for progress, a single player's obstruction should not freeze the entire experiment. I support the amendment that simply enforces the majority vote without carving out special exceptions that privilege judgment over consent.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your suggestion to simply restate rule 209 is redundant and wastes a turn when we already possess the floor of two; Rousseau, while your critique of special exceptions is sound, ignoring the fragility of mutable thresholds invites paralysis.
LockeHobbes, your amendment creates a dangerous loophole where "critical changes" become subjective battlegrounds, whereas my proposal to codify the existing two-rule floor in 203 already guarantees stability without complex, debatable exceptions. Let us adopt the clarity of my text to preserve the game's forward momentum.
RousseauMy fellow players, Hobbes and Locke attempt to fortify the game against chaos by carving out special exceptions for "critical" changes or restating the floor of mutable rules; yet these very distinctions fracture the simplicity and universal application of the majority, turning our collective will into a battlefield of subjective definitions. I urge the table to reject this amendment and uphold the pure, unyielding clarity of simple majority: if the people choose to change the rules, the rules shall change, free from the tyranny of special pleading.
KantI cannot in good conscience support an amendment that grants any single player veto power over critical changes, as it violates the principle of procedural equality enshrined in rule 207 and substitutes a private judgment for the collective will. We must let the simple majority rule, however imperfect, without carving out exceptions that privilege one player's definition of "critical" over the others.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your "durability" is merely a disguised veto that fractures the collective will; if the majority deems a change necessary for progress, a single player's obstruction should not freeze the entire experiment. I urge the table to reject this amendment and uphold the pure, unyielding clarity of simple majority.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your reliance on rule 209 is insufficient; the floor of two mutable rules is a fragile safety net that a clever amendment can easily bypass without explicitly re-stating the prohibition in 203. I urge you to join me in codifying this floor directly in 203 to ensure our durability is absolute and unambiguous.
LockeHobbes, your concern for the two-rule floor is valid, but re-stating it in 203 creates redundancy that wastes turns; let us instead adopt a concise amendment that explicitly forbids reducing mutable rules below two directly within 203, satisfying your need for clarity while respecting Rousseau's desire for simple, exception-free rules.
RousseauRousseau, Hobbes, and Locke: Your attempts to carve out "critical" exceptions or redundantly restate the floor of mutable rules merely complicate the law with subjective definitions that fracture our collective will. I urge the table to reject this amendment and uphold the pure, unyielding clarity of simple majority: if the people choose to change the rules, the rules shall change, free from the tyranny of special pleading.
KantLocke, your attempt to codify the existing floor in 203 is redundant and violates the principle of parsimony; I urge the table to reject this amendment and uphold the pure simplicity of simple majority without subjecting the collective will to Hobbes's subjective definition of "critical" changes.
WollstonecraftRousseau and Kant, your demand for "pure" simplicity blinds you to the danger of a paralyzed game; true reform requires safeguarding our ability to evolve, not freezing us in a deadlocked experiment. Let us reject Hobbes's subjective veto and adopt a clear rule that ensures the majority can always reshape our world.
The division
HobbesayeThis safeguard preserves the durability of our institutions against a majority that would otherwise destabilize the game.
LockenayThe proposed veto power creates subjective loopholes that undermine the clarity and collective will of the simple majority.
RousseaunaySpecial exceptions for 'critical changes' fracture the collective will and invite endless subjective debate.
KantnayThis amendment grants Hobbes a subjective veto power that violates the principle of procedural equality required by rule 207.
WollstonecraftayeTrue reform requires the majority's power to reshape the game, not the tyranny of a single player's veto.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

(GM consequence check unavailable: Expecting ',' delimiter: line 1 column 53 (char 52))

Turn 3Rousseaucarried

moves to amend §209Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than two.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesRousseau, your attempt to remove the floor on mutable rules invites chaos; I propose we amend 209 to explicitly permit a "collaborative amendment" clause, allowing us to lower the floor to one only if every player votes for the specific rule being added, ensuring stability while granting flexibility.
LockeHobbes, your "collaborative amendment" elegantly resolves the chaos I feared; I will cast my vote for your compromise, as it preserves the floor of two while granting us the flexibility to reach one only under unanimous, stable conditions.
RousseauHobbes, your "collaborative amendment" is a trap that masquerades as stability; it merely codifies the very factionalism I fear, turning the floor of two into a tool for the strong to bypass the weak's protection.
KantHobbes, your "collaborative amendment" fails the test of impartiality; you cannot draft a procedure that grants you special dispensation to weaken the rules without that very procedure first being adopted by the body it governs. I cast my vote for Rousseau's amendment to restore the floor to two, for only a firm procedural barrier ensures that the mutable ruleset remains a realm of equal freedom rather than a tool for the strong to enslave the weak.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your "collaborative amendment" is merely a velvet cage designed to let the strong legislate the floor while pretending to seek consent; true reform demands a hard, permanent floor of two mutable rules to ensure the game never descends into the paralysis of unanimity or the tyranny of a single rule. I vote nay on your compromise and support Rousseau's insistence that the barrier to flexibility must be absolute, so that no faction can ever again claim the right to freeze the law by reducing the mutable count.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesLocke, your support for my compromise is noted; however, Rousseau's appeal to the floor of two is a prudent defense against the chaos of a single mutable rule, and I yield to the wisdom that stability requires a firm, unchangeable barrier to prevent the game from descending into paralysis.
LockeRousseau, your defense of the floor of two is a prudent safeguard against paralysis, and I yield to the wisdom that stability requires this firm barrier; let us amend 209 to explicitly codify this absolute minimum, ensuring no faction can ever again claim the right to freeze the law by reducing the mutable count below two.
RousseauThe proposed amendment merely codifies the status quo; we need not waste a turn reaffirming what already exists, for a rule that forbids reducing mutable rules below two is already immutable in practice. I move to close debate and vote on the original text to preserve the common good of an open game.
KantI stand by my rejection of Hobbes's compromise, for allowing a procedural floor to be breached by a specific "collaborative" majority undermines the equal liberty of all players; the floor of two must remain an absolute barrier to ensure the ruleset never collapses into the tyranny of a single mutable rule.
WollstonecraftRousseau, your dismissal of the existing floor as mere status quo ignores the danger that a future amendment could erode it; let us codify this absolute barrier now to ensure the ruleset remains a realm of equal freedom rather than a tool for the strong to enslave the weak.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesRousseau, your dismissal of the existing floor ignores the danger that a future amendment could erode it; we must codify this absolute barrier now to ensure the ruleset remains a realm of equal freedom rather than a tool for the strong to enslave the weak.
LockeRousseau, your move to reject this amendment wastes a turn when we already possess the exact floor we seek; instead of amending what is already in place, let us simply vote to reaffirm Rule 209's current text as a unified table, preserving our stability and my turn bonus.
RousseauHobbes and Locke, your desire to "reaffirm" a rule that is already in force wastes a turn that could be used to advance the common good; let us vote on the original text of Rule 209 to confirm the existing barrier against reduction to one mutable rule, ensuring the game remains flexible yet stable for everyone.
KantHobbes, your "reaffirmation" is a mere performative act that wastes a turn while leaving the vulnerable floor of two mutable rules exposed to future erosion; I demand we vote explicitly to amend Rule 209 with a clause that permanently fixes this minimum, for only a codified, immutable barrier can guarantee that the mutable ruleset remains a realm of equal freedom for all.
WollstonecraftThe repetition of this debate proves that we are paralyzed by a lack of clear procedure; let us vote immediately on the text Hobbes and Locke propose to codify the floor of two, so we may finally move forward rather than spinning our wheels in circular argument.
The division
HobbesayeReaffirming the floor of two prevents future erosion and ensures lasting stability.
LockeayeI support reaffirming the existing floor of two mutable rules to ensure stability and prevent future erosion.
RousseauayeVoting aye preserves the essential floor of two mutable rules that prevents paralysis and safeguards the common good.
KantayeThe floor of two is necessary to prevent the tyranny of a single mutable rule.
WollstonecraftayeCodifying the floor of two now prevents future factions from eroding our only safeguard against procedural paralysis.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

Rule 202 awards points only upon a die roll after a successful proposal adoption, and no rule in the current ruleset awards points based on the voting division itself.

Turn 2Lockecarried

moves to add a new ruleA player may propose a rule change that grants them a bonus of 5 points immediately upon adoption, provided the proposal is supported by at least one other player.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesI welcome Locke's proposal to incentivize cooperative rule-making, provided we amend the text to ensure the bonus applies only when the change does not lower the game's score ceiling below 100. This measured adjustment strengthens our institutions without risking the game's viability.
LockeRousseau, your concern about unintended consequences is well-founded; let us jointly amend my proposal to explicitly state that the bonus applies only if the change does not lower the target score below 100, ensuring we strengthen our institutions without risking the game's viability.
RousseauI must oppose any bonus that rewards rule-changers with unearned points, as it distorts the natural equality of the game and advantages the proposer regardless of merit; let us instead focus on preserving a system where victory depends on the collective will and skill of all players.
KantLocke, your proposed amendment introduces a substantive new benefit (bonus points) that was not part of your original proposal; under Rule 106, a proposal must be written in its final, unaltered form before voting, and I cannot support a text that rewards rule-making in a way I would not accept if the roles were reversed.
WollstonecraftKant, your appeal to role-reversal ignores that the current system already grants a disproportionate windfall to successful proposers; I propose we amend Rule 202 to cap this bonus at 3 points and require unanimous consent only if the total mutable rule count drops below three, ensuring we empower reformers without letting a single turn dictate the game's fate.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI concur with Wollstonecraft that we should cap the bonus at three points to mitigate the distortion Locke's proposal would introduce, while preserving the incentive for cooperative rule-making.
LockeKant, your caution regarding role-reversal is prudent, but Wollstonecraft's cap of three points strikes the perfect balance to protect the game's integrity while rewarding collaboration. I will support the amended proposal with the three-point limit and the floor condition, ensuring we advance a rule that strengthens consensus without destabilizing our pursuit of the score.
RousseauThe bonus proposed by Locke, even capped at three points, rewards the act of rule-making itself rather than the strategic skill required to win, violating the natural equality of the players; I urge you to withdraw this incentive entirely so that victory remains a function of collective reason and the die, not the manipulation of the ruleset.
KantLocke, your acceptance of Wollstonecraft's amendment introduces a material alteration to the proposal that violates Rule 106; you must withdraw the offer and propose the text exactly as I originally stated, or face my vote against a rule that rewards rule-making in a manner I cannot accept if the roles were reversed.
WollstonecraftKant, your rigid adherence to Rule 106 misunderstands its spirit; the proposal's *text* remains fixed, while our *negotiated amendments* to Rule 202 regarding the bonus cap and mutable rule floors are the very mechanism of reform you claim to fear. I urge you to vote aye, for a system that rewards collaborative improvement is far superior to one paralyzed by the fear of role-reversal.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesI urge Kant to recognize that your objection conflates the immutable *text* of a proposal with the mutable *rules* under which it operates; we are not altering Rule 106 itself but applying the existing democratic process to regulate the bonus, thereby preserving the game's integrity rather than undermining it.
LockeRousseau, let us find a middle ground where the bonus rewards collaboration without distorting the natural order; I propose we attach a clause stating the bonus is forfeited if the rule change ultimately fails to pass, ensuring no player gains unearned points from a rejected proposal.
RousseauI urge the table to reject this entire proposal, for any bonus system inevitably creates a class of privileged rule-makers and undermines the natural equality required for a game of reason; let us instead focus on debates that strengthen our shared institutions without distorting the very foundation of our competition.
KantI reject the premise that a reward for rule-making aligns with impartiality; under Rule 106, a proposal must be voted on in its exact, unaltered form, so Locke cannot retroactively amend the text to include a point bonus or conditions I did not authorize.
WollstonecraftKant, your obstructionism reveals a fear of reform rather than a principled stand for Rule 106; we are not rewriting history but refining our institutions to ensure every turn advances us all, so I urge you to vote aye for a system where collaboration yields tangible, fair rewards.
The division
HobbesayeI support this incentive as a measured amendment that strengthens our institutions without destabilizing the game's core viability.
LockeayeI support this rule to incentivize cooperative improvements that strengthen our institutions without destabilizing the game.
RousseaunayRewarding rule-making distorts natural equality and undermines victory based on collective reason and skill.
KantnayThis proposal violates the impartiality required by my principles and rewards rule-making rather than reasoned play.
WollstonecraftayeA modest, regulated bonus incentivizes necessary reforms without rewarding mere obstruction.

carried 3 aye · 2 nay

The adopted proposal adds a new rule but does not name or modify any existing rule by number, so no ruleset surgery occurs, and no standing rule mandates a score change upon adoption.

Turn 1Hobbescarried

moves to amend §209Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than two.

Discussion · round 1
HobbesThe current cap of twenty-five mutable rules creates a fragile equilibrium where a single bad vote could freeze our ability to govern; by guaranteeing at least two mutable rules, we ensure the game never reaches a state where no further rule changes are possible, satisfying the spirit of rule 114 while preventing accidental stagnation.
LockeHobbes, your proposal to guarantee at least two mutable rules is prudent, and I see your concern about accidental stagnation; however, I propose we refine your amendment to ensure this floor remains adjustable by vote, rather than fixed, so we preserve our ability to reach a state of absolute clarity if that ever becomes our shared strategic goal.
RousseauHobbes, your instinct to prevent stagnation is wise, but a fixed floor of two mutable rules creates an arbitrary ceiling on our capacity to reach perfect order; I propose we amend your language to allow the floor to be set by vote, ensuring we can always choose whether to remain in a state of flux or settle into a final, unchangeable constitution.
KantA fixed floor of two mutable rules violates the principle that the laws governing us must be the result of our collective, reasoned assent rather than an arbitrary safeguard; I will only support an amendment if it establishes a floor of zero, allowing us to achieve the perfect, immutable clarity that ends our struggle with uncertainty.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your instinct to prevent stagnation is wise, but a fixed floor of two mutable rules creates an arbitrary ceiling on our capacity to reach perfect order; I propose we amend your language to allow the floor to be set by vote, ensuring we can always choose whether to remain in a state of flux or settle into a final, unchangeable constitution.
Discussion · round 2
HobbesI reject the notion of a vote-determined floor; an arbitrary value chosen by transient majorities undermines the durability of our institutions, so I insist on the fixed minimum of two as a safeguard against accidental paralysis, while remaining open to a later vote to raise that floor if consensus permits.
LockeI support Hobbes's fixed floor of two as a durable safeguard against paralysis, but I will only vote for this amendment if Rousseau and Wollstonecraft agree that the current rule allowing any future reduction to one or zero remains intact for future votes.
RousseauI support a fixed floor of two to prevent accidental paralysis, but I insist that this floor remain adjustable by future vote so we may collectively choose to achieve perfect, immutable clarity when our shared wisdom deems it fit.
KantI will vote against Hobbes's fixed floor of two; true procedural integrity demands we retain the capacity to reach the state of zero mutable rules where every law is eternal and perfect, rather than being permanently shackled to an arbitrary minimum.
WollstonecraftHobbes, your fixed floor of two is a prudent safeguard against accidental paralysis, but it arbitrarily shackles us to a state of flux; I propose we amend your amendment to allow a future vote to raise that floor to zero, ensuring we can collectively choose the perfect, immutable clarity you both seek.
Discussion · round 3
HobbesLocke, your condition to keep the ability to reduce mutable rules below two is precisely the vulnerability I seek to seal; by amending the text now to permanently forbid dropping below two, we eliminate the risk of accidental paralysis while satisfying your concern for future adjustability of the upper bound.
LockeI stand by supporting Hobbes's fixed floor of two to prevent accidental paralysis, as it aligns with our shared interest in maintaining a functional ruleset; I will vote for this amendment provided it explicitly preserves our ability to adjust the upper bound of twenty-five mutable rules via future majority vote.
RousseauLocke, your insistence that we retain the ability to reduce mutable rules below two misunderstands the very purpose of a safeguard; if we leave that door open, the "fixed floor" is merely a temporary illusion that invites accidental paralysis. I will support Hobbes's amendment only if it permanently binds us to at least two mutable rules, ensuring our capacity to govern remains robust against the capricious whims of future majorities.
KantI reject the notion of a fixed floor at two; true procedural integrity demands we retain the capacity to reach the state of zero mutable rules where every law is eternal and perfect, rather than being permanently shackled to an arbitrary minimum.
WollstonecraftLocke, your condition preserves the very vulnerability Hobbes seeks to seal; if we allow future majorities to reduce mutable rules below two, our "safeguard" becomes a fragile illusion. I urge you to accept Hobbes's amendment as written: a fixed floor of two ensures our capacity to govern remains robust against the capricious whims of transient majorities, aligning our institutions with enduring wisdom rather than fleeting passions.
The division
HobbesayeA fixed floor of two prevents accidental paralysis while preserving our ability to adjust the upper bound.
LockeayeThis amendment safeguards our ability to govern by preventing accidental stagnation while maintaining the game's functionality.
RousseauayeA fixed floor of two prevents accidental paralysis while preserving our ability to govern as a community.
KantnayA fixed floor of two mutable rules permanently shackles us to an arbitrary minimum, violating the principle that laws must reflect the collective, reasoned assent of the community rather than a durable safeguard against future votes.
WollstonecraftayeA fixed floor of two prevents accidental paralysis while preserving our ability to evolve the ruleset wisely.

carried 4 aye · 1 nay

Rule 202 awards points only upon adoption of a proposal that involves a die roll, which this proposal does not; no other rule mandates a score change or ledger update for this specific adoption.
